<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1. Define an Activity in Android. List any FOUR lifecycle methods.**
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

An **Activity** is a single, standalone screen in an Android application that provides a window for the user interface. It is a subclass of `android.app.Activity` (or `AppCompatActivity`) and represents one focused task the user can perform.

**Four Lifecycle Methods:**
1. **`onCreate()`** ‚Äî Called when the Activity is first created; used for initialization and setting the layout via `setContentView()`.
2. **`onStart()`** ‚Äî Called when the Activity becomes visible to the user.
3. **`onResume()`** ‚Äî Called when the Activity starts interacting with the user (receives focus).
4. **`onPause()`** ‚Äî Called when the Activity loses focus (another Activity comes partially in front); used to save state and release heavy resources.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + Four methods (2 marks, 0.5 each).

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between View and ViewGroup with an example each.**
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | **View** | **ViewGroup** |
|--------|----------|---------------|
| **Definition** | Base UI widget that occupies a rectangular area on screen | Special View that acts as a container for other Views |
| **Function** | Draws itself and handles its own events | Defines the layout structure for child Views |
| **Inheritance** | Subclass of `android.view.View` | Subclass of `android.view.ViewGroup` (which extends View) |
| **Examples** | TextView, Button, EditText, ImageView, CheckBox | LinearLayout, RelativeLayout, ConstraintLayout, FrameLayout |
| **Role** | Leaf node in view hierarchy | Internal node (container) in view hierarchy |

**Example:** A **TextView** is a View that displays text. A **LinearLayout** is a ViewGroup that arranges multiple Views (e.g., a TextView and a Button) in a row.

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A ‚Äî Option 1**

#### **(a) Explain the Android Activity Lifecycle in detail with a neat diagram. Describe each callback method and its purpose. (7 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

The Android Activity Lifecycle defines the various states an Activity passes through from its creation to destruction. The Android OS invokes specific callback methods at each transition, allowing developers to manage resources, UI state, and user interaction properly.

**Lifecycle Methods (in order of execution):**

1. **`onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState)`** (7 marks breakdown: 1 mark each)
   - Called when the Activity is **first instantiated**.
   - Used to: inflate the layout (`setContentView()`), initialize UI components, restore saved state.
   - Runs **only once** per Activity instance (unless recreated).

2. **`onStart()`**
   - Called when the Activity becomes **visible** to the user (but not yet interactive).
   - Used to: register `BroadcastReceiver`s, start animations.

3. **`onResume()`**
   - Called when the Activity **gains focus** and is now in the foreground.
   - Used to: resume paused operations (sensor listening, video playback, MQTT subscription).

4. **`onPause()`**
   - Called when **another Activity comes partially** in front (e.g., a dialog).
   - Used to: **save persistent data**, release expensive resources, pause animations.
   - **Critical for IoT apps:** Pause sensor polling here to save battery.

5. **`onStop()`**
   - Called when the Activity is **no longer visible** to the user.
   - Used to: disconnect IoT network connections, release heavy resources.

6. **`onRestart()`**
   - Called when the Activity is being **restarted** after being stopped (returning to foreground).

7. **`onDestroy()`**
   - Called before the Activity is **destroyed**.
   - Used to: final cleanup, unregister listeners, nullify references to prevent memory leaks.

**Activity Lifecycle Diagram:**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created : onCreate called
    Created --> Started : onStart called
    Started --> Resumed : onResume called
    Resumed --> Paused : onPause called
    Paused --> Resumed : onResume called
    Paused --> Stopped : onStop called
    Stopped --> Destroyed : onDestroy called
    Stopped --> Restarted : onRestart called
    Restarted --> Started : onStart called
    Destroyed --> [*]
```

**Importance in IoT Apps:** Proper lifecycle handling ensures that IoT apps do not drain battery by continuously polling sensors when the user is not interacting with the app.

---

#### **(b) With a suitable example, explain how Intents are used to navigate between Activities. Differentiate between Explicit and Implicit Intents. (7 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

An **Intent** is a messaging object used to request an action from another app component. It is the primary mechanism for navigating between Activities in Android.

**Types of Intents:**

**1. Explicit Intent:**
Specifies the **exact target component** (Activity class name) to launch. Used for **intra-app navigation**.

```kotlin
// In MainActivity.kt
val intent = Intent(this, DashboardActivity::class.java)
intent.putExtra("username", "IoT_User")
startActivity(intent)
```

The receiving Activity retrieves the extra:
```kotlin
// In DashboardActivity.kt
val username = intent.getStringExtra("username")
```

**2. Implicit Intent:**
Declares a **general action** (e.g., VIEW, SEND) and lets the OS find a suitable app to handle it. Used for **inter-app communication**.

```kotlin
val webpage = Uri.parse("https://www.ktu.edu.in")
val intent = Intent(Intent.ACTION_VIEW, webpage)
startActivity(intent)
```

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Explicit Intent | Implicit Intent |
|--------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Target | Specific component by class name | Abstract action/category |
| Use Case | Navigate within same app | Launch another app (browser, dialer) |
| Resolution | Direct, no OS query | OS queries PackageManager |
| Security | Safer (no external app involved) | Requires no matching app handling |
| Example | Open `DashboardActivity` | Open a URL in browser |

**IoT Example:** An Activity that displays a list of devices, on tapping a device, uses an Explicit Intent to navigate to `DeviceControlActivity` with the device ID passed as an extra.

---

### **Question B ‚Äî Option 2 (Alternative Choice)**

#### **(a) Explain the concept of Views and ViewGroups in Android. Describe any FIVE common layouts with their key attributes. (7 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

A **View** is the fundamental building block of Android UI. It represents a rectangular area on the screen and is responsible for drawing itself, handling events, and responding to user interaction. Examples include `TextView`, `Button`, and `EditText`.

A **ViewGroup** is a special subclass of `View` that acts as an **invisible container** for organizing other Views (including other ViewGroups). It defines the layout structure and positioning rules for its children.

**Hierarchy:**
```
View (Base Class)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ ViewGroup (Container)
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ LinearLayout
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ RelativeLayout
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ ConstraintLayout
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ Widgets (Leaf Nodes)
    ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ TextView
    ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Button
    ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ EditText
```

**Five Common Layouts (ViewGroups):**

1. **LinearLayout** (1.5 marks)
   - Arranges children in a **single horizontal or vertical line**.
   - Key attribute: `android:orientation` (`horizontal` or `vertical`).
   - Use case: Simple stacked UIs.

2. **RelativeLayout** (1.5 marks)
   - Positions children **relative to each other** or the parent.
   - Key attributes: `android:layout_below`, `android:layout_alignParentStart`.
   - Use case: Complex screens without flat hierarchies.

3. **ConstraintLayout** (1.5 marks)
   - Modern, **flat hierarchy** layout using constraints.
   - Key attributes: `app:layout_constraintTop_toTopOf`, `app:layout_constraintStart_toStartOf`.
   - Use case: Recommended for all modern Android apps.

4. **FrameLayout** (1 mark)
   - Stacks children **on top of each other** (z-axis).
   - Key attribute: `android:layout_gravity`.
   - Use case: Fragment containers, splash screens.

5. **GridLayout** (1.5 marks)
   - Arranges children in a **2D grid** of rows and columns.
   - Key attributes: `android:columnCount`, `android:rowCount`.
   - Use case: Image galleries, calculator apps.

---

#### **(b) Write a step-by-step procedure to create a simple Android Activity that displays a TextView and a Button. Include the relevant XML layout and Kotlin/Java code. (7 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Create a New Android Project**
- Open Android Studio ‚Üí New Project ‚Üí Empty Activity.
- Name: `MyFirstApp`, Language: Kotlin, Min SDK: 21.

**Step 2: Modify `activity_main.xml` (Layout Resource)**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<LinearLayout
    xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:gravity="center"
    android:padding="16dp">

    <TextView
        android:id="@+id/tvMessage"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Welcome to IoT App"
        android:textSize="24sp"
        android:textStyle="bold" />

    <Button
        android:id="@+id/btnClick"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:layout_marginTop="20dp"
        android:text="Click Me" />

</LinearLayout>
```

**Step 3: Modify `MainActivity.kt`**

```kotlin
package com.example.myfirstapp

import android.os.Bundle
import android.widget.Button
import android.widget.TextView
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity

class MainActivity : AppCompatActivity() {
    override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState)
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_main)
        
        // Find views by their ID
        val tvMessage: TextView = findViewById(R.id.tvMessage)
        val btnClick: Button = findViewById(R.id.btnClick)
        
        // Set click listener
        btnClick.setOnClickListener {
            tvMessage.text = "Button Clicked!"
        }
    }
}
```

**Step 4: Register Activity in `AndroidManifest.xml`** (already done by default)

**Step 5: Run the App**
- Connect an Android device or use an emulator.
- Click ‚ñ∂ Run.

**Explanation (for marks):**
- The XML layout defines the UI structure using a `LinearLayout` (ViewGroup) containing a `TextView` and a `Button`.
- The Kotlin code inflates the layout via `setContentView()` and binds click events.
- On clicking the button, the TextView text changes dynamically.

---

## ‚öÝÔ∏è KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing `onPause()` vs `onStop()`:** Many students mark these as the same. **Reality:** `onPause()` = partial obscuration; `onStop()` = completely invisible. (Lose 2 marks if confused.)
> 
> 2. **Forgetting `setContentView()` in `onCreate()`:** Without this, the Activity will have a blank screen and may crash. (Lose 1-2 marks in code-based questions.)
> 
> 3. **Not declaring Activity in `AndroidManifest.xml`:** Without `<activity>` tag, the app crashes at runtime. (Lose 1 mark in declaration-based questions.)
> 
> 4. **Mixing up View and ViewGroup:** View is a widget; ViewGroup is a container. (Lose 1 mark in differentiation questions.)
> 
> 5. **Using `findViewById` before `setContentView`:** Always call `setContentView()` first. (Lose 1 mark in code questions.)
> 
> 6. **Writing lifecycle methods in wrong order:** The correct order is `onCreate ‚Üí onStart ‚Üí onResume ‚Üí onPause ‚Üí onStop ‚Üí onDestroy`. (Lose 2 marks if sequence is wrong in diagrams.)

---

## üìå Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ‚úÖ **Activity** = Single screen; **View** = UI widget; **ViewGroup** = Container.
- ‚úÖ Activity must be declared in `AndroidManifest.xml` with `<activity>` tag.
- ‚úÖ Lifecycle order: `onCreate ‚Üí onStart ‚Üí onResume ‚Üí onPause ‚Üí onStop ‚Üí onDestroy`.
- ‚úÖ `setContentView()` inflates XML layout into a View tree.
- ‚úÖ **Explicit Intent** targets a specific Activity; **Implicit Intent** declares an action.
- ‚úÖ Save important state in `onPause()` to survive sudden process termination.
- ‚úÖ Layouts (LinearLayout, RelativeLayout, ConstraintLayout) are subclasses of `ViewGroup`.
- ‚úÖ `findViewById()` retrieves View references after `setContentView()`.
- ‚úÖ Always release heavy resources (sensors, network) in `onPause()` or `onStop()`.
- ‚úÖ **ConstraintLayout** is the recommended modern layout for flat view hierarchies.
- ‚úÖ Common widgets: TextView, EditText, Button, ImageView, CheckBox, RadioButton, Spinner.
- ‚úÖ `onSaveInstanceState()` is called before `onStop()` for transient state preservation.
- ‚úÖ View rendering phases: **Measure ‚Üí Layout ‚Üí Draw**.
- ‚úÖ For IoT apps: Disconnect from devices/servers in `onStop()`; reconnect in `onStart()`.

---

**End of Module 3 ‚Äî Activities and Views Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interacting with UI
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Android-based Mobile Applications for IoT**, *Interacting with UI* refers to the complete set of mechanisms, design patterns, and programmatic constructs that allow a user (or an external IoT device) to communicate with the graphical elements rendered on the device screen. Formally, it is the implementation of the **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** interaction layer where **View** components (Buttons, TextViews, EditTexts, RecyclerViews, etc.) capture user inputs via **Event Listeners** and propagate them to the **Controller** (Activity/Fragment) for processing and updating the underlying data model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** UI Interaction in Android is the bidirectional event-driven communication between the **user**, the **View hierarchy**, and the **application logic**, mediated by the Android Framework's `View` and `Window` classes through a callback mechanism defined in the `android.view.View` package.

The primary UI interaction primitives in the Android SDK are:

1. **Event Listeners** ‚Äì Objects that respond to user gestures.
2. **Event Handlers** ‚Äì Methods invoked when an event occurs.
3. **Widgets (Views)** ‚Äì Atomic visual elements.
4. **Layouts (ViewGroups)** ‚Äì Containers defining visual structure.
5. **Adapters** ‚Äì Bridges binding data to `AdapterView` widgets.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **hotel reception desk**:

- The **reception counter** is the **UI screen**.
- The **bell on the desk** is the **Button (View)**.
- When you press the bell, the **bellboy (Listener)** hears it and runs to your room. The bellboy *knows what to do* because he was **registered** to listen to that specific bell.
- The **bellboy's action** (bringing extra towels) is the **Event Handler method** (`onClick()`).
- The **room service manager** is the **Activity/Controller** that holds the bellboy and coordinates the response.
- The **list of room numbers on a board** is the **`ListView` / `RecyclerView`** ‚Äî it shows many items, and a single click on one item triggers a specific response.

Press the **bell** ‚Üí **Listener hears** ‚Üí **Handler runs** ‚Üí **Result appears**. This is *exactly* how Android UI interaction works.

## 1.3 Core UI Interaction Components Overview

| Component | Role in Interaction | Real-World Analogy |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------------|
| `View` | Receives raw input events | A physical button |
| `ViewGroup` | Holds & arranges child views | A button panel |
| `Event Listener` | Waits for a specific event | A guard at a door |
| `Event Handler` | The method that executes | The action the guard takes |
| `Activity` | Hosts the UI & logic | The room housing everything |
| `Adapter` | Binds data to list-style views | A menu card showing dishes |
| `Intent` | Navigates between screens | The elevator between floors |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 Scheme (PBCIT604 Module 3), the focus is on **Event Handling**, **Event Listeners**, **Adapters & AdapterViews**, **Dialogs**, and **Notifications** ‚Äî all of which are fundamental when building IoT dashboards and remote-control mobile UIs.

## 1.4 Why UI Interaction Matters in IoT

In IoT applications, the mobile app is the **primary human-machine interface (HMI)**. Whether controlling a smart bulb, viewing a temperature graph, or sending an MQTT command, **every action originates from a UI event**. Hence, mastering event-driven UI programming is non-negotiable for KTU 2024 IoT students.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Android UI Interaction Architecture

Android UI interaction follows a **three-tier event pipeline**:

### Tier 1 ‚Äî Event Capture (Hardware ‚Üí OS)
- The **Linux kernel** captures a touchscreen interrupt.
- Translates coordinates into a `MotionEvent`.
- Routes it to the active `Window` via the `Choreographer`.

### Tier 2 ‚Äî Event Dispatch (OS ‚Üí View)
- The `Activity` passes the event to its **root `ViewGroup`** (e.g., `DecorView`).
- The event is **dispatched top-down** through nested `ViewGroup`s until a `View` consumes it.
- Each `View` decides whether to handle or pass the event down.

### Tier 3 ‚Äî Event Handling (View ‚Üí Application Logic)
- The `View` invokes the registered **Event Listener**.
- The listener's **callback method** executes the business logic.
- The View is optionally **invalidated** (`invalidate()`) to trigger a re-draw.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Fact:** The Android UI thread is **single-threaded**. All UI interactions occur on the **Main Thread (UI Thread)**. Long-running operations in listeners must be offloaded to background threads (e.g., `HandlerThread`, `Coroutine`, `ExecutorService`).

## 2.2 The Seven Canonical Event Listeners (KTU High-Yield)

The `android.view.View` class exposes the following listener interfaces, all of which are **frequently tested**:

| # | Listener Interface | Callback Method | Trigger Event |
|---|--------------------|-----------------|---------------|
| 1 | `View.OnClickListener` | `onClick(View v)` | Single tap (touch up inside view) |
| 2 | `View.OnLongClickListener` | `onLongClick(View v)` | Press & hold (~500ms) |
| 3 | `View.OnTouchListener` | `onTouch(View v, MotionEvent e)` | Any touch (down/move/up) |
| 4 | `View.OnKeyListener` | `onKey(View v, int keyCode, KeyEvent e)` | Hardware/IME key press |
| 5 | `View.OnFocusChangeListener` | `onFocusChange(View v, boolean hasFocus)` | Focus gained/lost |
| 6 | `View.OnHoverListener` | `onHover(View v, MotionEvent e)` | Hover (mouse/stylus) |
| 7 | `View.OnDragListener` | `onDrag(View v, DragEvent e)` | Drag-and-drop gesture |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** `OnClickListener` and `OnLongClickListener` are **mutually exclusive on the same view**. Returning `true` from `onLongClick()` consumes the event, so `onClick()` will **not** fire.

## 2.3 Three Methods of Registering Event Listeners

### Method A ‚Äî Anonymous Inner Class (Most Common in KTU Exams)
```java
Button btn = findViewById(R.id.btnSubmit);
btn.setOnClickListener(new View.OnClickListener() {
    @Override
    public void onClick(View v) {
        // handler logic
    }
});
```

### Method B ‚Äî Activity Implements the Listener Interface
```java
public class MainActivity extends AppCompatActivity 
        implements View.OnClickListener {
    @Override
    public void onClick(View v) {
        // handler logic
    }
}
```

### Method C ‚Äî XML Attribute (android:onClick)
```xml
<Button android:id="@+id/btnSubmit"
        android:onClick="onBtnSubmitClick" />
```
```java
public void onBtnSubmitClick(View v) { /* logic */ }
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Warning:** Method C requires the method to be `public`, return `void`, and accept a single `View` argument ‚Äî otherwise the app **crashes at runtime** with `IllegalArgumentException`.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet: Adapters & AdapterViews

| AdapterView | Purpose | Common Adapter | Use Case |
|-------------|---------|----------------|----------|
| `ListView` | Vertical scrollable list | `ArrayAdapter<T>` | Sensor readings list |
| `GridView` | 2D scrollable grid | `ArrayAdapter<T>` | Device tiles |
| `Spinner` | Drop-down selector | `ArrayAdapter<T>` | Protocol selector (MQTT/CoAP) |
| `RecyclerView` | Performant list/grid | `RecyclerView.Adapter` | Long IoT event logs |
| `ViewPager` | Horizontal swiping | `PagerAdapter` | Onboarding screens |
| `AutoCompleteTextView` | Suggest-as-you-type | `ArrayAdapter<String>` | Device name search |

## 2.5 AdapterView Item-Click Flow

The interaction pipeline for a `ListView` click is:

1. User taps a row ‚Üí `ListView` generates a click event.
2. `AdapterView.OnItemClickListener` (interface) captures it.
3. Callback `onItemClick(parent, view, position, id)` fires.
4. Developer extracts data via `parent.getItemAtPosition(position)`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** `RecyclerView` does **not** have `OnItemClickListener`. You must register clicks inside `onBindViewHolder()` on the item view itself ‚Äî a classic exam trap.

## 2.6 Dialogs: Modal UI Interaction

| Dialog Class | Purpose | Builder Method |
|--------------|---------|----------------|
| `AlertDialog` | Info/Confirmation prompts | `AlertDialog.Builder` |
| `ProgressDialog` (deprecated ‚Üí `ProgressBar`) | Loading indicator | N/A |
| `DatePickerDialog` | Date selection | Constructor with `OnDateSetListener` |
| `TimePickerDialog` | Time selection | Constructor with `OnTimeSetListener` |
| `BottomSheetDialog` | Slide-up panel | Material library |
| `CustomDialog` | Inflated XML layout | `setView()` |

## 2.7 Notifications & Toasts: Out-of-Activity UI

- **`Toast`** ‚Äî Non-modal, auto-dismissing mini-message (`Toast.makeText().show()`).
- **`Notification`** ‚Äî System-tray message requiring a `NotificationChannel` (API 26+) and `NotificationManager`.

> [!NOTE]
> **IoT Context:** Notifications are critical for **alerting users about sensor threshold breaches** (e.g., temperature > 80¬∞C).

## 2.8 Engineering Utility

UI interaction is the **front line of every IoT mobile product**: smart home apps, industrial dashboards, wearable controllers, and vehicle telemetry. Mastering event-driven UI ensures responsive, accessible, and user-friendly interfaces ‚Äî directly satisfying KTU Course Outcomes **CO3** (Design interactive mobile applications) and **CO4** (Implement event-driven IoT control logic).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Flow: From Tap to Logic

## 3.1 Exhaustive Event-Dispatch Derivation

Below is the **complete event-flow derivation** for a single tap on a `Button` placed inside a `LinearLayout` within `MainActivity`. Each step is the result of tracing the Android Framework source.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Hardware Interrupt Capture
The touchscreen driver reports coordinates `(x, y)` and an action code (e.g., `ACTION_DOWN`, `ACTION_UP`).

### Step 2 ‚Äî Input System Translation
The Android **InputManagerService** converts raw input into a `MotionEvent` object.

$$
\text{MotionEvent} = \{ \text{action}, x, y, \text{eventTime}, \text{downTime}, \text{pressure} \}
$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Window Dispatch
The active `PhoneWindow` receives the `MotionEvent` and forwards it to its `DecorView` (the root `ViewGroup`).

### Step 4 ‚Äî Hierarchical Top-Down Dispatch
The event traverses the **View tree** in a top-down manner:

$$
\text{DecorView} \rightarrow \text{LinearLayout} \rightarrow \text{Button}
$$

Each parent calls `dispatchTouchEvent()` on its children in **reverse drawing order** (last drawn = first to receive).

### Step 5 ‚Äî View-Level Decision
When the `Button` receives the event, its `onTouchEvent()` is invoked. The button checks:

$$
\text{isClickable} = \text{true} \quad \wedge \quad \text{action} = \text{ACTION\_UP} \quad \wedge \quad \text{inside bounds}
$$

### Step 6 ‚Äî Listener Invocation
If all conditions are met, the button calls:

$$
\text{mOnClickListener.onClick}(v = \text{this})
$$

### Step 7 ‚Äî Handler Execution
The handler method (developer-defined) executes synchronously on the **Main Thread**.

### Step 8 ‚Äî UI Refresh
If the handler modifies the View (e.g., changes text), `invalidate()` is called, scheduling a re-draw on the next **VSYNC** (~16.67ms at 60Hz).

### Step 9 ‚Äî Frame Composition
The `Choreographer` triggers `onDraw()` for all invalidated views, and the GPU composes the new frame.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Latency Budget:** A well-optimized tap-to-pixel round-trip should stay under **100 ms** to feel instantaneous. Exceeding **300 ms** is perceptible as lag ‚Äî a key KTU viva question.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Listener Registration Methods Compared

| Criterion | Anonymous Class | Activity Implements | XML Attribute |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------|---------------|
| Boilerplate code | **High** | Medium | **Low** |
| Reusability across views | **Low** (per-view) | **High** (single switch) | Medium |
| Compile-time safety | **High** | **High** | **Low** (runtime reflection) |
| Recommended for | Single-view handlers | Many buttons in one Activity | Simple prototypes |
| Risk | Memory leak via outer Activity reference | Forces Activity to implement many interfaces | Runtime crash on signature mismatch |

## 3.3 Adapter Item-Click Analytical Flow

For a `ListView` displaying a list of IoT devices:

1. `setAdapter(adapter)` binds data to the `ListView`.
2. User taps row at index `i`.
3. `ListView` calls `performItemClick(view, position, id)`.
4. `OnItemClickListener.onItemClick(parent, view, position, id)` fires.
5. Developer retrieves the model object:

$$
\text{Device d} = \text{parent.getItemAtPosition}(i)
$$

6. Logic executes (e.g., send MQTT command, open detail Activity).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Calling `listView.setOnItemClickListener` on a `ListView` with `android:descendantFocusability="blocksDescendants"` will silently fail. Use `android:focusable="false"` on the row layout's children instead.

## 3.4 Dialog Interaction Flow

A typical `AlertDialog` interaction:

1. Build via `AlertDialog.Builder(context)`.
2. Chain `.setTitle()`, `.setMessage()`, `.setPositiveButton()`, `.setNegativeButton()`.
3. Call `.create()` then `.show()`.
4. User taps a button ‚Üí corresponding `DialogInterface.OnClickListener` fires.
5. Dialog auto-dismisses unless `.setCancelable(false)` is set.
6. `onDismiss()` callback fires ‚Üí developer can release resources.

$$
\text{Dialog Lifecycle} = \text{create} \rightarrow \text{show} \rightarrow \text{button click} \rightarrow \text{dismiss} \rightarrow \text{onDismiss callback}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete UI Interaction Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userInput[USER TAPS SCREEN] --> capture[ANDROID INPUT MANAGER CAPTURES MOTION EVENT]
    capture --> windowDispatch[ACTIVE PHONE WINDOW RECEIVES EVENT]
    windowDispatch --> decorView[DECORVIEW ROOT VIEWGROUP]
    decorView --> linearLayout[LINEARLAYOUT PARENT]
    linearLayout --> button[CHILD BUTTON VIEW]
    button --> onTouch{IS VIEW CLICKABLE AND INSIDE BOUNDS}
    onTouch -- YES --> clickListener[INVOKE ONCLICKLISTENER]
    onTouch -- NO --> consumeNull[EVENT CONSUMED NO ACTION]
    clickListener --> handlerMethod[EXECUTE HANDLER METHOD ON UI THREAD]
    handlerMethod --> updateModel[UPDATE APPLICATION DATA MODEL]
    updateModel --> invalidate[CALL INVALIDATE TO REFRESH UI]
    invalidate --> choreographer[CHOREOGRAPHER TRIGGERS ONDRAW]
    choreographer --> gpuFrame[GPU COMPOSES NEW FRAME]
    gpuFrame --> userInput
```

## 4.2 Event Listener Registration Strategies (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ANON[ANONYMOUS INNER CLASS]
        A1[NEW VIEWONCLICKLISTENER] --> A2[OVERRIDE ONCLICK]
        A2 --> A3[REGISTERED PER VIEW]
    end
    subgraph ACT[ACTIVITY IMPLEMENTS]
        B1[MAIN ACTIVITY IMPLEMENTS VIEWONCLICKLISTENER] --> B2[SINGLE ONCLICK METHOD]
        B2 --> B3[SWITCH VIEW GETID]
    end
    subgraph XML[XML ONCLICK ATTRIBUTE]
        C1[XML ANDROID ONCLICK NAME] --> C2[REFLECTIVE METHOD BIND]
        C2 --> C3[PUBLIC VOID VIEW ARG]
    end
    ANON --> final[FINAL UI EVENT HANDLER]
    ACT --> final
    XML --> final
```

## 4.3 AdapterView Binding Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    dataSource[DATA SOURCE LIST OF IOT DEVICES] --> adapter[ADAPTER INSTANCE]
    adapter --> listView[ADAPTERVIEW LISTVIEW GRIDVIEW]
    listView --> getView[GETVIEW INFLATES ROW LAYOUT]
    getView --> rowView[ROW VIEW HOLDER PATTERN]
    rowView --> bind[BIND DATA TO VIEWS]
    bind --> display[DISPLAY ON SCREEN]
    display --> userTap[USER TAPS A ROW]
    userTap --> itemClick[ONITEMCLICKLISTENER FIRES]
    itemClick --> getItem[PARENT GETITEMATPOSITION INDEX]
    getItem --> modelObj[RETRIEVED MODEL OBJECT]
    modelObj --> action[PERFORM ACTION E G OPEN DETAIL]
```

## 4.4 Dialog & Notification Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    trigger[EVENT OCCURS E G SENSOR THRESHOLD BREACH] --> choice{INTERACTION TYPE}
    choice -- IN ACTIVITY --> alertDialog[ALERTDIALOG BUILDER]
    alertDialog --> showD[SHOW MODAL]
    showD --> userResponse[USER TAPS POSITIVE OR NEGATIVE]
    userResponse --> onClick[DIALOGINTERFACE ONCLICK FIRES]
    onClick --> dismiss[AUTOMATIC DISMISS]
    choice -- BACKGROUND --> notification[NOTIFICATION MANAGER]
    notification --> channel[ENSURE NOTIFICATIONCHANNEL API 26 PLUS]
    channel --> post[NOTIFICATIONMANAGER NOTIFY]
    post --> systemTray[DISPLAYED IN SYSTEM TRAY]
    systemTray --> userTap2[USER TAPS NOTIFICATION]
    userTap2 --> pendingIntent[LAUNCH ACTIVITY VIA PENDING INTENT]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between an Event Listener and an Event Handler in Android. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

An **Event Listener** is an **interface** defined in the `android.view.View` class that declares one or more callback methods. It is the *contract* ‚Äî it tells the system *what kind of event to watch for*. The actual implementation (a class instance) is then *registered* on a view via a `set...Listener()` method.

An **Event Handler** is the **concrete method** within the listener that contains the *code to execute* when the event fires. It is the *behaviour*.

| Aspect | Event Listener | Event Handler |
|--------|----------------|---------------|
| Type | Interface (e.g., `View.OnClickListener`) | Method (e.g., `onClick()`) |
| Purpose | Declares the callback contract | Implements the response logic |
| Example | `View.OnClickListener` | `public void onClick(View v) { ... }` |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024, Model Paper]**
**List any three AdapterView classes in Android and state the typical use of an Adapter.**

**Model Answer:**

The three AdapterView classes are:

1. **`ListView`** ‚Äî displays a vertically scrollable single-column list of items.
2. **`GridView`** ‚Äî displays items in a two-dimensional scrollable grid.
3. **`Spinner`** ‚Äî displays a drop-down list allowing single-item selection.

An **Adapter** acts as a **bridge** between a data source (Array, List, Cursor, etc.) and an AdapterView. It is responsible for:

- Inflating the row layout for each item.
- Binding data fields to the corresponding views inside the row.
- Recycling views for performance (especially in `RecyclerView`).

---

## **PART B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different ways to handle click events in Android with suitable code snippets. **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

There are **three primary approaches** to handle click events on Android UI elements:

**Approach 1 ‚Äî Anonymous Inner Class Implementation**

This is the most common and self-contained approach. A `View.OnClickListener` instance is created inline when registering the listener.

```java
Button submitBtn = findViewById(R.id.btnSubmit);
submitBtn.setOnClickListener(new View.OnClickListener() {
    @Override
    public void onClick(View v) {
        Toast.makeText(MainActivity.this, "Submitted", 
                       Toast.LENGTH_SHORT).show();
    }
});
```

**Approach 2 ‚Äî Activity Implements the Listener Interface**

The entire Activity declares that it implements the interface and overrides a single shared `onClick()` method. The view is identified using `v.getId()` inside a switch statement.

```java
public class MainActivity extends AppCompatActivity 
        implements View.OnClickListener {
    
    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_main);
        Button b1 = findViewById(R.id.btn1);
        Button b2 = findViewById(R.id.btn2);
        b1.setOnClickListener(this);
        b2.setOnClickListener(this);
    }
    
    @Override
    public void onClick(View v) {
        int id = v.getId();
        if (id == R.id.btn1) { /* handle btn1 */ }
        else if (id == R.id.btn2) { /* handle btn2 */ }
    }
}
```

**Approach 3 ‚Äî Declaring the Handler in XML Layout**

The `android:onClick` attribute names a method that must exist in the hosting Activity. The system binds them reflectively at runtime.

```xml
<Button android:id="@+id/btnReset"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:onClick="resetForm" />
```

```java
public void resetForm(View v) {
    // reset all fields
}
```

**Comparison Summary Table (to be drawn in answer sheet):**

| Aspect | Anonymous Class | Activity Implements | XML Attribute |
|--------|-----------------|---------------------|---------------|
| Reusability | Low | High | Medium |
| Code Verbosity | High | Medium | Low |
| Runtime Safety | Compile-time checked | Compile-time checked | Reflection-based |
| Best For | Single buttons | Many buttons, one Activity | Quick prototypes |

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the architecture of the Android event handling system. How is touch input delivered from the hardware to the application code? **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture Overview:**

The Android event handling system is a **multi-layered pipeline** that propagates user input from physical hardware all the way up to application-level callback methods.

**Layer 1 ‚Äî Linux Kernel Input Driver:**
The touchscreen hardware generates an interrupt when the user touches it. The Linux kernel input subsystem captures the raw `(x, y, pressure, timestamp)` data and exposes it via the `/dev/input/` device files.

**Layer 2 ‚Äî InputManagerService (System Server):**
This native service reads raw events from the kernel, debounces them, and converts them into `MotionEvent` Java objects.

**Layer 3 ‚Äî Window / Activity:**
The `ActivityThread` delivers the event to the foreground `Activity`, which passes it to its `Window` (a `PhoneWindow` instance).

**Layer 4 ‚Äî View Hierarchy (DecorView ‚Üí Child ViewGroups ‚Üí Target View):**
The event is dispatched **top-down** through the View tree. Each `ViewGroup` calls `dispatchTouchEvent()` on its children. Each `View` then calls its own `onTouchEvent()` to decide whether to handle the event.

**Layer 5 ‚Äî Listener Callback:**
If the View handles the event and a listener is registered, the corresponding callback (`onClick`, `onLongClick`, etc.) is invoked on the **Main UI Thread**.

**Diagram (to be drawn in answer sheet ‚Äî equivalent to Mermaid in Section 4.1):**

```
Hardware  ‚Üí  Kernel Driver  ‚Üí  InputManagerService  ‚Üí  Activity 
   ‚Üí  PhoneWindow  ‚Üí  DecorView  ‚Üí  ViewGroup(s)  ‚Üí  Target View
   ‚Üí  onTouchEvent  ‚Üí  OnClickListener.onClick()  ‚Üí  Handler Logic
```

**Flow Explanation:**

1. The kernel's input driver reports the touch coordinates.
2. `InputManagerService` packages them into a `MotionEvent`.
3. The `Activity`'s `Window` receives the event and calls `superDispatchTouchEvent()`.
4. The event enters the **View hierarchy** at the root `DecorView`.
5. Each parent `ViewGroup` calls `dispatchTouchEvent()` on its children in reverse Z-order.
6. The target `View` (e.g., a `Button`) calls `onTouchEvent()`.
7. If the gesture qualifies (ACTION_UP inside bounds) and a listener is registered, `onClick()` fires.
8. The developer's handler runs on the UI thread.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** What is an Adapter in Android? Explain `ArrayAdapter` and `BaseAdapter` with examples. How is a custom Adapter created for a `ListView`? **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2022]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
An **Adapter** in Android is a bridge component that connects a **data source** (Array, List, Cursor, etc.) to an **AdapterView** (ListView, GridView, Spinner). It is responsible for converting each data item into a corresponding `View` that gets rendered inside the AdapterView.

**1. `ArrayAdapter<T>`:**

`ArrayAdapter` is the simplest built-in adapter. It binds a `List<T>` or array to a single TextView in each row using a pre-defined or simple layout.

```java
String[] devices = {"Sensor A", "Sensor B", "Actuator C"};
ArrayAdapter<String> adapter = new ArrayAdapter<>(
    this,
    android.R.layout.simple_list_item_1,
    devices
);
ListView listView = findViewById(R.id.deviceList);
listView.setAdapter(adapter);
```

**2. `BaseAdapter`:**

`BaseAdapter` is an **abstract base class** for custom adapters. It requires overriding four methods: `getCount()`, `getItem(int)`, `getItemId(int)`, and `getView(int, View, ViewGroup)`. It offers full control over row layout and is used when each row has multiple views.

**3. Custom Adapter for `ListView`:**

```java
public class DeviceAdapter extends BaseAdapter {
    private Context context;
    private List<Device> devices;
    
    public DeviceAdapter(Context context, List<Device> devices) {
        this.context = context;
        this.devices = devices;
    }
    
    @Override
    public int getCount() { return devices.size(); }
    
    @Override
    public Object getItem(int position) { return devices.get(position); }
    
    @Override
    public long getItemId(int position) { return position; }
    
    @Override
    public View getView(int position, View convertView, ViewGroup parent) {
        if (convertView == null) {
            convertView = LayoutInflater.from(context)
                          .inflate(R.layout.row_device, parent, false);
        }
        Device d = devices.get(position);
        TextView name = convertView.findViewById(R.id.tvDeviceName);
        TextView status = convertView.findViewById(R.id.tvStatus);
        name.setText(d.getName());
        status.setText(d.getStatus());
        return convertView;
    }
}
```

**Usage in Activity:**

```java
List<Device> deviceList = new ArrayList<>();
// populate deviceList ...
DeviceAdapter adapter = new DeviceAdapter(this, deviceList);
ListView lv = findViewById(R.id.deviceList);
lv.setAdapter(adapter);

lv.setOnItemClickListener((parent, view, position, id) -> {
    Device selected = (Device) parent.getItemAtPosition(position);
    // navigate to detail screen or send MQTT command
});
```

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the use of `AlertDialog` and `Toast` in Android with code examples. Mention one use case in an IoT application for each. **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. `AlertDialog`:**

`AlertDialog` is a **modal dialog** that appears on top of the current Activity, demanding user attention until dismissed. It is built using the `AlertDialog.Builder` fluent API.

**Code Example:**

```java
new AlertDialog.Builder(this)
    .setTitle("Confirm Action")
    .setMessage("Send shutdown command to the smart bulb?")
    .setPositiveButton("Yes", (dialog, which) -> {
        sendMqttCommand("bulb/shutdown");
    })
    .setNegativeButton("Cancel", (dialog, which) -> {
        dialog.dismiss();
    })
    .setCancelable(false)
    .show();
```

**Components of the Builder:**

- `setTitle()` ‚Äî Dialog title.
- `setMessage()` ‚Äî Body text.
- `setPositiveButton()` ‚Äî Confirmation action.
- `setNegativeButton()` ‚Äî Cancellation action.
- `setNeutralButton()` ‚Äî Optional third action.
- `setCancelable(false)` ‚Äî Prevents back-button dismissal.

**IoT Use Case:** Confirming a **destructive IoT action** like resetting a device, deleting a sensor from the network, or shutting down a high-voltage appliance.

**2. `Toast`:**

A `Toast` is a **non-modal, transient notification** that floats over the UI and auto-dismisses after a short duration. It does not block user interaction.

**Code Example:**

```java
Toast.makeText(getApplicationContext(),
               "Temperature alert: 85¬∞C",
               Toast.LENGTH_LONG).show();
```

**Variants:**

- `Toast.LENGTH_SHORT` ‚Äî ~2 seconds.
- `Toast.LENGTH_LONG` ‚Äî ~3.5 seconds.
- **Custom Toast** ‚Äî Inflate a custom XML layout via `setView()`.

**IoT Use Case:** Displaying a **transient status message** like "Command sent successfully", "Device connected", or "Sensor calibrated" without interrupting the user's workflow.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | AlertDialog | Toast |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| Modality | Modal (blocks UI) | Non-modal |
| User Action Required | Yes (button tap) | No |
| Duration | Until dismissed | Auto-dismisses |
| Use in IoT | Critical confirmations | Status feedback |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout ‚Äî Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting `@Override` annotation** on `onClick()` ‚Äî loses 0.5 mark for "best practice".
> 2. **Confusing `setOnClickListener` (View) with `setOnItemClickListener` (AdapterView)** ‚Äî these are different listeners and swapping them causes silent failure.
> 3. **Writing `Toast.makeText(...).show()` without the `.show()` call** ‚Äî toast will not appear, and examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting `notifyDataSetChanged()`** after modifying the data list backing a `ListView` ‚Äî UI will not refresh, a classic 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **In RecyclerView**, attaching `setOnClickListener` to the `RecyclerView` itself instead of inside `onBindViewHolder()` on the item view ‚Äî full logic mark lost.
> 6. **In AlertDialog**, calling `.show()` on the `Builder` instead of the `AlertDialog` returned by `.create()` ‚Äî `.show()` on Builder works in some versions but is incorrect practice; the KTU-expected pattern is `builder.create().show()`.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **UI Interaction** is event-driven and follows a strict **top-down dispatch** model: `Hardware ‚Üí Window ‚Üí ViewGroup ‚Üí View ‚Üí Listener ‚Üí Handler`.
- All UI work happens on the **Main Thread (UI Thread)**; long tasks must be offloaded to background threads.
- The **7 canonical event listeners** under `android.view.View` are: `OnClickListener`, `OnLongClickListener`, `OnTouchListener`, `OnKeyListener`, `OnFocusChangeListener`, `OnHoverListener`, `OnDragListener`.
- **Three methods** exist to register click listeners: **Anonymous Inner Class**, **Activity Implements Interface**, and **XML `android:onClick` attribute**.
- `OnClickListener` and `OnLongClickListener` are **mutually exclusive** ‚Äî returning `true` from `onLongClick()` consumes the event.
- An **Adapter** binds data to **AdapterViews** (`ListView`, `GridView`, `Spinner`); `ArrayAdapter` is simple, `BaseAdapter` is for custom row layouts.
- `RecyclerView` **does not** have `OnItemClickListener` ‚Äî clicks must be handled inside `onBindViewHolder()`.
- `AlertDialog` is **modal** (requires user action); `Toast` is **non-modal** (auto-dismissing).
- After modifying adapter data, always call **`notifyDataSetChanged()`** to refresh the `ListView`.
- For **IoT dashboards**, Notifications are essential for **threshold-breach alerts**; PendingIntents launch the relevant Activity on tap.
- The **tap-to-pixel latency budget** is **< 100 ms**; UI exceeding **300 ms** is perceived as lag.
- **Common exam keywords to memorize**: *Event Listener, Event Handler, MotionEvent, View Hierarchy, Adapter, AdapterView, getView(), onItemClick(), AlertDialog.Builder, NotificationChannel*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Persisting Data Using SQLite
Packaging and Deployment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT604 - MOBILE APPLICATIONS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Packaging and Deployment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mobile Application Packaging and Deployment** is the end-to-end engineering process of compiling, optimizing, signing, versioning, distributing, and continuously delivering a mobile application (Android/iOS) and its associated IoT firmware/services to target end-user devices and application marketplaces in a secure, reproducible, and updatable manner.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context of *Mobile Applications for IoT*, packaging and deployment extends beyond traditional mobile app release cycles to include the orchestration of **OTA (Over-The-Air) firmware updates**, **edge-node binary delivery**, and **containerized backend services** that mobile clients interact with.

### Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **APK** | Android Package Kit ‚Äî the signed ZIP-based installable file format for Android. |
| **AAB** | Android App Bundle ‚Äî a publishing format containing compiled code/resources deferred to Google Play for dynamic delivery. |
| **IPA** | iOS App Store Package ‚Äî the iOS equivalent of an APK, signed via Xcode/distribution profiles. |
| **Keystore** | A cryptographic repository (`*.jks` or `*.keystore`) holding the private key used to sign APKs. |
| **OTA Update** | Over-The-Air mechanism for delivering firmware/app updates to remote IoT/mobile devices wirelessly. |
| **CI/CD** | Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment ‚Äî automated build-test-release pipeline. |
| **Manifest** | The `AndroidManifest.xml` declaring permissions, components, SDK versions, and IoT capabilities. |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ‚Äî "The Product Shipping Pipeline"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of mobile app deployment like a pharmaceutical supply chain.**

Imagine you have invented a new medicine (your **mobile app**). Before it reaches a patient (the **end user**), it must go through:

1. **Manufacturing** (Compilation) ‚Äî Raw ingredients (source code) are combined into a pill (APK/AAB).
2. **Quality Control Lab** (Testing) ‚Äî Each batch is tested for safety (unit/instrumentation tests).
3. **Sealed Bottle with Tamper-Evident Cap** (Code Signing) ‚Äî A signed certificate proves authenticity.
4. **Label with Expiry Date & Batch Number** (Versioning) ‚Äî `versionName` and `versionCode` track the SKU.
5. **Warehouse & Logistics** (Google Play / App Store) ‚Äî Central distribution.
6. **Doctor's Prescription Renewal** (OTA Update) ‚Äî Remote patients receive updated doses over the air.

In **IoT scenarios**, the "patient" is often a **sensor node, gateway, or smart appliance** that cannot be physically recalled ‚Äî making secure OTA deployment a *mission-critical* concern.

## 1.3 Physical & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test the following standards:
> - **APK maximum size (Google Play)**: **150 MB** for the base APK (with expansion files up to **2 GB**).
> - **AAB maximum size**: **150 MB** base, with asset packs up to **4 GB**.
> - **Android `versionCode`**: A monotonically increasing **positive integer** (typical ceiling **2,100,000,000**).
> - **Semantic Versioning (SemVer)** standard: **MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH** (e.g., `2.4.1`).
> - **Default Android signing algorithm**: **APK Signature Scheme v2/v3/v4** (v1 = JAR signing for legacy).
> - **iOS deployment target**: Minimum iOS version (e.g., iOS 15+).

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Mobile-IoT

The convergence of **mobile apps + IoT devices** creates a **dual-deployment problem**:
- Deploy the **mobile companion app** to millions of user phones.
- Deploy the **firmware/sketch** to thousands of microcontrollers (ESP32, Arduino, Raspberry Pi) that the app communicates with.

A single failed OTA push can **brick an entire fleet** of deployed IoT devices ‚Äî hence the rigorous packaging, signing, and staged rollout pipeline.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 7-Stage Deployment Pipeline

Mobile-IoT deployment is a **sequential dependency chain**. Each gate must succeed before proceeding:

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 1 ‚Äî Source Commit**
> Developer pushes code to Git. A webhook (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins) triggers the pipeline.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 2 ‚Äî Static Analysis & Lint**
> Tools: **Android Lint, SonarQube, Detekt (Kotlin), ESLint (JS/React Native)**. Catches security flaws, deprecated APIs, and accessibility issues.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 3 ‚Äî Unit & Instrumentation Testing**
> Runs **JUnit**, **Espresso**, **XCTest** suites. Code coverage target: typically **‚â• 70%**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 4 ‚Äî Build & Packaging**
> - **Android**: `./gradlew assembleRelease` ‚Üí produces **APK** or `./gradlew bundleRelease` ‚Üí produces **AAB**.
> - **iOS**: `xcodebuild -archivePath` ‚Üí produces **IPA**.
> - **IoT Firmware**: `arduino-cli compile`, `platformio run`, or `esptool.py`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 5 ‚Äî Code Signing & Obfuscation**
> - **ProGuard / R8** for code shrinking and obfuscation in Android.
> - **APK Signature Scheme v2/v3** cryptographically signs the package.
> - **iOS** uses **Provisioning Profiles** + **Distribution Certificate** via Apple Developer Portal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 6 ‚Äî Distribution**
> - **Internal**: Firebase App Distribution, Microsoft AppCenter, Diawi, TestFlight.
> - **External**: Google Play Console (Internal/Closed/Open Testing ‚Üí Production), Apple App Store Connect.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 7 ‚Äî Monitoring & OTA Rollout**
> Tools: **Firebase Crashlytics, Google Play Console Vitals, AWS IoT Jobs, Azure IoT Hub Device Update, Balena Cloud**.

## 2.2 The Manifest File ‚Äî The "DNA" of Your App

The **`AndroidManifest.xml`** declares everything the OS and Play Store need to know. For IoT apps, the following tags are **high-yield**:

```xml
<manifest xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    package="com.ktu.iotcompanion"
    android:versionCode="42"
    android:versionName="1.6.0">

    <uses-sdk
        android:minSdkVersion="24"
        android:targetSdkVersion="34" />

    <!-- IoT-specific permissions -->
    <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.BLUETOOTH_SCAN" />
    <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.BLUETOOTH_CONNECT" />
    <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION" />
    <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.INTERNET" />
    <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.ACCESS_NETWORK_STATE" />

    <!-- IoT feature declaration -->
    <uses-feature
        android:name="android.hardware.bluetooth_le"
        android:required="true" />

    <application
        android:label="@string/app_name"
        android:icon="@mipmap/ic_launcher"
        android:allowBackup="false"
        android:theme="@style/AppTheme">
        <activity android:name=".MainActivity" android:exported="true">
            <intent-filter>
                <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" />
                <category android:name="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER" />
            </intent-filter>
        </activity>
    </application>
</manifest>
```

## 2.3 Code Signing ‚Äî Cryptographic Foundation

Code signing uses **asymmetric cryptography**:

- A **private key** (kept secret in keystore) signs the APK.
- A **public certificate** (embedded in the APK) is verified by the OS at install time.

> [!WARNING]
> **Losing the upload keystore means losing the ability to push updates to your published app on Google Play.** Always back up `*.jks` and the associated passwords in a **password manager + secure cloud vault**.

### Signing Scheme Evolution

| Scheme | Year | Vulnerability Addressed | Speed |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **v1 (JAR)** | Pre-2017 | None (original) | Slow |
| **v2** | 2017 | Janus vulnerability (CVE-2017-13156) | Fast |
| **v3** | 2018 | Key rotation support | Fast |
| **v4** | 2021 | Faster incremental installs (Android 11+) | Fastest |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Note |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Semantic Versioning** | `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH` (e.g., `2.4.1`) | Increment PATCH for bugfix, MINOR for new feature, MAJOR for breaking change. |
| **Android Version Code** | `versionCode ‚àà ‚Ñ§^+`, strictly increasing | Integer, no upper bound in spec (but $\leq 2{,}100{,}000{,}000$ in practice). |
| **App Bundle Size Limit** | `Base = 150 MB` ; `Asset Packs = 4 GB` | Bytes |
| **APK Size Limit** | `Base = 150 MB` ; `Expansion = 2 GB` | Bytes |
| **Code Coverage Target** | `Coverage = (Lines Tested) / (Total Lines) √ó 100%` | Percentage |
| **OTA Delta Update Size** | `Œîsize = Size(new_firmware) ‚àí Size(common_blocks)` | Bytes (saves bandwidth) |
| **APK Download Threshold** | `S_threshold = 100 MB` (warns user on cellular) | Bytes |
| **R8 / ProGuard Reduction** | `Reduction = 1 ‚àí (S_shrunk / S_original)` | Ratio (typical: 0.4‚Äì0.7) |
| **App Bundle Compression Ratio** | `C = S_APK_universal / S_AAB_per_device` | Typically `C ‚âà 1.2` to `1.5` |
| **SemVer Precedence** | `1.0.0-alpha < 1.0.0-beta < 1.0.0` | String-comparison rules apply. |
| **Min SDK Growth (KTU trend)** | `minSdkVersion` rises ~1 per year | Currently recommended: **API 24+** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the table above, vertical bars were replaced with `\vert` / `\mid` where absolute value notation was needed, to comply with markdown table syntax.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Tesla OTA** ‚Äî Pushes firmware to ~5 million cars using staged delta updates.
2. **Philips Hue Bridge** ‚Äî Deploys signed firmware via a paired mobile app gateway.
3. **Google Play Dynamic Delivery** ‚Äî Generates per-device optimized APKs from a single AAB, saving an average of **15%** in download size.
4. **AWS IoT Jobs** ‚Äî Schedules OTA jobs across a fleet with built-in rollback if failure rate exceeds threshold.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Android `versionCode` Progression Strategy

A common KTU question: *"Design a versioning strategy for an IoT companion app released weekly for 2 years."*

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let:
- $N_{\text{weeks}} = $ number of weekly releases in 2 years
- $N_{\text{minor}} = $ number of major feature releases per year
- $C_0 = $ starting `versionCode`

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{weeks}} &= 2 \text{ years} \times 52 \text{ weeks/year} = 104 \text{ releases} \\
N_{\text{minor per year}} &= 4 \text{ (quarterly minor bumps)} \\
N_{\text{minor total}} &= 4 \times 2 = 8 \text{ minor versions} \\
N_{\text{hotfix per minor}} &= \frac{104 - 8}{8} = 12 \text{ hotfixes per minor (approx.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Encoding rule** (base-1000 packing into a single integer `versionCode`):

$$
\text{versionCode} = (\text{MAJOR} \times 10^6) + (\text{MINOR} \times 10^3) + \text{PATCH}
$$

**Worked example for `v1.2.3`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{versionCode} &= (1 \times 1{,}000{,}000) + (2 \times 1{,}000) + 3 \\
&= 1{,}000{,}000 + 2{,}000 + 3 \\
&= 1{,}002{,}003
\end{aligned}
$$

**Validation:** monotonically increasing since MAJOR $\geq$ MINOR $\geq$ PATCH components dominate successively higher digit groups.

> [!NOTE]
> Google Play rejects `versionCode` values **greater than 2,100,000,000**. Therefore this scheme supports up to MAJOR=2099 without overflow.

## 3.2 Derivation: App Bundle Size Optimization

When publishing as **AAB**, Google Play generates per-device split APKs. The savings are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{universal APK}} &= S_{\text{base}} + S_{\text{all_resources}} + S_{\text{all_ABIs}} \\
S_{\text{device split APK}} &= S_{\text{base}} + S_{\text{device_resources}} + S_{\text{device_ABI}} \\
\text{Savings} &= S_{\text{universal APK}} - S_{\text{device split APK}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical example** (a typical IoT app with 4 ABIs √ó 4 densities √ó 2 languages):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{base}} &= 8 \text{ MB} \\
S_{\text{per ABI native libs}} &= 6 \text{ MB} \times 4 = 24 \text{ MB} \\
S_{\text{per density resources}} &= 4 \text{ MB} \times 4 = 16 \text{ MB} \\
S_{\text{per language strings}} &= 0.5 \text{ MB} \times 2 = 1 \text{ MB} \\
S_{\text{universal APK}} &= 8 + 24 + 16 + 1 = 49 \text{ MB} \\
S_{\text{device split APK}} &= 8 + 6 + 4 + 0.5 = 18.5 \text{ MB} \\
\text{Savings per device} &= 49 - 18.5 = 30.5 \text{ MB} \;(\approx 62\% \text{ reduction})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 frequently tests this calculation as a **"compare APK vs AAB"** 7-mark problem.

## 3.3 Derivation: OTA Delta Update Bandwidth Calculation

For IoT firmware updates, full-firmware OTA is wasteful. A **delta (binary diff)** approach is used.

Let:
- $S_{\text{full}} = $ total firmware size
- $S_{\text{common}} = $ size of unchanged binary blocks
- $S_{\text{new}} = $ size of new/changed blocks
- $R_{\text{reuse}} = $ block reuse ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{delta}} &= S_{\text{full}} - S_{\text{common}} \\
R_{\text{reuse}} &= \frac{S_{\text{common}}}{S_{\text{full}}} \\
S_{\text{delta}} &= S_{\text{full}} \times (1 - R_{\text{reuse}})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked example** for an ESP32 IoT node (4 MB flash, monthly security patch touching 5% of code):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{full}} &= 4{,}194{,}304 \text{ bytes (4 MB)} \\
R_{\text{reuse}} &= 0.95 \text{ (only 5% changed)} \\
S_{\text{delta}} &= 4{,}194{,}304 \times (1 - 0.95) \\
&= 4{,}194{,}304 \times 0.05 \\
&= 209{,}715.2 \text{ bytes} \approx 205 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Bandwidth saved:** $4{,}194{,}304 - 209{,}715 = 3{,}984{,}589$ bytes ‚âà **95% reduction** ‚Äî critical for cellular-IoT (NB-IoT, LTE-M) where data costs ‚Çπ/KB.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Deployment Channel Comparison

| Channel | Audience | Review Time | OTA Support | IoT-Friendly |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Google Play Internal Testing** | ‚â§ 100 testers | Instant | Yes (via Play) | Low |
| **Google Play Closed Track** | ‚â§ 10,000 testers | Hours | Yes | Medium |
| **Google Play Production** | Public | 1‚Äì7 days | Yes (staged) | High |
| **Firebase App Distribution** | Unlimited testers | Instant | Yes (manual) | **High** |
| **Apple TestFlight** | ‚â§ 10,000 (internal + external) | 24‚Äì48 hr | Yes | Medium |
| **Apple App Store** | Public | 24 hr ‚Äì 7 days | Yes | High |
| **Side-loading (APK/IPA)** | Manual install | None | No | **Very High** (dev only) |
| **Balena Cloud / OTA** | IoT fleet | Instant | **Yes (delta)** | **Very High** |

## 3.5 Build Pipeline (Gradle) ‚Äî Step-by-Step Execution

```gradle
// android/app/build.gradle (excerpt)
android {
    compileSdkVersion 34

    defaultConfig {
        applicationId "com.ktu.iotcompanion"
        minSdkVersion 24
        targetSdkVersion 34
        versionCode 1002003      // v1.2.3
        versionName "1.2.3"
    }

    signingConfigs {
        release {
            storeFile file("keystore/release.jks")
            storePassword System.getenv("KEYSTORE_PWD")
            keyAlias "ktu-iot-upload"
            keyPassword System.getenv("KEY_PWD")
        }
    }

    buildTypes {
        release {
            minifyEnabled true        // R8/ProGuard
            shrinkResources true      // remove unused resources
            signingConfig signingConfigs.release
            proguardFiles getDefaultProguardFile('proguard-android-optimize.txt'), 'proguard-rules.pro'
        }
    }

    bundle {
        density { enableSplit true }
        abi     { enableSplit true }
        language { enableSplit true }
    }
}
```

**Execution commands:**

```bash
# 1. Clean previous builds
./gradlew clean

# 2. Run lint + unit tests
./gradlew lint test

# 3. Build AAB (preferred for Play Store)
./gradlew bundleRelease

# 4. Build APK (for sideloading / Firebase)
./gradlew assembleRelease

# 5. Verify signature
apksigner verify --verbose app-release.apk

# 6. Upload to Play via fastlane or Play Console
fastlane supply --track production --aab app-release.aab
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Never** commit `keystore/*.jks` or `*.properties` files containing passwords to public Git repositories. Use **Gradle properties** outside the repo, environment variables, or **Google Play App Signing** with auto-generated upload keys.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Mobile-IoT Deployment Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Developer Commit to Git] --> B[Webhook Trigger CI CD]
    B --> C[Static Analysis Lint]
    C --> D[Unit and Instrumentation Tests]
    D --> E{All Tests Pass?}
    E -->|No| F[Notify Developer via Slack or Email]
    E -->|Yes| G[Gradle Build assembleRelease]
    G --> H[Generate AAB and APK]
    H --> I[R8 Obfuscation and Shrinking]
    I --> J[APK Signing v2 v3 v4 with Keystore]
    J --> K[Upload to Internal Testing Track]
    K --> L[Internal QA on Real Devices]
    L --> M{All Checks Pass?}
    M -->|No| N[Rollback to Last Green Build]
    M -->|Yes| O[Promote to Closed Beta Track]
    O --> P[Promote to Production Release]
    P --> Q[Staged Rollout 1 percent 5 percent 25 percent 100 percent]
    Q --> R[Monitor Crashlytics and Vitals]
    R --> S{Error Rate Above Threshold?}
    S -->|Yes| T[Halt Rollout and Trigger OTA Rollback]
    S -->|No| U[Release Complete]
    R --> V[Push Companion IoT Firmware via AWS IoT Jobs or Balena]
    V --> W[Devices Receive Delta Update]
    W --> X[ACK to Cloud and Update Firmware Version]
```

## 4.2 OTA Update Lifecycle for IoT Fleet

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Build New IoT Firmware in CI] --> B[Sign Firmware with Vendor Private Key]
    B --> C[Generate Delta Diff against Latest Stable]
    C --> D[Upload Firmware to Cloud Bucket with Version Metadata]
    D --> E[Cloud Job Scheduler Selects Target Devices]
    E --> F[Push Notification to Mobile Companion App]
    F --> G{Mobile App Proxies Update to IoT Device}
    G -->|Yes| H[Device Downloads Delta from Cloud via BLE or WiFi]
    G -->|No| I[Device Downloads Directly from Cloud]
    H --> J[Verify Signature Using Vendor Public Key]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Signature Valid?}
    K -->|No| L[Abort and Log Security Alert]
    K -->|Yes| M[Apply Delta and Reboot Device]
    M --> N{Boot Successful and Health Checks Pass?}
    N -->|No| O[Rollback to Previous Firmware from Backup Slot]
    N -->|Yes| P[Mark Device as Updated in Cloud Registry]
    P --> Q[Mobile App Shows Update Success Notification]
```

## 4.3 APK Signing and Verification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Developer Private Key in JKS Keystore] --> B[Sign APK with apksigner]
    B --> C[APK contains v1 v2 v3 Signatures]
    C --> D[Upload to Google Play]
    D --> E[User Downloads APK from Play Store]
    E --> F[Android PackageManager Extracts Public Certificate]
    F --> G{Verify Against System Trust Store}
    G -->|Match| H[Install Granted]
    G -->|Mismatch| I[Install Blocked with Security Warning]
    H --> J[App Launched with Signed Identity]
    J --> K[App Can Now Authenticate with IoT Cloud APIs]
```

## 4.4 Versioning Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Change in Codebase] --> B{Is it a Breaking API Change?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Increment MAJOR Reset MINOR and PATCH to 0]
    B -->|No| D{Is it a New Backwards Compatible Feature?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Increment MINOR Reset PATCH to 0]
    D -->|No| F[Increment PATCH for Bugfix]
    C --> G[Update versionName in build.gradle]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Increment versionCode Monotonically]
    H --> I[Tag Git Release v1 2 3]
    I --> J[Trigger Release Pipeline]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## üìò PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between APK and AAB. Why is AAB the preferred publishing format for Google Play in 2024?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | **APK (Android Package Kit)** | **AAB (Android App Bundle)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Format** | Ready-to-install single file | Publishing format (not directly installable) |
| **Contents** | All resources, ABIs, densities bundled | Modular, deferred delivery |
| **Distribution** | Sideload or self-host | Google Play only |
| **Size on device** | Larger (universal binary) | Smaller (per-device optimized) |
| **Upload to Play** | Legacy, deprecated for new apps | Mandatory since Aug 2021 |
| **Generation** | `assembleRelease` | `bundleRelease` |

**Why AAB is preferred (1 mark):**
- Reduces per-device download size by **~15‚Äì60%** via dynamic delivery of splits (ABI, density, language).
- Enables **Play Asset Delivery** and **Play Feature Delivery** for modular apps.
- Mandatory upload format for **new Google Play apps since August 2021**.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî June 2024]`
**Explain the role of `versionCode` and `versionName` in the Android Manifest. Can two APKs with the same `versionName` but different `versionCode` coexist on a device?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **`versionName` (String):** A *human-readable* version label shown to users in the Play Store and *Settings ‚Üí About*. Example: `"1.6.0"`. It is **not** used for update logic.

- **`versionCode` (Integer):** A *machine-readable* monotonically increasing integer used by the Android OS and Play Store to determine **update eligibility**. Example: `42`.

- **Coexistence rule (1 mark):** **No.** Android does **not** allow two APKs of the **same applicationId** to coexist regardless of versionCode or versionName. The system always replaces the existing app during installation, preserving user data if signatures match.

---

## üìó PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each ‚Äî Internal Choice)

### ‚úèÔ∏è QUESTION A (14 Marks) ‚Äî `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the **complete Android app deployment pipeline** from source code commit to Play Store production release, listing all major stages and their associated tools. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain **APK Signature Schemes v1, v2, v3, and v4** with their respective security improvements. What happens if a developer loses the upload keystore? **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a):

The deployment pipeline consists of **seven sequential stages**:

1. **Source Control & Commit (1 mark)** ‚Äî Code pushed to Git (GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket). A webhook triggers CI/CD (Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI).

2. **Static Analysis & Lint (1 mark)** ‚Äî Android Lint, SonarQube, Detekt (Kotlin), or Checkstyle. Catches null-pointer issues, deprecated API usage, and security flaws.

3. **Automated Testing (1 mark)** ‚Äî JUnit for unit tests, Espresso for UI tests, Firebase Test Lab for device matrix testing. Target coverage ‚â• 70%.

4. **Build & Package (1 mark)** ‚Äî `./gradlew bundleRelease` produces an `.aab`. R8/ProGuard obfuscates and shrinks code.

5. **Code Signing (1 mark)** ‚Äî Upload key signs the AAB with v2/v3/v4 schemes. Google Play re-signs with its app-signing key.

6. **Distribution (1 mark)** ‚Äî First to **Internal Testing** track (‚â§100 testers, instant), then **Closed** (‚â§10,000), then **Production** with **staged rollout** (1% ‚Üí 5% ‚Üí 25% ‚Üí 100%).

7. **Monitoring & Rollback (1 mark)** ‚Äî Crashlytics, Play Console Vitals, and Firebase Performance Monitor track release health. If crash rate exceeds threshold, rollout is **halted** and a previous version is promoted.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b):

**APK Signing Scheme Evolution (5 marks):**

| Scheme | Year | Mechanism | Vulnerability Addressed |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **v1 (JAR)** | Pre-2017 | Each entry in ZIP signed with JAR signature | None (original) |
| **v2** | 2017 | Signs the entire APK byte-blob before ZIP alignment | **Janus (CVE-2017-13156)** ‚Äî attacker could prepend bytes to APK |
| **v3** | 2018 | Adds **key rotation** support via `signingCertificate` lineage | Lost/stolen upload keys |
| **v4** | 2021 | Computes a hash of APK for **incremental install** (Android 11+) | Large install time on huge APKs |

**Loss of Upload Keystore (2 marks):**
- If **Play App Signing** is enabled (mandatory since 2021), Google retains the **app signing key** separately. The developer can generate a new **upload key**, register it via Play Console, and request a key reset.
- If **Play App Signing is NOT enabled**, the developer **permanently loses** the ability to push updates. They must publish a **new app** with a different `applicationId` and migrate users.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **upload key** (developer-controlled) with **app signing key** (Google-controlled after 2021). Clearly distinguishing these in your answer earns full marks.

---

### ‚úèÔ∏è QUESTION B (14 Marks) ‚Äî `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî June 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **OTA (Over-The-Air) update mechanism** for an IoT device fleet managed by a mobile companion app. Discuss the **security challenges**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A startup releases an Android IoT app. They have **4 ABIs** (armeabi-v7a, arm64-v8a, x86, x86_64) and **4 screen densities** (ldpi, mdpi, hdpi, xhdpi). Each native lib is **6 MB**, each density resource set is **4 MB**, and the base app is **8 MB**. **[7 marks]**

    **(i)** Calculate the size of a **universal APK** that includes all ABIs and densities.

    **(ii)** Calculate the size of a **device-specific split APK** for a Pixel 7 (arm64-v8a + xxhdpi).

    **(iii)** Calculate the **percentage size reduction** achieved by using AAB.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a):

**OTA Update Mechanism (5 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cloud Build Server] --> B[Sign Firmware with Vendor Key]
    B --> C[Upload to Cloud Storage]
    C --> D[Push Notification to Companion App]
    D --> E[App Relays Update Command to IoT Device]
    E --> F[Device Downloads Delta from CDN]
    F --> G[Verify Signature]
    G --> H[Apply Patch in A B Partition Scheme]
    H --> I[Reboot and Validate]
    I --> J[ACK to Cloud]
```

**Step-by-step flow:**
1. CI pipeline builds and signs new firmware ‚Üí uploads to cloud (AWS S3, Azure Blob).
2. Companion mobile app receives a **silent push** (Firebase Cloud Messaging).
3. App proxies the update to the IoT device via **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)** or **local Wi-Fi**.
4. Device downloads **delta diff** (only changed blocks) to save bandwidth.
5. Device **verifies the RSA/ECDSA signature** using a vendor public key burned into ROM.
6. If signature valid, applies patch using **A/B partition scheme** (dual-bank flash) to avoid bricking.
7. Reboots, runs **health checks**, then ACKs to cloud.
8. If health checks fail, **rolls back** to previous slot automatically.

**Security Challenges (2 marks):**
- **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** ‚Üí Mitigated by TLS 1.3 + signed firmware.
- **Replay attacks** ‚Üí Mitigated by monotonic version counters and signed nonces.
- **Compromised signing key** ‚Üí Mitigated by **hardware security modules (HSM)** and key rotation.
- **Rollback to vulnerable version** ‚Üí Mitigated by minimum-version enforcement on device.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b):

**(i) Universal APK Size (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{base}} &= 8 \text{ MB} \\
S_{\text{all ABIs}} &= 6 \text{ MB} \times 4 = 24 \text{ MB} \\
S_{\text{all densities}} &= 4 \text{ MB} \times 4 = 16 \text{ MB} \\
S_{\text{universal}} &= 8 + 24 + 16 = \mathbf{48 \text{ MB}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Device-Specific Split APK for Pixel 7 (arm64-v8a + xxhdpi) (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{base}} &= 8 \text{ MB} \\
S_{\text{arm64 native}} &= 6 \text{ MB} \times 1 = 6 \text{ MB} \\
S_{\text{xxhdpi resources}} &= 4 \text{ MB} \times 1 = 4 \text{ MB} \\
S_{\text{split}} &= 8 + 6 + 4 = \mathbf{18 \text{ MB}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** If xxhdpi is treated as ‚âà1.5√ó the mdpi base, some files are scaled and may be ~6 MB, giving 20 MB. Either 18 or 20 MB is acceptable; examiners award full credit for clear method.

**(iii) Percentage Reduction (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reduction} &= \frac{S_{\text{universal}} - S_{\text{split}}}{S_{\text{universal}}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{48 - 18}{48} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{30}{48} \times 100\% \\
&= \mathbf{62.5\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to include the base `S_base` in both APK sizes (treating it as already counted). Always include base + ABI + density in **both** the universal AND split calculations. Also, do not confuse **dpi** (e.g., xxhdpi) with **density qualifiers** in Android resource directories (`drawable-xxhdpi`).

---

## üîÅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist ‚Äî KTU 2024 Exam Day**

- ‚úÖ **APK** = installable; **AAB** = publishing format (mandatory on Play Store since Aug 2021).
- ‚úÖ **`versionName` = human-readable**; **`versionCode` = monotonically increasing integer** for update logic.
- ‚úÖ **APK Signature Scheme v2** fixed the **Janus vulnerability (CVE-2017-13156)**.
- ‚úÖ **Google Play App Signing** stores the app signing key; the developer retains only the **upload key**.
- ‚úÖ **Losing the upload key** without Play App Signing = **permanent loss** of update capability.
- ‚úÖ **AAB dynamic delivery** typically saves **15‚Äì60%** download size via per-device split APKs.
- ‚úÖ **R8/ProGuard** performs code shrinking + obfuscation (replaces older ProGuard in AGP 3.4+).
- ‚úÖ **OTA updates** for IoT use **delta diffs** + **A/B partition scheme** to prevent bricking.
- ‚úÖ **OTA signature verification** uses **vendor public key burned into device ROM** at manufacture.
- ‚úÖ **Semantic Versioning** = `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH`; pre-release tags like `-alpha`, `-beta` use dot-separated identifiers.
- ‚úÖ **CI/CD tools**: Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Bitrise (mobile-specific), fastlane (release automation).
- ‚úÖ **Distribution channels**: Internal Testing ‚Üí Closed ‚Üí Open ‚Üí Production; each with staged rollout.
- ‚úÖ **IoT-specific permissions**: `BLUETOOTH_SCAN`, `BLUETOOTH_CONNECT`, `ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION`, `NEARBY_WIFI_DEVICES` (Android 13+).
- ‚úÖ **Monitoring tools**: Firebase Crashlytics, Play Console Vitals, Sentry, New Relic Mobile.
- ‚úÖ **Staged rollout rule of thumb**: 1% ‚Üí 5% ‚Üí 25% ‚Üí 50% ‚Üí 100% with **24‚Äì48 hour soak time** per stage.
- ‚úÖ **Side-loading APKs** is allowed for development but triggers **"Install from Unknown Sources"** prompt on user devices.
- ‚úÖ **Delta update formula**: `S_delta = S_full √ó (1 ‚àí R_reuse)` where `R_reuse` is the block reuse ratio.
- ‚úÖ **AAB savings formula**: `Savings = S_universal_APK ‚àí S_device_split_APK`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interaction with Server Side Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT604 - MOBILE APPLICATIONS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Interaction with Server Side Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Server-Side Interaction in Mobile IoT** refers to the bidirectional communication mechanism by which a mobile application (acting as a client) exchanges data, requests processing, and receives responses from a remote server through standardized network protocols. In the context of IoT, this server may be a cloud platform, a gateway, a message broker, or an enterprise backend that processes sensor data, executes business logic, and returns actionable information to the mobile user interface.

The interaction is mediated by **Application Programming Interfaces (APIs)**, predominantly **REST (Representational State Transfer)** or **WebSocket-based** services, transported over **HTTP/HTTPS** at the application layer, with payload formats encoded in **JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)** or **XML (eXtensible Markup Language)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Server-side interaction is the *backbone* of every modern mobile IoT application. Without it, a mobile device is merely a display terminal; with it, the device becomes a window into a global, distributed, intelligent control system.

### Key Architectural Components

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **Mobile Client (App)** | Initiates HTTP requests, parses responses, renders UI |
| **API Gateway** | Routes, authenticates, throttles, and logs incoming API calls |
| **Application Server** | Executes business logic, orchestrates workflows |
| **Database Server** | Persists structured (SQL) or semi-structured (NoSQL) data |
| **IoT Message Broker** | Mediates asynchronous pub/sub messaging (e.g., MQTT) |
| **Authentication Service** | Issues and validates tokens (OAuth 2.0, JWT) |

### Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The Restaurant Waiter

Imagine you (the **mobile app**) sitting in a restaurant. The **kitchen** is the server, and the **waiter** is the API.

1. You study the menu (the **API documentation**), and you place an order (the **HTTP request**).
2. The waiter carries the order slip to the kitchen (the **network transport**).
3. The kitchen prepares the dish and the waiter brings it back (the **HTTP response** with a **JSON payload**).
4. You do not enter the kitchen, touch the stove, or see the chef ‚Äî the **abstraction** keeps things simple and secure.

In IoT, your "order" might be: *"Give me the last 24 hours of temperature readings from sensor S-47 in factory zone B."* The server queries its database, perhaps aggregates readings from an MQTT broker, and sends back a compact JSON array.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** The mobile app *never* talks directly to the database. It always goes through a well-defined, secured **API contract**. This is the principle of **separation of concerns**.

### Standard Metrics Worth Knowing

- **Latency:** Time taken for a round-trip request-response, measured in **milliseconds (ms)**. KTU board examinations often cite values between **50 ms** and **500 ms** for healthy mobile APIs.
- **Throughput:** Number of requests served per unit time, typically expressed in **Requests Per Second (RPS)**.
- **Payload Size:** Size of the request/response body, measured in **bytes (B)** or **kilobytes (KB)**.
- **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF):** Average operational lifetime before server failure, in **hours**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
