## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Request‚ÄìResponse Lifecycle (Step-by-Step)

The interaction between a mobile app and a server follows a deterministic lifecycle:

1. **DNS Resolution:** The mobile app converts the human-readable hostname (e.g., `api.iotcloud.com`) into an IP address using the Domain Name System. Typical resolution time: **20‚Äì120 ms**.
2. **TCP Handshake:** A three-way SYN ‚Üí SYN-ACK ‚Üí ACK is performed to establish a reliable connection. Cost: **1 RTT (Round Trip Time)**.
3. **TLS Handshake (if HTTPS):** A cryptographic handshake negotiates session keys. Cost: **1‚Äì2 RTTs**, depending on TLS version (1.2 vs 1.3).
4. **HTTP Request Transmission:** The client sends method, headers, and optional body.
   - Common methods: **GET** (retrieve), **POST** (create), **PUT** (replace), **PATCH** (partial update), **DELETE** (remove).
5. **Server Processing:** The server authenticates the request, queries databases, runs business logic.
6. **HTTP Response Transmission:** The server returns a status code and a payload.
   - Status code classes: **1xx** (informational), **2xx** (success), **3xx** (redirection), **4xx** (client error), **5xx** (server error).
7. **Connection Management:** With HTTP/1.1 keep-alive or HTTP/2 multiplexing, the TCP connection is reused for subsequent requests.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why HTTP/2 Matters for IoT:** HTTP/2 introduces **header compression (HPACK)** and **binary framing**, reducing payload size by roughly **30‚Äì50%** compared to HTTP/1.1. For battery-powered IoT gateways, this translates directly into reduced radio-on time and energy savings.

### 2.2 Data Interchange Formats

#### JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)
- **Lightweight**, text-based, human-readable.
- Built on two structures: **objects** (key‚Äìvalue pairs) and **arrays** (ordered lists).
- Typical IoT sensor payload in JSON:
  ```json
  {
    "deviceId": "S-47",
    "timestamp": 1718901234,
    "temperature": 27.4,
    "humidity": 62.1,
    "battery": 89
  }
  ```
- Average size overhead: **~20 bytes per field** (braces, quotes, commas).

#### XML (eXtensible Markup Language)
- **Verbose**, tag-based, supports namespaces and schemas (XSD).
- Used historically in **SOAP** web services and **industrial IoT (OPC-UA)** stacks.
- Overhead is typically **2√ó to 5√ó** larger than the equivalent JSON.

### 2.3 REST Architectural Constraints (The "6 Pillars")

A **RESTful API** is not merely an HTTP API; it must obey six constraints defined by **Roy Fielding (2000)**:

| # | Constraint | Practical Implication |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Client‚ÄìServer** | UI and data storage evolve independently |
| 2 | **Statelessness** | Each request carries all info; server holds no session state |
| 3 | **Cacheability** | Responses must declare themselves cacheable or not |
| 4 | **Uniform Interface** | Standardized resource URIs and HTTP verbs |
| 5 | **Layered System** | Client cannot tell if it is talking to origin or proxy |
| 6 | **Code-on-Demand (optional)** | Server can send executable code (e.g., JavaScript) |

### 2.4 Authentication and Authorization

| Mechanism | Use Case | Security Level |
|---|---|---|
| **API Key** | Simple scripts, low-risk devices | Low |
| **Basic Auth (Base64)** | Legacy systems, internal tools | Very Low |
| **OAuth 2.0** | Third-party delegated access (e.g., Google login) | High |
| **JWT (JSON Web Token)** | Stateless session management | High |
| **mTLS (Mutual TLS)** | Device-to-cloud in industrial IoT | Very High |
| **HMAC Signatures** | Webhook verification | High |

> [!NOTE]
> **JWT Anatomy:** A JSON Web Token consists of three Base64-URL-encoded segments separated by dots: `header.payload.signature`. The signature is computed using **HMAC-SHA256** or an **RSA/ECDSA** private key.

### 2.5 Real-Time and Push Communication

For mobile IoT, polling the server is wasteful. Two modern alternatives dominate:

- **WebSockets (RFC 6455):** A full-duplex channel over a single TCP connection. Used for dashboards, chat, live sensor streams. Latency: **~10‚Äì50 ms**.
- **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport):** A publish/subscribe protocol over TCP, designed for constrained devices. Brokers include **Mosquitto**, **HiveMQ**, **AWS IoT Core**. Uses topics such as `factory/zone-B/sensor/S-47/temp`.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $RTT = 2 \times t_{prop} + t_{proc}$ | Round-Trip Time (propagation + processing) | seconds (s) |
| $T_{total} = T_{DNS} + T_{TCP} + T_{TLS} + T_{req} + T_{server} + T_{resp}$ | Total API call latency | seconds (s) |
| $Throughput = \dfrac{N_{req}}{\Delta t}$ | Requests served per interval | req/s |
| $Payload_{JSON} \approx n_{fields} \times 20$ bytes | Approximate JSON overhead | bytes (B) |
| $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (wireless link) | decibels (dB) |
| $P_{tx} = V \times I$ | Power consumed by radio transmission | watts (W) |
| $E_{bit} = \dfrac{P_{tx}}{R_{bit}}$ | Energy per transmitted bit | joules per bit (J/bit) |
| $Availability = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$ | System uptime percentage | percent (%) |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in **mobile network planning**, **IoT gateway sizing**, **API performance benchmarking**, and **battery-life estimation** for field-deployed IoT sensors. A 4G LTE typical **$RTT$** is around **50‚Äì100 ms**, while 5G reduces it to **~10 ms**, dramatically improving the responsiveness of mobile IoT control loops.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Latency of a Mobile API Call

We model the full latency $T_{total}$ experienced by a mobile user from the moment the *Send* button is tapped to the moment the response is rendered on screen.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Identify the contributing delays:**

$$
T_{total} = T_{DNS} + T_{TCP} + T_{TLS} + T_{HTTP} + T_{server} + T_{render}
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Express each component in terms of RTT:**

The DNS lookup requires a full RTT to the resolver, the TCP handshake is one RTT, and the TLS 1.2 handshake is two RTTs. Therefore:

$$
T_{DNS} = 1 \times RTT
$$

$$
T_{TCP} = 1 \times RTT
$$

$$
T_{TLS\,1.2} = 2 \times RTT
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Combine the connection-establishment overhead:**

$$
T_{conn} = T_{DNS} + T_{TCP} + T_{TLS} = 4 \times RTT
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Express HTTP request and response transmission:**

The HTTP request travels one way (half RTT) and the response travels one way (half RTT). For a small payload where $t_{transmission} \ll t_{propagation}$, we approximate:

$$
T_{HTTP} \approx 1 \times RTT
$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Server processing time:**

This is application-specific. A simple database query may take **$T_{server} \approx 20$ ms**; an analytics pipeline with machine-learning inference may take **$T_{server} \approx 500$ ms**.

**Step 6 ‚Äî Render time on the mobile UI:**

For a 60 fps display, a single frame is **$1000/60 \approx 16.67$ ms**. A list of 100 JSON items rendered in a `RecyclerView` may take **$T_{render} \approx 50$ ms** including JSON parsing.

**Step 7 ‚Äî Substitute numerical values:**

Assume a 4G connection with **$RTT = 80$ ms** and **$T_{server} = 20$ ms**, **$T_{render} = 50$ ms**.

$$
T_{total} = (4 \times 80) + (1 \times 80) + 20 + 50
$$

$$
T_{total} = 320 + 80 + 20 + 50 = 470 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 8 ‚Äî Conclude with a KTU-style result:**

$$
\boxed{T_{total} = 470 \text{ ms}}
$$

This is well within the **500 ms** threshold considered *acceptable* for mobile API calls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight for Board Exams:** When the question mentions "faster network," substitute a smaller RTT. For 5G with **$RTT = 10$ ms**, the same call would complete in **$T_{total} = 4(10) + 10 + 20 + 50 = 120$ ms**, a **3.9√ó speedup** purely from the network layer.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Energy Cost of Data Transmission in IoT

For battery-powered IoT nodes reporting to a mobile dashboard via a cellular link, energy efficiency is critical.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Define the bit energy:**

$$
E_{bit} = \frac{P_{tx}}{R_{bit}}
$$

where $P_{tx}$ is the transmit power in watts and $R_{bit}$ is the bit rate in bits per second.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Total energy for transmitting a payload of $N$ bits:**

$$
E_{total} = N \times E_{bit} = N \times \frac{P_{tx}}{R_{bit}}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Plug in typical LTE Cat-M1 values:**

Let $P_{tx} = 0.5$ W, $R_{bit} = 300$ kbps $= 300 \times 10^3$ bps, and payload $N = 1$ KB $= 8192$ bits.

$$
E_{total} = 8192 \times \frac{0.5}{300 \times 10^3}
$$

$$
E_{total} = 8192 \times 1.667 \times 10^{-6} \text{ J}
$$

$$
E_{total} \approx 0.01365 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Estimate battery life:**

A typical IoT coin cell stores **$E_{bat} = 2400$ mAh at 3 V**, which equals:

$$
E_{bat} = 2.4 \times 3 = 7.2 \text{ Wh} = 7.2 \times 3600 = 25920 \text{ J}
$$

Number of transmissions possible (assuming energy budget is the only constraint):

$$
N_{tx} = \frac{25920}{0.01365} \approx 1.9 \times 10^6 \text{ transmissions}
$$

If the device reports every 10 minutes, the theoretical lifetime is:

$$
\text{Lifetime} = \frac{1.9 \times 10^6 \times 10}{60 \times 24 \times 365} \approx 36 \text{ years}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world correction:** Self-discharge, sleep current, and protocol overhead reduce this by 90‚Äì99%. The KTU board typically accepts the theoretical derivation as the *expected* answer.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | REST/HTTP | WebSocket | MQTT |
|---|---|---|---|
| Communication Pattern | Request‚ÄìResponse | Full-Duplex | Publish‚ÄìSubscribe |
| Connection | Short-lived (or keep-alive) | Persistent | Persistent |
| Header Overhead | High (~200‚Äì800 B/req) | Low after handshake | Very Low (2-byte fixed header) |
| Best Suited For | CRUD operations | Live dashboards | Constrained IoT devices |
| Server Push | No (polling needed) | Yes | Yes (via broker) |
| Typical Latency | 50‚Äì500 ms | 10‚Äì50 ms | 20‚Äì100 ms |
| Battery Friendliness | Poor | Moderate | Excellent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Use **REST** for control commands, **WebSocket** for live visualization, and **MQTT** for telemetry from constrained devices. KTU examiners love this three-way distinction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Mobile IoT Client‚ÄìServer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Mobile["Mobile Device"]
        UI["User Interface Layer"]
        API_Client["API Client Library"]
        Cache["Local Cache SQLite or Realm"]
        UI --> API_Client
        API_Client --> Cache
    end

    subgraph Network["Network Layer"]
        DNS["DNS Resolver"]
        LB["Load Balancer"]
    end

    subgraph Cloud["Cloud Server Side"]
        Gateway["API Gateway"]
        Auth["Auth Service JWT or OAuth"]
        AppServer["Application Server"]
        DB[("Database SQL or NoSQL")]
        Broker["MQTT Broker"]
    end

    subgraph Devices["IoT Field Devices"]
        Sensor1["Temperature Sensor"]
        Sensor2["Humidity Sensor"]
        Actuator["Smart Relay"]
    end

    API_Client -->|HTTPS Request| DNS
    DNS --> LB
    LB --> Gateway
    Gateway --> Auth
    Auth --> AppServer
    AppServer --> DB
    AppServer --> Broker
    Broker <-->|MQTT Publish| Sensor1
    Broker <-->|MQTT Publish| Sensor2
    Broker -->|Command| Actuator
    AppServer -->|JSON Response| API_Client
```

### 4.2 REST API Request‚ÄìResponse Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant App as Mobile App
    participant Gateway as API Gateway
    participant Auth as Auth Service
    participant Server as App Server
    participant DB as Database

    App->>Gateway: GET /api/v1/sensors/S-47/readings  (Bearer JWT)
    Gateway->>Auth: Validate Token
    Auth-->>Gateway: 200 OK (user authorized)
    Gateway->>Server: Forward Request
    Server->>DB: SELECT * FROM readings WHERE device_id='S-47' LIMIT 100
    DB-->>Server: Result Set (rows)
    Server-->>Gateway: HTTP 200  (JSON payload)
    Gateway-->>App: HTTP 200  (JSON payload)
    App->>App: Parse JSON, Update UI
```

### 4.3 OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Flow (Used by Mobile Apps)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant App as Mobile App
    participant AuthServer as Authorization Server
    participant API as Resource Server

    User->>App: Tap "Login with Google"
    App->>AuthServer: Authorize Endpoint  (client_id, redirect_uri, scope)
    AuthServer-->>User: Login + Consent Screen
    User->>AuthServer: Grant Permission
    AuthServer-->>App: Authorization Code
    App->>AuthServer: Exchange Code for Access Token  (client_secret)
    AuthServer-->>App: access_token  +  refresh_token
    App->>API: GET /user/profile  (Authorization: Bearer access_token)
    API-->>App: 200 OK  (User Profile JSON)
```

### 4.4 MQTT Pub/Sub Topology for IoT Telemetry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Field["IoT Field"]
        S1["Sensor S-47"]
        S2["Sensor S-48"]
    end
    Broker[("MQTT Broker")]
    MobileApp["Mobile Dashboard"]
    Cloud["Cloud Analytics"]

    S1 -->|publish topic=factory/B/temp| Broker
    S2 -->|publish topic=factory/B/humid| Broker
    Broker -->|subscribe topic=factory/B/+| MobileApp
    Broker -->|subscribe topic=factory/B/#| Cloud
```

### 4.5 Error Handling Flow (HTTP Status Code Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([HTTP Response Received]) --> C1{Status Class?}
    C1 -->|2xx| OK[Render Data  Display Success Toast]
    C1 -->|3xx| Redir[Follow Location Header or Show Redirect Message]
    C1 -->|4xx| Client[Parse error JSON, Show Validation Error to User]
    C1 -->|5xx| Server[Log Error, Retry with Exponential Backoff, Show 'Try Again Later']
    Client --> Sub{Status Code?}
    Sub -->|401| Reauth[Trigger Re-login or Refresh Token]
    Sub -->|403| Perm[Show 'Permission Denied']
    Sub -->|404| NotFound[Show 'Resource Not Found']
    Sub -->|429| Throttle[Honor Retry-After Header, Back Off]
    Server --> Retry{Retries Less Than 3?}
    Retry -->|Yes| Wait[Wait 2^n seconds] --> RetryReq[Resend Request]
    Retry -->|No| Fail[Show Fatal Error Dialog]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024]**
*Define RESTful web services. List any four HTTP methods used in REST with their significance in mobile IoT applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for designing networked applications that uses stateless, client‚Äìserver communication over HTTP. It treats data as **resources** identified by URIs and manipulated using standard HTTP methods.

Four HTTP methods:
- **GET** ‚Äî Retrieves a resource (e.g., fetch sensor readings). Idempotent and cacheable.
- **POST** ‚Äî Creates a new resource (e.g., register a new IoT device). Non-idempotent.
- **PUT** ‚Äî Replaces an existing resource entirely (e.g., update device configuration).
- **DELETE** ‚Äî Removes a resource (e.g., decommission a sensor).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between JSON and XML as data interchange formats in mobile applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | JSON | XML |
|---|---|---|
| Syntax | Key‚Äìvalue pairs, minimal | Tag-based, verbose |
| Size | Smaller (no closing tags for primitives) | Larger (every tag has an open and close form) |
| Parsing Speed | Faster, native to JavaScript | Slower, requires a DOM/SAX parser |
| Schema Support | Optional (JSON Schema) | Strong (XSD, DTD) |
| Typical IoT Use | REST APIs, MQTT payloads | SOAP, OPC-UA, legacy SCADA |

**Conclusion:** JSON is preferred in modern mobile IoT due to its compactness and parsing efficiency.

---

### Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2024]

**(a)** Explain the client‚Äìserver architecture used in mobile IoT applications with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, describe the OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code flow used by a mobile app to access IoT cloud resources. Mention any two security benefits. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The client‚Äìserver architecture for mobile IoT consists of three logical tiers:

**1. Presentation Tier (Mobile Client):** The mobile application running on Android/iOS. It contains the UI layer, local cache, and API client library. The UI layer renders data using widgets (`RecyclerView`, `ListView`); the API client (Retrofit, Volley, OkHttp) constructs HTTP requests and deserializes JSON responses using libraries like Gson or Moshi.

**2. Logic Tier (Application Server):** Hosted on cloud platforms such as AWS, Azure, or Google Cloud. It contains the API Gateway (Kong, AWS API Gateway), authentication service, business logic (Node.js, Spring Boot, Django), and message broker (Mosquitto, RabbitMQ).

**3. Data Tier (Storage & Devices):** Comprises relational databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL), NoSQL stores (MongoDB, DynamoDB, InfluxDB for time-series), and the physical IoT devices communicating via MQTT, CoAP, or HTTP.

**Block Diagram** (refer to the Mermaid architecture diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes).

**Role of each layer:**
- *Client* ‚Üí initiates requests, renders responses, handles local state.
- *Gateway* ‚Üí authenticates, rate-limits, routes, logs.
- *App Server* ‚Üí enforces business rules, orchestrates workflows.
- *Database* ‚Üí persists historical data, supports analytics.
- *IoT Devices* ‚Üí sense and actuate; publish telemetry via broker.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Flow ‚Äî Example:**

Consider a mobile app **"SmartFarm"** that wants to read soil moisture data from a user's farm sensors stored on **AgriCloud**.

**Step 1:** The user taps *"Connect to AgriCloud"* in the SmartFarm app. The app opens the Authorization Server's login page in a secure `WebView` (or system browser via Android's *Chrome Custom Tabs*).

**Step 2:** The Authorization Server displays a consent screen listing the requested scopes: `read:sensors`, `write:actuators`.

**Step 3:** The user authenticates (password, fingerprint, or federated SSO) and approves the scopes.

**Step 4:** The Authorization Server redirects back to the app with a short-lived **authorization code** (valid for ~10 minutes).

**Step 5:** The app's backend exchanges this code (along with its `client_secret`) for an **access token** and a **refresh token**.

**Step 6:** The app stores tokens securely in **Android Keystore** or **iOS Keychain** and attaches the access token to every subsequent API call as `Authorization: Bearer <token>`.

**Step 7:** When the access token expires (typically after 1 hour), the app silently uses the refresh token to obtain a new one.

**Two Security Benefits:**
- **No password storage:** The mobile app never sees the user's AgriCloud password, eliminating credential theft via reverse engineering.
- **Scoped, revocable access:** The user can grant only `read:sensors` and later revoke it from the AgriCloud dashboard without affecting their account password.
- *(Bonus)* Short-lived tokens limit the blast radius of any token leak.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2023]

**(a)** Compare REST, WebSocket, and MQTT communication protocols for mobile IoT. State one ideal use-case for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile app must fetch 1 KB of JSON data from a cloud server over 4G (RTT = 100 ms). Compute the total round-trip latency, assuming DNS, TCP, and TLS 1.2 handshakes are required. Neglect server processing and rendering time. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Comparison Table (refer to Analytical Comparison Matrix in Section 3.3):**

| Feature | REST/HTTP | WebSocket | MQTT |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pattern | Request‚ÄìResponse | Bidirectional | Pub/Sub |
| Connection | Per-request or keep-alive | Persistent | Persistent |
| Overhead | High | Low | Very Low |
| Push Support | No | Yes | Yes (via broker) |
| Best for | CRUD | Dashboards | Constrained sensors |

**Use-cases:**
- **REST:** Sending a command to a smart plug to turn ON/OFF (one-off control).
- **WebSocket:** Live temperature graph updating every second in a weather dashboard.
- **MQTT:** A soil-moisture sensor in a remote farm publishing readings every 5 minutes over a cellular IoT (NB-IoT) link.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Payload size: $N = 1$ KB $= 8192$ bits
- 4G RTT: $RTT = 100$ ms
- Assume transmission time is negligible compared to propagation.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Connection Establishment Cost:**

$$
T_{DNS} = 1 \times RTT = 100 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{TCP} = 1 \times RTT = 100 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{TLS\,1.2} = 2 \times RTT = 200 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî HTTP Request/Response Transmission:**

$$
T_{HTTP} = 1 \times RTT = 100 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Total Latency:**

$$
T_{total} = T_{DNS} + T_{TCP} + T_{TLS} + T_{HTTP}
$$

$$
T_{total} = 100 + 100 + 200 + 100
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{total} = 500 \text{ ms}}
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Verification using the closed-form formula:**

$$
T_{total} = (1 + 1 + 2 + 1) \times RTT = 5 \times 100 = 500 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Engineering Insight:**

If the app reused a persistent connection (HTTP keep-alive or HTTP/2), the $T_{DNS}$, $T_{TCP}$, and $T_{TLS}$ costs would be paid only once per session. For subsequent calls, $T_{total}$ would drop to just **$T_{HTTP} = 100$ ms** ‚Äî a **5√ó speedup**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting TLS overhead:** Many students compute only `DNS + TCP + HTTP`, omitting the TLS handshake. In a 2023 KTU paper, this cost **2 marks** per sub-question.
> 2. **Confusing `PUT` with `PATCH`:** `PUT` replaces the entire resource; `PATCH` applies a partial modification. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 3. **Writing REST in lowercase ("rest") or with quotes ("RESTful"):** Always capitalize consistently as **REST** or **RESTful**.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark sub-questions:** A neat, labeled block diagram is worth **2 marks** in itself.
> 5. **Ignoring unit conversions in numerical problems:** Forgetting that $1$ KB $= 8192$ bits (not $1000$ or $8000$) is the most common arithmetic error.
> 6. **Not stating the response status code:** When discussing APIs, always mention the HTTP status (e.g., `HTTP 200 OK`).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **REST** is the dominant mobile IoT API style ‚Äî **stateless, cacheable, uniform interface**.
- **HTTP methods** to memorize: `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `PATCH`, `DELETE` ‚Äî know which is **idempotent**.
- **JSON** is preferred over XML for mobile IoT due to smaller size and faster parsing.
- **Status code classes:** 1xx ‚Üí Informational, 2xx ‚Üí Success, 3xx ‚Üí Redirection, 4xx ‚Üí Client Error, 5xx ‚Üí Server Error.
- **OAuth 2.0** is the industry-standard delegated authorization; **JWT** is the modern token format.
- **WebSocket** enables full-duplex real-time push; ideal for dashboards.
- **MQTT** is the lightweight pub/sub protocol designed for constrained IoT devices.
- **Latency formula:** $T_{total} = (T_{DNS} + T_{TCP} + T_{TLS}) + T_{HTTP} + T_{server} + T_{render}$.
- **HTTP/2** reduces header overhead via HPACK compression and supports multiplexing.
- **Always secure tokens** in Android Keystore / iOS Keychain ‚Äî never in plain `SharedPreferences`.
- **Use exponential backoff** for retries on `5xx` errors; respect the `Retry-After` header for `429`.
- **Energy formula** $E_{bit} = P_{tx}/R_{bit}$ governs IoT battery-life estimation.
- **Cache aggressively** on the client to reduce both latency and battery consumption.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using Google Maps, GPS and WiFi
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT604 - MOBILE APPLICATIONS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Using Google Maps, GPS and WiFi

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Location-Based Sensing in IoT?

**Location-Based Sensing** is the foundation of context-aware IoT applications. It refers to the ability of a mobile device to determine, interpret, and act upon its physical position on Earth by fusing data from three primary sources: **Global Positioning System (GPS)** satellites, **Wi-Fi** access points, and **cellular networks**. In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus for Mobile Applications for IoT, this is implemented primarily through the **Google Play Services Location API** (also called the **Fused Location Provider**), which abstracts the hardware complexity and returns a unified, high-accuracy location object to the Android application.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBCIT604, Module 3):** Students must understand that *no single positioning technology is best for all scenarios*. GPS is accurate outdoors but power-hungry and fails indoors. Wi-Fi and cellular triangulation work indoors but with lower accuracy. The Fused Location Provider intelligently combines them ‚Äî this is a guaranteed exam point.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Positioning

| Pillar | Underlying Physics / Tech | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **GPS** | Time-of-flight of signals from ‚â•4 satellites | Outdoor, vehicular IoT |
| **Wi-Fi Positioning** | RSSI fingerprinting + BSSID lookup | Indoor, malls, airports |
| **Cell Tower Triangulation** | Signal strength from 3+ cell towers | Urban canyon, low battery |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ‚Äî "The Delivery Boy with Three Compasses":** Imagine a delivery boy in a dense Kerala town trying to find a customer. He has **three compasses**:
> 1. A **Satellite Compass** (GPS) ‚Äî extremely accurate when he is in the open field, but useless inside concrete buildings.
> 2. A **Wi-Fi Compass** ‚Äî every router he has ever connected to has a "fingerprint" stored in a giant database (Google's location servers). He can ask: *‚ÄúWhat is my location relative to the nearby routers' MAC addresses?‚Äù*
> 3. A **Cell Tower Compass** ‚Äî the roughest but most universally available; if he can hear three cell towers, he can triangulate.
> The Fused Location Provider is the **smart brain** that picks the right compass (or blends all three) based on accuracy, power, and speed requirements.

## 1.4 Key Constants and Metrics

- **Speed of Light (c):** $c = 2.998 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ ‚Äî basis of GPS time-of-flight calculations.
- **GPS Accuracy:** $\pm 3$ to $\pm 10$ meters outdoors (under open sky).
- **Wi-Fi Accuracy:** $\pm 5$ to $\pm 50$ meters indoors.
- **Cell Tower Accuracy:** $\pm 100$ to $\pm 1000$ meters.
- **Cold Start TTFF (Time To First Fix):** 30‚Äì60 s; **Warm Start:** 5‚Äì10 s; **Hot Start:** < 1 s.
- **Earth's Radius (R):** $R \approx 6371$ km (used in Haversine formula).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 How GPS Works ‚Äî Operational Logic

GPS is a **passive, one-way ranging system** with three segments:

1. **Space Segment:** A constellation of at least **24 satellites** (currently 31 operational) orbiting at an altitude of $\approx 20{,}200$ km with a period of **11 hours 58 minutes**. Each satellite continuously broadcasts a *navigation message* containing its position and the precise transmission time.
2. **Control Segment:** Ground stations that monitor satellite orbits (ephemeris) and clock drift, uploading corrections.
3. **User Segment:** The mobile device receiver that decodes the signals.

**Operational Steps:**
- Each satellite transmits a **pseudorandom code** modulated on L1 (1575.42 MHz) and L2 (1227.60 MHz) carriers.
- The receiver compares the time the signal was sent ($\tau_s$) with the time it was received ($\tau_r$). The difference is the **pseudorange**:
$$\rho = c \cdot (\tau_r - \tau_s)$$
- Because the receiver's clock is not synchronized with atomic clocks on the satellites, the pseudorange has a clock-bias error $b$. The corrected range equation is:
$$\rho_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_u)^2 + (y_i - y_u)^2 + (z_i - z_u)^2} + c \cdot b$$
where $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ is the i-th satellite's position and $(x_u, y_u, z_u)$ is the user's unknown position.
- **At least 4 satellites** are required to solve for the four unknowns $(x_u, y_u, z_u, b)$ in 3D space.

## 2.2 How Wi-Fi Positioning Works

Wi-Fi positioning leverages two complementary techniques:

1. **RSSI-based Trilateration:** The Received Signal Strength Indicator is converted to distance using the **log-distance path loss model**:
$$P_r(d) = P_r(d_0) - 10 \cdot n \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma$$
where $P_r(d_0)$ is the power at reference distance $d_0$ (usually 1 m), $n$ is the path loss exponent (typically 2‚Äì4 indoors), and $X_\sigma$ is shadow fading noise.

2. **BSSID Fingerprinting:** Every Wi-Fi Access Point has a unique **MAC address (BSSID)**. Google, Apple, and Mozilla maintain massive databases mapping BSSIDs to lat/long coordinates. The device's location is determined by querying this database with the visible BSSID list.

## 2.3 Why the Fused Location Provider?

The Android **FusedLocationProviderClient** is the modern, recommended way to receive location updates. It intelligently combines GPS, Wi-Fi, and cell tower data using a *sensor-fusion* approach similar to a Kalman filter. It is **battery-optimized** and abstracts the OS-level complexity.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Pseudorange | $\rho = c \cdot \Delta t$ | meters; $c$ in m/s |
| Trilateration unknowns | 4 satellites needed | 3 for $(x,y,z)$ + 1 for clock bias $b$ |
| Haversine distance | $d = 2R \cdot \arcsin\left(\sqrt{\sin^2(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}) + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \sin^2(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2})}\right)$ | R = 6371 km; $\phi,\lambda$ in radians |
| Bearing (initial) | $\theta = \text{atan2}(\sin\Delta\lambda \cos\phi_2, \cos\phi_1 \sin\phi_2 - \sin\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \cos\Delta\lambda)$ | radians, convert to degrees |
| Log-distance path loss | $P_r(d) = P_r(d_0) - 10n\log_{10}(d/d_0)$ | dBm, $n$ is path loss exponent |
| Power accuracy tradeoff | $\text{Power} \propto 1/\text{Accuracy}^2$ | High accuracy = high battery drain |
| Cold TTFF | $30$ ‚Äì $60$ seconds | Almanac download required |
| Altitude from GPS | Provided in `Location.getAltitude()` | meters above WGS-84 ellipsoid |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The Haversine formula is used in nearly every IoT asset-tracking, fleet management, and ride-hailing application (Uber, Ola, Swiggy). Mastering it is mandatory for KTU's IoT lab viva and for any placement interview in product-based companies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Haversine Formula

The Haversine formula computes the great-circle distance between two points on a sphere. It is preferred over the law of cosines for small distances because it avoids floating-point precision loss.

**Given:** Two points $P_1 = (\phi_1, \lambda_1)$ and $P_2 = (\phi_2, \lambda_2)$ on a sphere of radius $R$, where $\phi$ is latitude and $\lambda$ is longitude (in radians).

**Step 1: Define the central angle $\Delta\sigma$ between the two points using spherical law of cosines:**

$$\cos(\Delta\sigma) = \sin\phi_1 \sin\phi_2 + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \cos(\Delta\lambda)$$

where $\Delta\lambda = \lambda_2 - \lambda_1$.

**Step 2: Rearrange to isolate $\Delta\sigma$:**

$$\Delta\sigma = \arccos(\sin\phi_1 \sin\phi_2 + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \cos(\Delta\lambda))$$

**Step 3: For small angles, the arccos becomes numerically unstable. Apply the half-angle identity:**

$$\cos(\Delta\sigma) = 1 - 2\sin^2\left(\frac{\Delta\sigma}{2}\right)$$

Solving for $\sin^2(\Delta\sigma/2)$:

$$\sin^2\left(\frac{\Delta\sigma}{2}\right) = \frac{1 - \cos(\Delta\sigma)}{2}$$

**Step 4: Substitute the law-of-cosines expression for $\cos(\Delta\sigma)$:**

$$\sin^2\left(\frac{\Delta\sigma}{2}\right) = \frac{1 - (\sin\phi_1 \sin\phi_2 + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \cos(\Delta\lambda))}{2}$$

**Step 5: Split the numerator into two Haversine terms (the "Haversine" trick):**

$$\sin^2\left(\frac{\Delta\sigma}{2}\right) = \sin^2\left(\frac{\phi_2 - \phi_1}{2}\right) + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \sin^2\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)$$

This is the famous **Haversine of central angle** formula.

**Step 6: Take the square root and multiply by $2R$ to get the arc length (great-circle distance):**

$$d = 2R \cdot \arcsin\left(\sqrt{\sin^2\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \sin^2\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)}\right)$$

**Final Compact Form:**

$$d = 2R \cdot \arcsin\left(\sqrt{\sin^2\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \cdot \sin^2\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity Check:** For $\Delta\phi = 0$ and $\Delta\lambda = 0$, the inside becomes $\sqrt{0} = 0$, so $d = 0$. ‚úì
> For points on the equator 90¬∞ apart: $d = 2 \cdot 6371 \cdot \arcsin(1) = 2 \cdot 6371 \cdot \pi/2 \approx 10007$ km, which is one quarter of Earth's circumference. ‚úì

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A delivery drone reports its location as $(\phi_1, \lambda_1) = (10.0261¬∞\text{N}, 76.3125¬∞\text{E})$ (this is **Cochin, Kerala**). A customer is at $(\phi_2, \lambda_2) = (10.0275¬∞\text{N}, 76.3150¬∞\text{E})$. Compute the distance using the Haversine formula.

**Step 1: Convert to radians.**

$$\phi_1 = 10.0261 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.17500 \text{ rad}$$

$$\phi_2 = 10.0275 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.17503 \text{ rad}$$

$$\lambda_1 = 76.3125 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.33194 \text{ rad}$$

$$\lambda_2 = 76.3150 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.33199 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2: Compute the deltas.**

$$\Delta\phi = 0.17503 - 0.17500 = 0.00003 \text{ rad}$$

$$\Delta\lambda = 1.33199 - 1.33194 = 0.00005 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3: Compute the half-angle sines.**

$$\sin^2\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) = \sin^2(0.000015) \approx (1.5 \times 10^{-5})^2 = 2.25 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$\sin^2\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right) = \sin^2(0.000025) \approx (2.5 \times 10^{-5})^2 = 6.25 \times 10^{-10}$$

**Step 4: Compute the cosine product.**

$$\cos\phi_1 \cdot \cos\phi_2 \approx \cos(0.17500) \cdot \cos(0.17503) \approx 0.98475 \times 0.98474 \approx 0.96950$$

**Step 5: Sum the terms inside the square root.**

$$a = 2.25 \times 10^{-10} + 0.96950 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$a = 2.25 \times 10^{-10} + 6.059 \times 10^{-10} = 8.309 \times 10^{-10}$$

**Step 6: Take the square root and arcsin.**

$$\sqrt{a} = \sqrt{8.309 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 2.883 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\arcsin(2.883 \times 10^{-5}) \approx 2.883 \times 10^{-5} \text{ rad} \quad (\text{since } \arcsin(x) \approx x \text{ for small } x)$$

**Step 7: Multiply by $2R$.**

$$d = 2 \times 6371000 \text{ m} \times 2.883 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$d = 12{,}742{,}000 \times 2.883 \times 10^{-5} \approx 367.3 \text{ meters}$$

**Result:** The drone is approximately **367 meters** from the customer ‚Äî a typical urban IoT delivery scenario.

## 3.3 Derivation of the GPS Pseudorange Equation

**Given:** Satellite $i$ at known position $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ in ECEF (Earth-Centered Earth-Fixed) coordinates.

**Step 1:** The actual (geometric) range is the Euclidean distance:
$$r_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_u)^2 + (y_i - y_u)^2 + (z_i - z_u)^2}$$

**Step 2:** The receiver clock has a bias $b$ (in seconds) relative to GPS time. The measured pseudorange is:
$$\rho_i = r_i + c \cdot b$$

**Step 3:** Squaring and expanding for 4 satellites gives a system of 4 nonlinear equations:
$$\rho_i^2 = (x_i - x_u)^2 + (y_i - y_u)^2 + (z_i - z_u)^2 + c^2 b^2 + 2r_i c b$$

**Step 4:** Subtracting the equation for satellite 1 from satellites 2, 3, 4 linearizes the system. Solving with linear algebra (e.g., least squares) yields $(x_u, y_u, z_u, b)$.

This is why **a minimum of 4 satellites** is required ‚Äî one for each unknown.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture of Location Stack in Android IoT

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    AppLayer["IoT Application Layer"] --> PermissionGate["Runtime Permission Gate ACCESS FINE LOCATION"]
    PermissionGate --> FusedClient["FusedLocationProviderClient"]
    FusedClient --> SensorFusion["Sensor Fusion Engine Kalman Filter"]
    SensorFusion --> GPSMod["GPS Receiver Module"]
    SensorFusion --> WiFiMod["WiFi Scan Module BSSID RSSI"]
    SensorFusion --> CellMod["Cell Tower Module"]
    SensorFusion --> SensorMod["Accelerometer Gyroscope"]
    GPSMod --> SatNet["GPS Satellite Constellation 24 plus satellites"]
    WiFiMod --> WiFiDB["Google WiFi BSSID Database"]
    CellMod --> CellTower["Nearby Cell Towers"]
    SensorFusion --> LocationObj["Unified Location Object lat long accuracy altitude"]
    LocationObj --> AppLayer
```

## 4.2 Location Request Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start([App Starts]) --> CheckPerm{"Permission Granted?"}
    CheckPerm -- No --> ReqPerm["Request ACCESS FINE LOCATION"]
    ReqPerm --> UserPrompt{User Decision}
    UserPrompt -- Denied --> Fallback["Use Last Known Location Coarse"]
    UserPrompt -- Granted --> CheckSetting{"Location Services ON?"}
    Fallback --> CheckSetting
    CheckPerm -- Yes --> CheckSetting
    CheckSetting -- No --> PromptUser["Prompt user to enable GPS"]
    PromptUser --> CheckSetting
    CheckSetting -- Yes --> BuildReq["Build LocationRequest Priority HIGH ACCURACY"]
    BuildReq --> SendReq["requestLocationUpdates"]
    SendReq --> Callback["onLocationChanged callback"]
    Callback --> UpdateUI["Update Map / IoT Actuator"]
    UpdateUI --> Callback
```

## 4.3 Trilateration Geometry Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Geometry["Trilateration Principle"]
        SatA["Satellite A range ra"]
        SatB["Satellite B range rb"]
        SatC["Satellite C range rc"]
        SatD["Satellite D range rd clock bias"]
        UserPos["User Position xu yu zu"]
    end
    SatA -.spherical surface.-> UserPos
    SatB -.spherical surface.-> UserPos
    SatC -.spherical surface.-> UserPos
    SatD -.spherical surface.-> UserPos
```

## 4.4 Google Maps SDK Integration Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DevConsole["Google Cloud Console Enable Maps SDK for Android"]
    DevConsole --> APIKey["Generate API Key SHA1 restricted"]
    APIKey --> Manifest["Add to AndroidManifest meta-data"]
    Manifest --> Layout["MapView or SupportMapFragment in XML"]
    Layout --> GetMap["getMapAsync onMapReadyCallback"]
    GetMap --> AddMarker["Add Marker Camera Update Polyline"]
    AddMarker --> UserInteract["User pan zoom click"]
    UserInteract --> Callback2["onMarkerClick onMapClick"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2024]**
**Differentiate between GPS-based positioning and Wi-Fi-based positioning in mobile IoT applications. Mention any two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**GPS-based Positioning:**
- Uses time-of-flight signals from at least 4 satellites.
- Accuracy: $\pm 3$ to $\pm 10$ m outdoors.
- Works only with line-of-sight to satellites (fails indoors).
- High power consumption ($\sim 50$ mA continuously).
- *Advantages:* Globally available, no internet required once ephemeris is loaded, very accurate outdoors.

**Wi-Fi-based Positioning:**
- Uses RSSI trilateration or BSSID fingerprinting against a cloud database.
- Accuracy: $\pm 5$ to $\pm 50$ m indoors.
- Works well indoors where GPS fails.
- Lower power consumption than GPS.
- *Advantages:* Works in indoor/urban-canyon environments, faster time-to-first-fix (~1 s), lower battery drain.

*(1 mark for GPS explanation, 1 mark for Wi-Fi explanation, 1 mark for advantages.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]**
**What is the Fused Location Provider in Android? Why is it preferred over the legacy `LocationManager` API?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Fused Location Provider** is a Google Play Services API (introduced in 2013) that intelligently combines inputs from GPS, Wi-Fi, cell towers, and device sensors (accelerometer, gyroscope) to produce a single, optimized location fix. *(1 mark)*

**Why preferred over `LocationManager`?**
1. **Automatic provider switching** ‚Äî it picks the best sensor combination for the requested accuracy/power profile. *(1 mark)*
2. **Built-in battery optimization** ‚Äî uses sensor batching and geofencing to reduce wakeups. *(0.5 marks)*
3. **Modern API design** ‚Äî callback-based with `LocationCallback`, easy integration with LiveData/Flow. *(0.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by (1) forgetting that GPS requires **4 satellites** minimum, not 3; (2) writing `android.permission.ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION` without also mentioning the **runtime permission request** for API 23+; (3) using `LocationManager` instead of `FusedLocationProviderClient` in code-based questions ‚Äî a guaranteed 2-mark penalty. Also, the Haversine formula must use **radians**, not degrees, in `Math.sin/cos` calls.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU Model Paper ‚Äì 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of the Android location stack. Discuss the role of the Fused Location Provider and describe how GPS, Wi-Fi, and cell-tower data are fused. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Two IoT sensor nodes are deployed in a smart farm. Node A is at $(10.0500¬∞\text{N}, 76.3500¬∞\text{E})$ and Node B is at $(10.0525¬∞\text{N}, 76.3525¬∞\text{E})$. Using the Haversine formula, compute the great-circle distance between them. Take Earth's radius $R = 6371$ km. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a) ‚Äî 7 marks:**

The Android location stack consists of **three layers**:

1. **Hardware Layer (Sensors):** *(1 mark)*
   - GPS receiver chip (u-blox, Qualcomm, etc.)
   - Wi-Fi radio (for BSSID scanning and RSSI)
   - Cellular modem (for cell tower IDs and signal strength)
   - MEMS sensors (accelerometer, gyroscope, magnetometer)

2. **OS / HAL Layer:** *(1 mark)*
   - Android's `android.location.LocationManager` (low-level)
   - GNSS HAL (Hardware Abstraction Layer) standardized since Android 7

3. **Fused Location Provider (FLP):** *(2 marks)*
   - Located in **Google Play Services**, NOT the AOSP.
   - Uses a **Kalman filter** to fuse sensor inputs.
   - For example, when the device is stationary (detected via accelerometer), the FLP can down-weight GPS noise and trust the cell-tower/Wi-Fi fix. When the user starts moving, GPS is re-enabled.

**Fusion Process:**
- **GPS** contributes high accuracy when satellite SNR is good.
- **Wi-Fi** contributes via BSSID lookups (latency ~200 ms to Google's API).
- **Cell** provides a coarse baseline fix even when GPS is off.
- The Kalman filter combines these as weighted measurements, producing a `Location` object with `getAccuracy()` in meters.

**Why FLP is superior:** *(1 mark)*
- Hides complexity from the developer.
- Handles sensor calibration, time-sync, and battery management.
- Provides consistent API across vendors (Samsung, Xiaomi, Pixel).

**Code sketch (conceptual):** *(2 marks)*

```java
FusedLocationProviderClient client = LocationServices.getFusedLocationProviderClient(this);
LocationRequest req = new LocationRequest.Builder(Priority.PRIORITY_HIGH_ACCURACY, 5000)
        .setMinUpdateIntervalMillis(2000)
        .build();
client.requestLocationUpdates(req, callback, Looper.getMainLooper());
```

---

**Model Solution for (b) ‚Äî 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Convert to radians.** *(1 mark)*

$$\phi_1 = 10.0500 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.17542 \text{ rad}$$

$$\phi_2 = 10.0525 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.17546 \text{ rad}$$

$$\lambda_1 = 76.3500 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.33260 \text{ rad}$$

$$\lambda_2 = 76.3525 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.33264 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2: Deltas.** *(1 mark)*

$$\Delta\phi = 0.17546 - 0.17542 = 0.00004 \text{ rad}$$

$$\Delta\lambda = 1.33264 - 1.33260 = 0.00004 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3: Half-angle sines.** *(1 mark)*

$$\sin^2(\Delta\phi/2) = \sin^2(0.00002) \approx 4.0 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$\sin^2(\Delta\lambda/2) = \sin^2(0.00002) \approx 4.0 \times 10^{-10}$$

**Step 4: Cosine product.** *(1 mark)*

$$\cos\phi_1 \cdot \cos\phi_2 \approx \cos^2(0.17544) \approx 0.9694$$

**Step 5: Combine inside square root.** *(1 mark)*

$$a = 4.0 \times 10^{-10} + 0.9694 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-10} = 4.0 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.9694 \approx 7.878 \times 10^{-10}$$

**Step 6: Square root and arcsin.** *(1 mark)*

$$\sqrt{a} \approx 2.807 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\arcsin(2.807 \times 10^{-5}) \approx 2.807 \times 10^{-5} \text{ rad}$$

**Step 7: Multiply by $2R$.** *(1 mark)*

$$d = 2 \times 6371000 \times 2.807 \times 10^{-5} \approx 357.7 \text{ meters}$$

**Final Answer:** The two IoT sensor nodes are approximately **357.7 meters** apart ‚Äî typical for a smart-farm sensor grid spacing.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU Model Paper ‚Äì 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of GPS. Derive the pseudorange equation and justify why a minimum of 4 satellites is required for a 3D fix. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss Wi-Fi positioning techniques in detail. Explain the log-distance path loss model and derive the relationship between RSSI and distance. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a) ‚Äî 7 marks:**

**Block Diagram (textual ‚Äî full marks for ASCII or Mermaid):** *(2 marks)*

```
[Satellites 24+] --(radio signal @ 1.575 GHz)--> [GPS Antenna] 
        --> [RF Front End] --> [Baseband Processor] 
        --> [Navigation Decoder] --> [PVT Engine] 
        --> [Location Fix lat, long, alt]
```

**Derivation of Pseudorange Equation:** *(3 marks)*

Let $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ be the i-th satellite's ECEF coordinates (known from the navigation message). The geometric range is:

$$r_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_u)^2 + (y_i - y_u)^2 + (z_i - z_u)^2}$$

The receiver clock has an unknown bias $b$ seconds. The signal's measured travel time is therefore $(\tau_r - \tau_s) = r_i/c + b$. Multiplying by $c$:

$$\rho_i = r_i + c \cdot b$$

$$\boxed{\rho_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_u)^2 + (y_i - y_u)^2 + (z_i - z_u)^2} + c \cdot b}$$

**Why 4 satellites?** *(2 marks)*

The system has **4 unknowns**: $x_u, y_u, z_u$ (3D position) and $b$ (clock bias). Each satellite provides **one equation** (one sphere). The intersection of:
- 1 sphere: 3D surface
- 2 spheres: circle
- 3 spheres: 2 points (ambiguity)
- **4 spheres: 1 unique point** ‚úì

Thus, a minimum of 4 satellites is mathematically required for a 3D fix. With only 3, we get two possible positions, and we cannot disambiguate without an altitude assumption (which is why old GPS receivers used 3 satellites + assumed sea level).

---

**Model Solution for (b) ‚Äî 7 marks:**

**Wi-Fi Positioning Techniques:** *(2 marks)*

1. **RSSI Trilateration:** Convert Received Signal Strength Indicator to distance using a path-loss model, then apply trilateration.
2. **BSSID Fingerprinting:** Match the visible list of Wi-Fi MAC addresses (BSSIDs) against Google's crowdsourced database to get a probabilistic location.
3. **Round-Trip Time (RTT) (Wi-Fi RTT / IEEE 802.11mc):** Measures the time for a signal to travel to/from an AP, giving sub-meter accuracy on supported devices.

**Log-Distance Path Loss Model:** *(3 marks)*

The free-space Friis transmission equation gives received power at distance $d$:

$$P_r(d) = P_t + G_t + G_r + 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)$$

In real environments, multipath and obstructions modify this. The general **log-distance model** is:

$$\boxed{P_r(d) = P_r(d_0) - 10 \cdot n \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma}$$

where:
- $P_r(d_0)$ is the received power at reference distance $d_0$ (typically 1 m).
- $n$ is the path loss exponent ($n=2$ free space, $n=3$‚Äì$4$ indoor, $n=4$‚Äì$6$ obstructed).
- $X_\sigma$ is a zero-mean Gaussian random variable (shadow fading, $\sigma$ in dB).

**Deriving distance from RSSI:** *(2 marks)*

Setting $X_\sigma = 0$ for the mean and rearranging:

$$10 \cdot n \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) = P_r(d_0) - P_r(d)$$

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) = \frac{P_r(d_0) - P_r(d)}{10n}$$

$$\boxed{d = d_0 \cdot 10^{\frac{P_r(d_0) - P_r(d)}{10n}}}$$

This is the fundamental equation used by every Wi-Fi trilateration library (e.g., indoor positioning in malls, hospitals, and airports).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Points (Must-Memorize for KTU):**

- **GPS needs 4 satellites** for 3D fix; 3 satellites + sea-level assumption = 2D fix.
- **Fused Location Provider** is part of Google Play Services, NOT AOSP.
- **Haversine formula uses radians**, not degrees.
- **Earth's Radius $R = 6371$ km** is the standard value to use in exams.
- **Path loss exponent $n$**: 2 (free space), 3‚Äì4 (indoor), 4‚Äì6 (obstructed).
- **Android Permissions for location:**
  - `ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION` (precise, uses GPS)
  - `ACCESS_COARSE_LOCATION` (approximate, uses Wi-Fi/cell)
  - From API 31+: `ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION` is gated by user choice between *Approximate* and *Precise*.
- **Google Maps API Key** must be restricted by SHA-1 fingerprint and package name in the Google Cloud Console.
- **Cold start TTFF = 30‚Äì60 s**, Warm = 5‚Äì10 s, Hot < 1 s.
- **Power Priority vs. Accuracy Priority** are trade-offs in `LocationRequest`.
- **WGS-84 datum** is the global standard for GPS coordinates.
- **RSSI to distance formula:** $d = d_0 \cdot 10^{(P_{d_0} - P_d)/(10n)}$ ‚Äî learn it cold.
- **Kalman filter** is the heart of sensor fusion in the FLP.
- **Cell tower accuracy (~100 m) < Wi-Fi accuracy (~10 m) < GPS accuracy (~5 m)** in open sky.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integration with Social Media Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT604 - MOBILE APPLICATIONS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Integration with Social Media Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Social Media Integration** in the context of Mobile Applications for IoT refers to the engineered process of embedding external social networking services (such as Facebook, Twitter/X, LinkedIn, Instagram, and Google) into native or hybrid mobile applications through standardized **Application Programming Interfaces (APIs)** and **Software Development Kits (SDKs)**. This integration enables three principal functional capabilities:

1. **Authentication** ‚Äî allowing users to log in to the application using their existing social media credentials (often termed *Social Login* or *Federated Identity*).
2. **Content Sharing** ‚Äî enabling the user to publish application-generated content (text, images, links, achievements) directly to their social feeds without leaving the host application.
3. **Data Retrieval & Synchronization** ‚Äî fetching user profile metadata, friend graphs, likes, and engagement signals via authorized **OAuth 2.0** access tokens.

From an **IoT perspective**, social media integration extends the *reach and interoperability* of physical devices by allowing sensor data, device states, and event-driven notifications to be disseminated through human-facing social channels ‚Äî effectively bridging the **cyber-physical-social (CPS) convergence**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Integration with social media is examined under the broader theme of "Application Layer Protocols and Cloud Services" in Module 3. Examiners expect familiarity with **OAuth 2.0**, **OpenID Connect**, **RESTful APIs**, and platform-specific SDKs (Facebook Graph API, Twitter API v2, Google Sign-In).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy ‚Äî "The Hotel Concierge & the VIP Wristband"**
> Imagine your mobile app is a five-star hotel. Normally, every guest (user) must show a unique ID card and sign a register. This is tedious and repetitive.
> Now suppose the guest arrives wearing a **VIP Wristband** issued by a trusted global concierge network (e.g., Facebook, Google). The hotel simply scans the wristband, the network confirms the guest's identity remotely, and entry is granted ‚Äî no new password required.
> The wristband is the **Access Token**, the concierge network is the **Authorization Server**, and the wristband scanner at the hotel door is the **Resource Server** (your app's backend). The guest never hands over their master credentials ‚Äî only a *time-limited, scope-bounded* wristband.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

The following metrics are universally observed across production-grade social media integrations:

- **Token Validity Window:** **3600 seconds (1 hour)** for most OAuth 2.0 access tokens.
- **Refresh Token Lifetime:** typically **60 days** (Facebook) to **100 days** (Google), after which re-authentication is mandatory.
- **Rate Limit (Twitter API v2):** **50 requests / 15 minutes / user** for POST endpoints.
- **Rate Limit (Facebook Graph API):** **200 calls / hour / user** for basic-tier applications.
- **HTTPS Requirement:** **TLS 1.2+** is mandatory for all OAuth redirect URIs.
- **Character Limit (Single Tweet):** **280 characters** (post-2018 standard).

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the **1-hour / 60-day** token lifecycle ‚Äî it is a frequent 3-mark question in KTU ESE.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Layer Architecture of Social Integration

A robust social media integration in a mobile-IoT application is structured into three logical layers:

### Layer 1 ‚Äî **Identity Layer (Authentication)**
- Implements **OAuth 2.0** with extensions such as **OpenID Connect (OIDC)**.
- Returns an *id_token* (JWT) containing verifiable user identity claims.
- Eliminates the need for users to remember application-specific passwords.

### Layer 2 ‚Äî **Authorization Layer (Permission Scopes)**
- Defines *what* the third-party app may access on behalf of the user.
- Common scopes: `email`, `public_profile`, `user_friends` (Facebook); `tweet.read`, `tweet.write` (Twitter); `openid`, `profile` (Google).
- Principle of **Least Privilege** dictates requesting only the minimum scopes required.

### Layer 3 ‚Äî **Data Exchange Layer (APIs)**
- Uses **RESTful HTTP endpoints** (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) returning **JSON** payloads.
- Operates over **HTTPS (port 443)**.
- Employs **Bearer Token Authentication** in the `Authorization` header.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three layers?** Separation of concerns allows independent evolution ‚Äî identity providers can upgrade authentication flows (e.g., OAuth 2.0 ‚Üí 2.1) without breaking the data layer.

## 2.2 The OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Flow (Step Logic)

The most secure and industry-standard flow for mobile-IoT apps is the **Authorization Code Flow with PKCE** (Proof Key for Code Exchange, RFC 7636):

1. **App Initiation** ‚Äî App generates a cryptographic *code_verifier* and its SHA-256 hash, the *code_challenge*.
2. **Authorization Request** ‚Äî App opens a system browser (via *Custom Tabs* on Android, *ASWebAuthenticationSession* on iOS) to the provider's `/authorize` endpoint.
3. **User Consent** ‚Äî User authenticates with the IdP and approves requested scopes.
4. **Authorization Code** ‚Äî IdP redirects back to the app with a short-lived `code` (valid ~10 minutes).
5. **Token Exchange** ‚Äî App's backend POSTs the `code` + `code_verifier` to `/token` endpoint.
6. **Access Token Issuance** ‚Äî IdP verifies the PKCE pair and returns `access_token`, `refresh_token`, and `id_token`.
7. **API Calls** ‚Äî App uses the `access_token` in `Authorization: Bearer <token>` headers.
8. **Token Refresh** ‚Äî When the access token expires, the app silently exchanges the `refresh_token` for a new pair.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | Standard Value | Unit / Scope |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_{acc}$ | Access token lifetime | $\mathbf{3600}$ | seconds |
| $T_{ref}$ | Refresh token lifetime | $\mathbf{60 \times 86400}$ | seconds (60 days) |
| $T_{code}$ | Authorization code lifetime | $\mathbf{600}$ | seconds |
| $H_{TLS}$ | Minimum TLS version | $\mathbf{1.2}$ | RFC 5246 |
| $L_{tweet}$ | Max single tweet length | $\mathbf{280}$ | characters |
| $R_{FB}$ | Facebook Graph rate limit | $\mathbf{200}$ | calls/hour/user |
| $R_{TW}$ | Twitter POST rate limit | $\mathbf{50}$ | calls/15-min/user |
| $C_{verifier}$ | PKCE code verifier length | $\mathbf{43 \le n \le 128}$ | characters |
| $H_{code\_chal}$ | $\text{SHA-256}(\text{code\_verifier})$ | ‚Äî | hex/base64url |
| $S_{req}$ | Standard request scope set | $\{\text{email, profile}\}$ | strings |
| $P_{LEAST}$ | Privilege principle | $\min(S)$ | ‚Äî |
| $N_{JWT}$ | JWT parts (header.payload.signature) | $\mathbf{3}$ | parts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These constants appear in KTU numerical/descriptive questions on token expiry calculations, rate-limit planning, and security compliance audits for IoT deployments.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in IoT Domains

| IoT Vertical | Social Integration Utility |
|---|---|
| **Smart Home** | User shares device scenes (e.g., "Movie Mode activated") to Instagram stories. |
| **Wearables** | Fitness achievement auto-posted to Facebook via Graph API. |
| **Industrial IoT** | Anomaly alerts broadcast to a private Slack/Twitter channel for operator response. |
| **Healthcare** | OAuth-secured patient dashboard sharing authorized metrics with caregivers. |
| **Smart Cities** | Public dashboards auto-tweet pollution/utility data via Twitter Bot accounts. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: OAuth 2.0 PKCE Token Lifecycle

The end-to-end temporal analysis of an OAuth 2.0 session for a mobile-IoT application can be derived as follows.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Total user-visible session time before forced re-login:**

$$
T_{session} = T_{ref}
$$

If $T_{ref} = 60 \text{ days} = 60 \times 86400 \text{ seconds}$, then:

$$
T_{session} = 5{,}184{,}000 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Number of access token renewals possible within one session:**

The refresh token may be exchanged for a new access token exactly once per access-token expiry. Therefore:

$$
N_{renew} = \left\lfloor \frac{T_{ref}}{T_{acc}} \right\rfloor
$$

Substituting standard values:

$$
N_{renew} = \left\lfloor \frac{5{,}184{,}000}{3600} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 1440 \right\rfloor = 1440
$$

So a mobile user can remain silently signed in for **1440 access-token rotations** before any re-authentication prompt is shown.

**Step 3 ‚Äî Authorization code window (race-condition safety):**

$$
T_{code} = 600 \text{ seconds} = 10 \text{ minutes}
$$

If a network latency $L$ is present, the usable exchange window is:

$$
T_{exch} = T_{code} - L
$$

For a typical mobile network with $L = 2 \text{ s}$:

$$
T_{exch} = 600 - 2 = 598 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî PKCE cryptographic binding:**

The code challenge is computed as:

$$
H_{code\_chal} = \text{Base64URL}\bigl(\text{SHA-256}(\text{code\_verifier})\bigr)
$$

Verification at the IdP:

$$
H_{code\_chal} \stackrel{?}{=} \text{Base64URL}\bigl(\text{SHA-256}(\text{received\_verifier})\bigr)
$$

If equality holds, the token endpoint issues the access token.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The floor function in Step 2 accounts for the partial-window scenario where the final refresh may exceed $T_{ref}$ and is therefore discarded by the IdP.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Major Social Identity Providers

| Feature | Facebook | Google | Twitter / X | LinkedIn |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Protocol** | OAuth 2.0 + OIDC | OAuth 2.0 + OIDC | OAuth 1.0a / 2.0 | OAuth 2.0 |
| **Default Scopes** | `email, public_profile` | `openid, email, profile` | `tweet.read, users.read` | `r_liteprofile, r_emailaddress` |
| **Access Token TTL** | 1‚Äì2 hours | 1 hour | 2 hours | 60 days |
| **Refresh Token** | Yes (long-lived) | Yes | No (uses PIN) | Yes |
| **Mobile SDK** | Facebook SDK / Login Kit | Google Sign-In | Twitter Kit | Sign-In with LinkedIn |
| **Primary API Style** | Graph API (v18+) | People API + REST | Twitter API v2 | REST + Voyager |
| **Rate Limit** | 200/hr | 1000/100s | 50/15-min | 500/day |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Sharing Intent Flow (Android)

1. **Trigger** ‚Äî User taps "Share" button in the IoT app (e.g., smart thermostat data).
2. **Intent Construction** ‚Äî App builds an `ACTION_SEND` intent with `type="text/plain"` and `EXTRA_TEXT` payload.
3. **Resolver Query** ‚Äî Android's PackageManager queries all installed apps declaring an intent filter for `ACTION_SEND`.
4. **Disambiguation Dialog** ‚Äî If multiple apps match (WhatsApp, Twitter, Gmail), Android shows a chooser dialog.
5. **App Launch** ‚Äî Selected social app's `Activity` receives the intent extras.
6. **Post Composition** ‚Äî Social app renders the text in its own composer UI.
7. **Network Publish** ‚Äî User taps "Tweet/Post"; the social SDK makes a POST request to its backend.
8. **Callback Return** ‚Äî Control returns to the host IoT app; `onActivityResult` is invoked with status code.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU, remember the intent action name: **`ACTION_SEND`** for single-share, **`ACTION_SEND_MULTIPLE`** for multi-content share.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Flow with PKCE

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant userApp as Mobile IoT App
    participant browser as System Browser
    participant idp as Social IdP
    participant backend as App Backend
    participant api as Resource API

    userApp->>userApp: Generate code_verifier and code_challenge
    userApp->>browser: Open /authorize with code_challenge
    browser->>idp: GET /authorize?client_id=...&scope=...
    idp->>userApp: User authenticates and grants consent
    idp->>browser: 302 Redirect with authorization code
    browser->>userApp: Deliver auth code via custom scheme
    userApp->>backend: Forward auth code to app backend
    backend->>idp: POST /token (code + code_verifier)
    idp->>idp: Verify SHA-256(code_verifier) = code_challenge
    idp->>backend: Return access_token, refresh_token, id_token
    backend->>api: GET /user/profile with Bearer access_token
    api->>backend: JSON user profile payload
    backend->>userApp: Authenticated session established
    userApp->>api: Subsequent API calls with access_token
    api-->>userApp: Resource data
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Social Integration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph userSide[User Device Side]
        uiLayer[UI Layer - Login Button, Share Button]
        intentLayer[Intent Dispatcher - ACTION_SEND]
        sdkLayer[Platform SDK - Facebook, Google, Twitter]
        tokenStore[Secure Token Vault - Keystore, Keychain]
    end

    subgraph networkSide[Network and Cloud Side]
        authServer[Authorization Server - OAuth 2.0 Endpoints]
        resourceServer[Resource Server - Graph API, People API]
        iotBridge[IoT Bridge - MQTT Broker, Cloud Functions]
    end

    subgraph dataLayer[Data and Analytics Side]
        userProfile[User Profile Store - Firebase, DynamoDB]
        eventLog[Event Log - BigQuery, ELK]
        shareLedger[Share Ledger - Firestore]
    end

    uiLayer --> sdkLayer
    uiLayer --> intentLayer
    sdkLayer --> tokenStore
    sdkLayer --> authServer
    sdkLayer --> resourceServer
    resourceServer --> userProfile
    iotBridge --> eventLog
    intentLayer --> shareLedger
    authServer --> tokenStore
```

## 4.3 Lifecycle of a Social-Linked IoT Account

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Unlinked
    Unlinked --> ConsentRequested: User taps Connect with Facebook
    ConsentRequested --> TokenIssued: User approves scopes
    ConsentRequested --> Unlinked: User denies or cancels
    TokenIssued --> Active: Tokens stored in secure vault
    Active --> Refreshing: Access token expired, refresh used
    Refreshing --> Active: New access token received
    Refreshing --> Unlinked: Refresh token revoked by user
    Active --> SharingContent: User triggers share intent
    SharingContent --> Active: Post published
    Active --> Unlinked: User revokes from social settings
    Unlinked --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Deep-Linking Architecture for Social App-to-App Routing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sourceApp[External Social App - Facebook, Twitter]
    deepLink[Universal Link / App Link]
    intentFilter[Manifest Intent Filter]
    activityRouter[App Router Activity]
    contentResolver[Content Resolver]
    targetScreen[Target Screen - Device Detail, Profile]

    sourceApp --> deepLink
    deepLink --> intentFilter
    intentFilter --> activityRouter
    activityRouter --> contentResolver
    contentResolver --> targetScreen
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define OAuth 2.0. List any two advantages of using OAuth 2.0 over traditional username-password authentication in mobile-IoT applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** OAuth 2.0 is an open-standard authorization framework (defined in RFC 6749) that enables a third-party application to obtain *limited* access to a user's resources on an HTTP service (the resource server) without exposing the user's long-term credentials. It uses short-lived *access tokens* delegated by an *authorization server*.
- **Advantage 1 (1 mark):** **Enhanced Security** ‚Äî the user's primary password is never shared with the third-party app; only a scope-bounded, time-limited token is exchanged, drastically reducing credential-theft surface area.
- **Advantage 2 (1 mark):** **Granular Scope Control & Revocability** ‚Äî users can selectively grant or revoke specific permissions (e.g., `email` only, not `friends_list`) from the social provider's settings page without affecting other apps.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the purpose of a *refresh token* in OAuth 2.0? Why is it not sent with every API request?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Purpose (1.5 marks):** A refresh token is a long-lived credential (typically **60 days** for Facebook, **100 days** for Google) used to obtain a new *access token* once the previous one expires, **without** prompting the user to re-authenticate. This enables seamless, uninterrupted sessions in mobile-IoT apps.
- **Why not with every API call (1.5 marks):** Sending the refresh token on every request would massively expand its exposure window to network interception, logging systems, and potential breaches. Access tokens (short-lived, 1 hour) act as a sacrificial, limited-scope credential; if compromised, the blast radius is bounded to one hour, after which the refresh token ‚Äî held securely in a Keystore/Keychain ‚Äî can rotate it.

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain the **OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Flow with PKCE** in detail. How does PKCE prevent authorization code interception attacks in mobile applications? (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a smart-home IoT mobile application integrated with Facebook and Twitter**. List the privacy considerations the developer must address. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution ‚Äî (a) ‚Äî 7 marks

**1. OAuth 2.0 Roles (1 mark):**
- *Resource Owner* ‚Äî the user.
- *Client* ‚Äî the mobile-IoT app.
- *Authorization Server* ‚Äî Facebook/Google IdP.
- *Resource Server* ‚Äî the API hosting user data.

**2. Flow Steps (4 marks):**
- **(i) Code Verifier Generation:** The app generates a high-entropy random string `code_verifier` (43‚Äì128 characters, from the unreserved character set `[A-Z, a-z, 0-9, -, ., _, ~]`).
- **(ii) Code Challenge Derivation:** $H_{code\_chal} = \text{Base64URL}(\text{SHA-256}(code\_verifier))$.
- **(iii) Authorization Request:** App opens a system browser to `https://www.facebook.com/v18.0/dialog/oauth?client_id=...&redirect_uri=...&response_type=code&code_challenge=...&code_challenge_method=S256&scope=email,public_profile`.
- **(iv) User Consent:** IdP authenticates the user and shows a consent screen.
- **(v) Authorization Code Grant:** IdP redirects to `redirect_uri?code=AUTH_CODE_HERE`.
- **(vi) Token Exchange:** App's backend POSTs to `https://graph.facebook.com/v18.0/oauth/access_token` with `code`, `code_verifier`, `client_id`, `client_secret`, `redirect_uri`.
- **(vii) Access Token Issued:** IdP verifies $\text{SHA-256}(code\_verifier) == code\_challenge$; on success, returns `access_token`, `refresh_token`, `expires_in`.
- **(viii) API Access:** Subsequent calls include `Authorization: Bearer <access_token>` header.

**3. PKCE Attack Mitigation (2 marks):**
In a classic authorization code interception attack, a malicious app installed on the same device registers the same custom URL scheme and steals the auth code. Without PKCE, the attacker redeems the code using their own `client_secret` and gains a valid access token. **With PKCE**, the stolen code is useless because the attacker does not possess the original `code_verifier` ‚Äî so the IdP's hash check fails and no token is issued. This binds the authorization code cryptographically to the requesting app instance.

#### Model Solution ‚Äî (b) ‚Äî 7 marks

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
+--------------------------------+
|  Smart Home IoT Mobile App     |
|  - Login Screen                |
|  - Device Dashboard            |
|  - Share Button                |
+---------------+----------------+
                |
       +--------v--------+
       |  OAuth Manager  |
       +--------+--------+
                |
   +------------+------------+
   |                         |
+--v---------+       +-------v--+
| Facebook   |       | Twitter  |
| SDK        |       | SDK      |
+-----+------+       +----+-----+
      |                   |
+-----+-------------------+-----+
|  HTTPS / TLS 1.2+              |
+--------------------------------+
                |
+-------v--------+       +--------v-------+
| Facebook Graph |       | Twitter API v2 |
| API            |       |                |
+-------+--------+       +--------+-------+
        |                          |
+-------+----------+    +-----------+-----+
| IoT Cloud        |    | Push Notif.    |
| (AWS IoT, Azure) |    | Service (FCM)  |
+------------------+    +----------------+
```

**Architecture Layers (2 marks):**
1. **Presentation Layer** ‚Äî mobile UI with social login buttons and share triggers.
2. **Service Layer** ‚Äî OAuth manager, token store, REST client.
3. **IoT Gateway Layer** ‚Äî MQTT/HTTPS bridge to cloud (AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub).
4. **Social API Layer** ‚Äî Facebook Graph API + Twitter API v2 endpoints.
5. **Notification Layer** ‚Äî FCM/APNs for cross-platform push delivery.

**Privacy Considerations (2 marks):**
- Request **minimum scopes** ‚Äî never ask for `user_friends` if not strictly needed.
- Store tokens in **Android Keystore / iOS Keychain**, never in plain `SharedPreferences`.
- Implement **token rotation** and **secure refresh**.
- Display a **privacy policy** link in the consent screen as per GDPR/Kerala DPDP Act.
- Allow **account unlinking** at any time from the app's settings page.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a)** Compare **OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC)**. When would you choose OIDC over plain OAuth 2.0 for social login? (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Deep Linking and App Links** in the context of social media integration. How does a Universal Link differ from a custom URL scheme? Provide an example flow. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution ‚Äî (a) ‚Äî 7 marks

| Parameter | OAuth 2.0 | OpenID Connect (OIDC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Authorization (access delegation) | Authentication (identity verification) |
| **Spec Document** | RFC 6749 | OpenID Connect Core 1.0 |
| **Token Type** | `access_token` | `access_token` + `id_token` (JWT) |
| **Identity Format** | None (profile API needed) | Standardized *UserInfo* claims (`sub`, `email`, `name`) |
| **Discovery** | None | `/.well-known/openid-configuration` |
| **Built on** | Standalone | OAuth 2.0 + ID Token layer |
| **Use Case** | API access only | "Sign in with Google/Apple/Facebook" |

**When to choose OIDC (3 marks):**
- When you need to **verify the user's identity** (i.e., know *who* they are, not just *what* they can do).
- When you need a **portable, signed JWT** that can be verified locally without an extra round-trip to the IdP.
- When your application is part of a **federated SSO ecosystem** (e.g., enterprise + consumer apps sharing one identity).
- When you need **standardized user claims** (`sub`, `email_verified`, `phone_number`) across multiple IdPs.

> Plain OAuth 2.0 is sufficient when you only need to call an API on the user's behalf (e.g., posting a tweet). For *login* itself, OIDC is the correct choice.

#### Model Solution ‚Äî (b) ‚Äî 7 marks

**Deep Linking Definition (2 marks):**
Deep linking is the practice of using a URI to launch an application on a mobile device and deliver a specific resource/screen within it, rather than the app's home screen. In social media integration, deep links enable a Facebook post to open your IoT app directly to a *specific device control panel*.

**Custom URL Scheme (1.5 marks):**
- Format: `myiotapp://device/thermostat?id=42`
- Registered in `AndroidManifest.xml` via `<intent-filter>` with `<data android:scheme="myiotapp"/>`.
- **Drawback:** Multiple apps can register the same scheme ‚Üí hijacking risk. No fallback to web.

**App Links / Universal Links (2 marks):**
- Format: `https://myiotapp.com/device/thermostat?id=42` (a real HTTPS URL).
- On Android: **App Links** (verified via Digital Asset Links JSON file at `/.well-known/assetlinks.json`).
- On iOS: **Universal Links** (verified via `apple-app-site-association` JSON).
- **Advantages:** unique ownership (domain verification), graceful web fallback, OS-level trust.

**Example Flow (1.5 marks):**
1. User clicks a link in a Facebook post: `https://myiotapp.com/device/thermostat?id=42`.
2. Android's `App Link Verifier` checks the `assetlinks.json` and confirms ownership.
3. The OS launches `MyIotApp` directly (skipping the browser).
4. The app's `MainActivity` parses the URI, extracts `device=thermostat` and `id=42`.
5. The router navigates to the `ThermostatDetailFragment`.
6. The fragment queries the IoT cloud for the device's current state and renders it.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ‚Äî Lose NO Marks!**
> 1. **Confusing OAuth 2.0 with OIDC** ‚Äî OAuth is for *authorization*, OIDC is for *authentication*. Examiners deduct 1‚Äì2 marks if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting the port** ‚Äî Always mention **HTTPS port 443** for social APIs; HTTP is rejected.
> 3. **Ignoring PKCE** ‚Äî For mobile apps, plain Authorization Code Flow (without PKCE) is considered insecure since 2019. Always mention PKCE for mobile contexts.
> 4. **Writing `client_secret` directly in a mobile app** ‚Äî This is a critical security flaw; examiners mark it as 0. The `client_secret` must live on a backend server.
> 5. **Mixing up `access_token` vs `id_token`** ‚Äî `access_token` grants API access; `id_token` (JWT) proves identity. Many students swap these.
> 6. **Custom URL Scheme vs Universal Links** ‚Äî do not call them "the same thing". Universal Links are verified HTTPS URLs, custom schemes are not.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **OAuth 2.0** = authorization framework (RFC 6749); **OIDC** = authentication layer built on top.
- **PKCE** is **mandatory** for mobile/native clients (RFC 7636).
- **Access Token** lifetime ‚âà **3600 s**; **Refresh Token** lifetime ‚âà **60 days**.
- **Authorization Code** lifetime ‚âà **10 minutes** (vulnerable to interception ‚Üí PKCE defends).
- **Three Layers of Integration:** Identity ‚Üí Authorization ‚Üí Data Exchange.
- **Share Intent on Android:** `ACTION_SEND` (single) or `ACTION_SEND_MULTIPLE` (multiple).
- **Token Storage:** Android Keystore / iOS Keychain ‚Äî never plain `SharedPreferences`.
- **HTTPS / TLS 1.2+** is non-negotiable for all social API traffic.
- **Deep Linking:** Custom URL schemes (hijackable) vs **App Links / Universal Links** (verified, secure).
- **Rate Limits:** Facebook 200/hr, Twitter 50/15-min, Google 1000/100s ‚Äî design retry-and-backoff logic.
- **Principle of Least Privilege:** request *minimum* scopes; allow **unlinking** at any time.
- **GDPR / Kerala DPDP Act compliance:** display a privacy policy, obtain explicit consent, support data deletion.
- **JWT = Header.Payload.Signature** ‚Äî base64url-encoded, signed with HS256 or RS256.
- **Cross-platform Push:** FCM (Android + iOS) is the de-facto standard for app-generated notifications.
- **Always validate the `iss`, `aud`, and `exp` claims** of any incoming `id_token` before trusting its identity.

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Mnemonic ‚Äî "SCOReD"**
> **S**cope (least privilege) ‚Üí **C**onsent (user grants) ‚Üí **O**Auth (token flow) ‚Üí **R**efresh (rotate) ‚Üí **e**ncrypt (HTTPS) ‚Üí **D**isconnect (unlink support).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
