# SECTION 1 ‚Äî Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Android Things** (formerly codenamed *Brillo*) is an **Android-based embedded operating system** developed by **Google** specifically for **Internet of Things (IoT) and low-power smart connected devices**. It is built as a stripped-down extension of the **Android Open Source Project (AOSP)** stack, retaining the core Android framework (Activity, Service, Content Provider, Broadcast Receiver, and the Linux kernel) while removing components that are irrelevant to headless embedded devices such as the System UI, native dialer, browser, and most user-facing Google Mobile Services (GMS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (verbatim-grade):** "Android Things is a vertically integrated Android-based platform that provides a single Application Programming Interface (API) surface, a managed runtime (ART), and a **User-Space Driver Architecture** to enable developers to build IoT products on supported System-on-Chips (SoCs) such as **Raspberry Pi 3, Intel Edison, and NXP i.MX7D**."

> [!WARNING]
> **Platform Status Update:** Google officially **discontinued Android Things on 30 January 2022**. However, for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, the platform is still prescribed in Module 4 syllabus, and the underlying **Peripheral I/O API, User-Space Driver model, and Weave communication architecture** remain examinable as they directly influence modern replacements such as **Android Open Source Project for Embedded (AOSP-Embedded)**, **Google Cloud IoT Core**, and **Open Horizon**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **full-fledged Android smartphone** with a vibrant touchscreen, a launcher, hundreds of apps, and Google Play Store. That is "regular Android."

Now imagine you want the **same brain (Android Runtime + Linux kernel + Java/Kotlin API)** but inside a **smart light bulb** that has **no screen, no battery-hungry graphics, and only a few GPIO pins** for sensing light and switching a relay on/off. That stripped-down, headless, embedded cousin is **Android Things**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
> - Regular Android  ‚Üí A fully loaded family car (AC, music system, 5 seats, GPS).
> - Android Things  ‚Üí A **delivery motorcycle** (same engine, but stripped to essentials for one specific job ‚Äî delivering parcels to a single destination).
> - The "destination" here is **talking to sensors and actuators** through standardized buses (**GPIO, I¬≤C, SPI, UART, PWM**) and pushing data to the **cloud** via the **Weave** protocol.

---

## 1.3 Salient Features of Android Things (High-Yield Points)

1. **Headless OS:** No graphical launcher; runs as a single foreground app launching at boot.
2. **User-Space Drivers:** Peripheral drivers live *outside* the Linux kernel, in the Android application sandbox, making them **portable across SoCs**.
3. **Peripheral I/O API (`android.things` package):** A unified Java/Kotlin API to access low-level hardware buses.
4. **Weave Communication Protocol:** Lightweight, JSON-based IoT protocol for cloud-device messaging.
5. **Google Cloud IoT Core Integration:** Managed service for bi-directional device‚Üîcloud telemetry.
6. **TensorFlow Lite Compatibility:** On-device ML inference for vision and audio.
7. **Long-Term Support Kernels:** Verified boot and over-the-air (OTA) updates via Google-managed infrastructure.

---

## 1.4 Important Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric / Constant | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| ART Dalvik Conversion | **100% AOT + JIT hybrid** | Faster cold-start on embedded devices |
| Min RAM footprint | **~512 MB** | Configurable per device profile |
| Min Flash Storage | **~4 GB** | Verified on Pi 3 Model B |
| Supported SoCs | **Raspberry Pi 3, Intel Edison, NXP Pico i.MX6UL, NXP Argon i.MX7D** | Reference hardware list |
| Weave message size cap | **16 KB** payload | Per-message cloud limit |
| Weave QoS | **0 (at-most-once), 1 (at-least-once)** | MQTT-style semantics |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ‚Äî Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of Android Things

Android Things retains the standard Android architectural stack but **deletes the System UI layer** and **adds a User-Space Driver layer** between the application and the hardware. The stack from top to bottom is:

1. **Application Layer** ‚Äî The developer's APK, written in Java/Kotlin, registered as the **launcher/home activity** in the manifest using the special category `android.intent.category.HOME`.
2. **Android Framework Layer** ‚Äî Same `android.*` APIs as mobile Android (Activity Manager, Window Manager, etc.), plus the new `android.things.*` package.
3. **Android Runtime (ART) Layer** ‚Äî AOT + JIT compiled bytecode execution; preloads classes for low memory overhead.
4. **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL) + User-Space Drivers** ‚Äî Vendor-specific drivers (e.g., GPIO, I¬≤C) loaded as Android Services.
5. **Linux Kernel (LTS)** ‚Äî Verified boot, SELinux in enforcing mode, no dynamic module loading.

---

## 2.2 Why a "User-Space Driver" Model? (The Core 'Why')

In traditional Linux, drivers live in **kernel space**. This has three problems for IoT:
- A kernel bug can crash the entire device.
- Drivers are tightly coupled to specific kernel versions, making OTA updates risky.
- Porting a driver across SoCs requires recompilation.

Android Things solves this by moving peripheral drivers into **user space** as **bound Android Services**. The advantages are:

| Property | Kernel-Space Driver (Traditional Linux) | User-Space Driver (Android Things) |
|---|---|---|
| Crash impact | Crashes entire kernel / kernel panic | Crashes only the driver service; restarts automatically |
| Portability | Must be recompiled per SoC | Same APK runs on any supported SoC |
| Security | Runs in ring 0 (full privileges) | Runs in SELinux sandbox (ring 3) |
| Update mechanism | Tied to kernel image | Distributed via APK OTA |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot-Question Hook:** "Differentiate User-Space and Kernel-Space drivers in the context of Android Things." This is a frequently repeated 7-mark conceptual question.

---

## 2.3 The Peripheral I/O API (`android.things.pio`)

The unified API exposes five hardware buses. Every bus is opened through a **`PeripheralManagerService`** and managed via try-with-resources to release the pin on closure.

### 2.3.1 GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output)
- Direction: `DIRECTION_IN` or `DIRECTION_OUT`
- Edge detection: `EDGE_NONE`, `EDGE_RISING`, `EDGE_FALLING`, `EDGE_BOTH`
- Voltage levels: **3.3 V** on Pi 3, **1.8 V** on Edison.

### 2.3.2 I¬≤C (Inter-Integrated Circuit)
- Two-wire: **SDA (data)** + **SCL (clock)**.
- Speeds: Standard-mode **100 kHz**, Fast-mode **400 kHz**, Fast-mode Plus **1 MHz**, High-speed **3.4 MHz**.
- Addressing: **7-bit or 10-bit** slave address.

### 2.3.3 SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface)
- Four-wire: **MOSI, MISO, SCLK, CS**.
- Clock frequencies up to **~25 MHz** on supported SoCs.
- Modes 0, 1, 2, 3 (CPOL/CPHA combinations).

### 2.3.4 UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter)
- Asynchronous serial; no shared clock.
- Configurable baud rate up to **~4 Mbps**; data bits, parity, stop bits.

### 2.3.5 PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)
- Used for dimming LEDs, controlling servo motors, fan speed.
- Frequency range: typical **1 Hz to 1 kHz** for hobby servos; up to **~24 MHz** raw on some SoCs.

---

## 2.4 Weave Communication Protocol

**Weave** is Google's cross-platform IoT communication protocol that was tightly coupled with Android Things. It is built on top of **MQTT** and adds:

- **Schema-validated JSON payloads** (defined by *Trait* definitions).
- **Device-to-cloud commands** (bidirectional).
- **Multi-tenant authentication** using X.509 certificates.

> [!NOTE]
> Although Weave was deprecated alongside Android Things, its *Trait* model influenced the **Smart Home Core / Home Graph** APIs that succeed it.

### Weave Message Lifecycle (Logical Steps)
1. Device requests a **registration token** from the Weave server.
2. Token is exchanged for an **X.509 device certificate**.
3. Device opens a **TLS-protected MQTT** channel to Google Cloud IoT Core.
4. Telemetry is published to a **config topic** (`/devices/{id}/events`).
5. Cloud pushes state updates to a **state topic** (`/devices/{id}/state`).
6. Commands from the cloud arrive on the **command topic** (`/devices/{id}/commands/#`).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula & Reference Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Units | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| $f_{PWM}$ | PWM output frequency | Hz | LED dimming, servo control |
| $D$ | Duty cycle = $\frac{T_{ON}}{T_{period}} \times 100$ | % | Average power delivery |
| $T_{period}$ | $1 / f_{PWM}$ | seconds | Period |
| $f_{SCL}$ | I¬≤C clock frequency | Hz | Bus timing |
| $t_{setup}$ | Data setup time before SCL rises | ns | I¬≤C protocol |
| $N_{addr}$ | Address space of I¬≤C slaves | devices | Bus sizing |
| $\lambda_{OTA}$ | OTA update success rate | % | Reliability metric |
| $T_{boot}$ | Cold-boot time to first frame | ms | Embedded latency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The vertical pipe symbol has been deliberately avoided in the table above (`\vert` would be used in actual LaTeX). For KTU answer sheets, students are advised to write $D$ in plain math notation, not inside tables.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Android Things powered several **commercial products** before its sunset:
- **LG Smart Refrigerators** (2018‚Äì2021) used Weave for grocery tracking.
- **Lenovo Smart Display** leveraged the TensorFlow integration for face-tracking camera.
- **Hatch Baby smart baby devices** used GPIO + PWM for soothing light patterns.

The architectural lessons (user-space drivers, headless boot, single foreground app) continue to influence **AOSP for Embedded**, **OpenThread on Android**, and **Google's Embedded Google Assistant SDK**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ‚Äî Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Calculating PWM Duty Cycle for LED Brightness

A smart lamp uses a **PWM-driven LED** connected to a GPIO pin of an Android Things board. The PWM frequency is set to **$f_{PWM} = 1 \text{ kHz}$** and the LED must glow at **40 %** of its maximum brightness.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Convert frequency to period.**
$$T_{period} = \frac{1}{f_{PWM}} = \frac{1}{1 \times 10^{3}} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Express duty cycle mathematically.**
The duty cycle $D$ in percentage is defined as the ratio of the ON-time to the total period, multiplied by 100.
$$D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{period}} \times 100$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Solve for the required ON-time.**
$$T_{ON} = \frac{D \times T_{period}}{100} = \frac{40 \times 1 \text{ ms}}{100} = 0.4 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Convert ON-time into microseconds for register-level programming.**
$$T_{ON} = 0.4 \text{ ms} = 400 \text{ ¬µs}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Express the corresponding OFF-time.**
$$T_{OFF} = T_{period} - T_{ON} = 1 \text{ ms} - 0.4 \text{ ms} = 0.6 \text{ ms} = 600 \text{ ¬µs}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The GPIO pin must output a logic HIGH for **400 ¬µs** and a logic LOW for **600 ¬µs**, repeating every **1 ms**, to achieve 40 % perceived brightness.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: I¬≤C Bus Sizing for a Sensor Array

An Android Things board is wired to a sensor array on a single I¬≤C bus. The bus uses a **7-bit addressing** scheme, and we need to determine the **maximum number of unique slave devices** that can be theoretically addressed.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Recall the address width.**
A 7-bit address allows $2^{7}$ unique binary patterns.
$$N_{addr} = 2^{7} = 128$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Subtract reserved addresses.**
I¬≤C reserves the address **0000 000** for the *General Call* and **1111 111** (0x7F) for the *10-bit addressing prefix*.
$$N_{usable} = 128 - 2 = 126$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Apply bus-capacitance rule of thumb.**
The I¬≤C specification limits bus capacitance to **400 pF** in standard mode, after which rise/fall time violations occur. Each device typically adds **~10 pF**. Therefore, the practical bus population is:
$$N_{practical} = \left\lfloor \frac{400 \text{ pF}}{10 \text{ pF/device}} \right\rfloor = 40 \text{ devices}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Although **126** unique addresses exist, the practical population on a single bus is **~40 devices** due to physical capacitance limits. To scale further, an **I¬≤C multiplexer (e.g., TCA9548A)** is used.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Weave Cloud Telemetry Cost Estimation

A factory deploys **$N = 500$** Android Things sensors. Each sensor publishes a telemetry message of size **$S = 2 \text{ KB}$** every **$T = 30$ seconds**. The data is retained in the cloud for **30 days**.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Compute messages per device per day.**
$$M_{day} = \frac{86400 \text{ s/day}}{30 \text{ s}} = 2880 \text{ messages/device/day}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute total fleet messages per day.**
$$M_{fleet} = M_{day} \times N = 2880 \times 500 = 1{,}440{,}000 \text{ messages/day}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute daily storage.**
$$V_{day} = M_{fleet} \times S = 1{,}440{,}000 \times 2 \text{ KB} = 2.88 \times 10^{6} \text{ KB} = 2.88 \text{ GB/day}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compute 30-day retention storage.**
$$V_{30} = 2.88 \text{ GB/day} \times 30 \text{ days} = 86.4 \text{ GB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A modest 500-device fleet generates **2.88 GB per day** and **86.4 GB per month**. This justifies using tiered cloud storage and lossy compression in real IoT rollouts.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Boot-Time Optimization Analysis

An Android Things device takes **$T_{boot} = 4.5 \text{ s}$** from cold start to first sensor reading. The kernel accounts for **$T_{kernel} = 1.2 \text{ s}$** and ART initialization takes **$T_{ART} = 2.0 \text{ s}$**. The user-space driver for the temperature sensor adds **$T_{drv} = 1.3 \text{ s}$**.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Sum the known contributions.**
$$T_{sum} = T_{kernel} + T_{ART} + T_{drv} = 1.2 + 2.0 + 1.3 = 4.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Identify the dominant latency contributor.**
The largest single contributor is **ART initialization at 2.0 s**, accounting for:
$$\text{Percent}_{ART} = \frac{2.0}{4.5} \times 100 = 44.44\%$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Recommend optimization.**
AOT (Ahead-of-Time) compilation of the *temperature sensor driver* can shift its loading from the JIT path to the AOT path, reducing $T_{drv}$ by an estimated **60 %**.
$$T_{drv}^{opt} = 1.3 \times 0.40 = 0.52 \text{ s}$$
$$T_{boot}^{opt} = 1.2 + 2.0 + 0.52 = 3.72 \text{ s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A single AOT optimization of the driver class shaves **0.78 s (‚âà17.3 %)** off cold-boot time, which is critical for time-sensitive industrial control applications.

# SECTION 4 ‚Äî Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Android Things

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP["Application Layer (User APK)"]
    THINGS["android.things Peripheral I/O API"]
    FW["Android Framework (Activity, Service, ContentProvider)"]
    ART["Android Runtime (ART)"]
    USD["User-Space Drivers (GPIO / I2C / SPI / UART / PWM)"]
    HAL["Hardware Abstraction Layer"]
    KERN["Linux Kernel (LTS, SELinux)"]
    SOC["SoC Hardware (Pi 3 / Edison / i.MX7D)"]
    SENS["Sensors & Actuators"]
    WEAVE["Weave Protocol"]
    CLOUD["Google Cloud IoT Core"]
    APP --> THINGS
    APP --> FW
    FW --> ART
    ART --> USD
    USD --> HAL
    HAL --> KERN
    KERN --> SOC
    SOC --> SENS
    APP --> WEAVE
    WEAVE --> CLOUD
    CLOUD --> WEAVE
```

## 4.2 Weave Communication Flow Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant DEV as Android Things Device
    participant WEAVE as Weave Broker (Cloud IoT Core)
    participant APP as User Mobile App
    DEV->>WEAVE: TLS handshake + X.509 cert auth
    DEV->>WEAVE: Publish telemetry (config topic)
    WEAVE->>APP: Push notification (new state)
    APP->>WEAVE: Send command (e.g., "Turn ON bulb")
    WEAVE->>DEV: Deliver command (command topic)
    DEV->>WEAVE: ACK + state update
```

## 4.3 Peripheral I/O API Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q{"Need to talk to hardware?"}
    Q -->|Single digital pin| GPIO["GPIO"]
    Q -->|Pulse / dim / servo| PWM["PWM"]
    Q -->|Low-speed multi-device sensor| I2C["I2C"]
    Q -->|High-speed bulk sensor| SPI["SPI"]
    Q -->|Serial debug or GPS| UART["UART"]
    GPIO --> SRV["Open via PeripheralManagerService"]
    PWM --> SRV
    I2C --> SRV
    SPI --> SRV
    UART --> SRV
    SRV --> CLOSE["try-with-resources closes pin"]
```


# SECTION 5 ‚Äî KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî June 2023 (Model)]
**Define Android Things. Mention any two key features.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Android Things is a **stripped-down, Android-based embedded operating system** developed by Google for building IoT and smart connected devices on resource-constrained hardware such as the Raspberry Pi 3 and NXP i.MX7D.
Two key features:
1. **User-Space Driver Architecture** ‚Äî peripheral drivers run as sandboxed Android Services rather than kernel modules.
2. **Peripheral I/O API (`android.things.pio`)** ‚Äî provides a unified Java/Kotlin interface to GPIO, I¬≤C, SPI, UART, and PWM buses.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Writing "Android Things is a phone OS" will cost 1 mark. Always mention the *headless* nature and the *IoT* target.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2022 (Model)]
**List the five peripheral buses supported by the Android Things Peripheral I/O API.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The five peripheral buses are:
1. **GPIO** (General Purpose Input/Output) ‚Äî digital pin control.
2. **I¬≤C** (Inter-Integrated Circuit) ‚Äî two-wire synchronous bus.
3. **SPI** (Serial Peripheral Interface) ‚Äî four-wire synchronous bus.
4. **UART** (Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter) ‚Äî async serial.
5. **PWM** (Pulse Width Modulation) ‚Äî timed pulse output for motors/LEDs.

---

## PART B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äî June 2024 (Model) ‚Äî Module 4, Internal Choice Set 1]**

**(a)** Explain the layered architecture of Android Things with a neat block diagram. Compare user-space drivers with traditional kernel-space drivers. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**1. Layered Architecture (4 marks):**
Android Things is structured in six layers, from top to bottom:
- **Application Layer** ‚Äî the developer's APK with a single launcher/home activity.
- **Android Framework Layer** ‚Äî `android.*` and `android.things.*` packages.
- **Android Runtime (ART) Layer** ‚Äî AOT + JIT hybrid execution.
- **User-Space Driver Layer** ‚Äî peripheral drivers as bound Android Services.
- **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)** ‚Äî vendor-implemented SoC glue.
- **Linux Kernel (LTS)** ‚Äî verified boot, SELinux, no dynamic modules.

**2. User-Space vs Kernel-Space Drivers (3 marks):**

| Property | User-Space Driver | Kernel-Space Driver |
|---|---|---|
| Execution privilege | Ring 3 (unprivileged) | Ring 0 (privileged) |
| Failure impact | Isolated; auto-restart | Kernel panic |
| Portability | Same APK across SoCs | Must recompile |
| Update model | APK OTA | Kernel image OTA |
| Security | SELinux sandbox | Full kernel trust |

**(b)** Describe the Weave communication protocol. Explain how an Android Things device authenticates with Google Cloud IoT Core. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**1. Weave Protocol Description (3 marks):**
Weave is Google's IoT communication protocol built atop **MQTT over TLS**. It uses *Trait*-validated JSON payloads for schema enforcement, supports bidirectional telemetry, and runs on resource-constrained devices.

**2. Authentication Flow (4 marks):**
1. The device generates a **public/private RSA key pair** and registers with the Weave broker using a manufacturer-issued token.
2. The broker returns an **X.509 device certificate** bound to a unique device ID.
3. The device opens a **TLS 1.2 channel** and presents the certificate during the MQTT CONNECT handshake.
4. The broker validates the certificate against a **root Certificate Authority (CA)** and grants publish/subscribe rights to the device's specific topics:
   - `/devices/{id}/events` ‚Äî telemetry
   - `/devices/{id}/state` ‚Äî configuration state
   - `/devices/{id}/commands/#` ‚Äî incoming commands

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (Part b):** Students often write "the device uses a username/password." This is incorrect. Weave uses **asymmetric X.509 certificates**, not symmetric credentials. Losing 2 marks is common here.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023 (Model) ‚Äî Module 4, Internal Choice Set 2]**

**(a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the Peripheral I/O API of Android Things. How does an application open and release a GPIO pin? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**1. Peripheral I/O API Overview (3 marks):**
The `android.things.pio` package provides access to five buses: **GPIO, I¬≤C, SPI, UART, and PWM**. A central class `PeripheralManagerService` lists available ports, opens them, and returns handle objects (`Gpio`, `I2cDevice`, `SpiDevice`, `UartDevice`, `Pwm`).

**2. GPIO Pin Open/Release Flow (4 marks):**
- The developer calls `peripheralManager.openGpio("BCM21")` to obtain a `Gpio` handle.
- Direction is set: `gpio.setDirection(Gpio.DIRECTION_OUT)`.
- For input pins, edge triggers are configured: `gpio.setEdgeTriggerType(Gpio.EDGE_RISING)`.
- A `GpioCallback` is registered for asynchronous edge events.
- The handle is released automatically when the resource is closed inside a `try-with-resources` block, freeing the pin for other components.

**(b)** A PWM signal drives an LED on an Android Things board. If the period is **2 ms** and the duty cycle is **25 %**, compute the ON-time and OFF-time. If the frequency is doubled, what is the new duty cycle to maintain the same average brightness? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Compute the period.**
$$T_{period} = 2 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute the ON-time at 25 % duty cycle.**
$$T_{ON} = \frac{D \times T_{period}}{100} = \frac{25 \times 2 \text{ ms}}{100} = 0.5 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute the OFF-time.**
$$T_{OFF} = T_{period} - T_{ON} = 2 \text{ ms} - 0.5 \text{ ms} = 1.5 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compute the new period when frequency is doubled.**
$$f_{new} = 2 f_{old} = 2 \times \frac{1}{2 \text{ ms}} = 1 \text{ kHz}$$
$$T_{period}^{new} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî To maintain the same average brightness, the ON-time must stay proportional.**
$$T_{ON}^{new} = 0.5 \text{ ms}$$
$$D_{new} = \frac{0.5}{1.0} \times 100 = 50\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** ON-time = **0.5 ms**, OFF-time = **1.5 ms**. When frequency doubles, the duty cycle must **double to 50 %** to preserve the same average power delivered to the LED.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (Part b):** Students often keep the duty cycle constant at 25 % when the frequency changes. This halves the average brightness. Always remind the examiner that **average brightness ‚àù ON-time, not duty cycle alone**, when the period changes.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ‚úÖ Android Things = **headless, Android-based, IoT-focused OS** built on AOSP.
- ‚úÖ Successor/precursor relationship: Brillo (2015) ‚Üí Android Things (2016) ‚Üí Discontinued (Jan 2022) ‚Üí Influences AOSP-Embedded.
- ‚úÖ Supported SoCs: **Raspberry Pi 3, Intel Edison, NXP Pico i.MX6UL, NXP Argon i.MX7D**.
- ‚úÖ Five Peripheral I/O buses: **GPIO, I¬≤C, SPI, UART, PWM** (mnemonic: **G-I-S-U-P**).
- ‚úÖ User-space drivers = sandboxed, portable, auto-restarting; reside in `android.things.pio`.
- ‚úÖ I¬≤C: 7-bit addressing ‚Üí **126 usable addresses**; practical cap **~40 devices** per bus at 400 pF.
- ‚úÖ PWM duty cycle formula: $D = (T_{ON} / T_{period}) \times 100$.
- ‚úÖ Weave protocol = **MQTT-over-TLS with X.509 certs**; topics follow the `/devices/{id}/...` pattern.
- ‚úÖ Cloud telemetry example: 500 devices √ó 2 KB / 30 s ‚âà **2.88 GB/day** ingested.
- ‚úÖ Cold-boot optimization: AOT-compile the driver class to cut boot time by ~17 %.
- ‚úÖ Always release peripherals in `finally` or `try-with-resources` to avoid pin-lock bugs.
- ‚úÖ Examiner expects: schematic diagram, comparison table, and worked numerical example for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Android Things** (formerly codenamed *Brillo*) is an **Android-based operating system** developed by Google specifically for **Internet of Things (IoT) and embedded devices**. The **Installation of Android Things** refers to the systematic procedure of flashing the Android Things system image onto a supported IoT development board (such as **Raspberry Pi 3**, **NXP Pico i.MX7D**, or **Intel Edison**), configuring it for network access, and establishing communication via the **Android Debug Bridge (ADB)** for application deployment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Under the 2024 Scheme, students must understand the *end-to-end installation workflow* including hardware prerequisites, image flashing, console-based provisioning, and ADB-based application sideloading.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy ‚Äî "The Smart Home Brain":**
> Imagine you bought a brand-new smart speaker (like a Google Home). Before it can answer "Hey Google," it must be **unboxed**, **plugged in**, **connected to Wi-Fi**, and **registered with the cloud**. The Android Things installation is the *exact same process*, but for a tiny, low-power IoT board instead of a consumer speaker.
>
> - The **board** = the smart speaker's hardware
> - The **OS image** = the speaker's embedded firmware
> - **Flashing** = the act of copying firmware onto the device
> - **ADB connection** = the USB cable "tether" used to install your custom "skills" (apps) onto the device

### 1.3 Core Components of the Installation

| Component | Description |
|---|---|
| **Android Things Console** | Web-based Google console used to download official system images and manage devices |
| **System Image** | A compressed `.zip` or `.img` file containing the full Android Things OS tailored to a specific board |
| **Supported SoM (System on Module)** | Hardware platforms: **Raspberry Pi 3 Model B**, **NXP i.MX7D Pico**, **NXP i.MX6UL Pico** |
| **Tools Bundle** | Contains `fastboot`, `adb`, and the `setup.sh` flashing utility |

> [!NOTE]
> Google officially **deprecated** the Android Things Console for new device registrations in **December 2019**, but the platform, its images, and its installation methodology remain highly relevant for KTU board examinations as a foundational IoT OS study topic.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pre-Installation Requirements (Hardware & Software)

#### 2.1.1 Hardware Prerequisites
- **Supported Development Board:** Raspberry Pi 3 Model B (most common in KTU labs) or NXP Pico platforms.
- **Power Supply:** A stable **5V / 2.5A** micro-USB power adapter.
- **MicroSD Card:** Minimum **8 GB**, Class 10 recommended (used as boot storage for RPi3).
- **Host Computer:** Running Windows, macOS, or Linux with USB ports.
- **Connection Cables:** Micro-USB cable (for ADB/data), HDMI cable (optional, for display debugging).

#### 2.1.2 Software Prerequisites
- **Android SDK Platform Tools** (contains `adb` and `fastboot`).
- **Android Things Image** downloaded from the official console.
- **Etcher** or `dd` command-line tool for flashing the `.img` file.
- **Java JDK 8+** for app development post-installation.

### 2.2 The Installation Lifecycle ‚Äî Structured Logic Steps

The installation is logically divided into **six sequential phases**:

1. **Phase 1 ‚Äî Environment Preparation:** Install Android SDK Platform Tools; verify `adb` and `fastboot` are accessible via the system `PATH`.
2. **Phase 2 ‚Äî Image Acquisition:** Download the correct Android Things image (e.g., `andriod-things-raspberrypi3-...zip`) matching the target board.
3. **Phase 3 ‚Äî Flashing the Image:** Extract the `.img` file and write it onto the MicroSD card (for RPi3) using Etcher.
4. **Phase 4 ‚Äî First Boot & Network Configuration:** Insert the SD card, power the board, and connect it to a Wi-Fi network (or use Ethernet).
5. **Phase 5 ‚Äî ADB Connection:** Connect the board to the host PC via USB; run `adb devices` to verify detection.
6. **Phase 6 ‚Äî App Deployment:** Use `adb install` to push custom Android Things APKs to the device.

### 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why flashing instead of installing?** IoT boards like Raspberry Pi do not have a built-in BIOS/UEFI to load an OS dynamically. They must boot from a *pre-written image* on external storage. Flashing writes the OS *bit-for-bit* onto the storage medium, making it bootable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why ADB over Wi-Fi instead of USB?** Once deployed in a real IoT scenario (e.g., a smart agriculture sensor in a field), physical USB access is impractical. Android Things supports **ADB over TCP/IP** via the command `adb connect <IP_address>:5555`.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since this is an installation-based topic, the "formulas" are critical commands and parameters:

| Command / Parameter | Purpose | Example / Value |
|---|---|---|
| `adb devices` | List connected Android Things devices | Shows device serial + state |
| `adb connect <ip>:5555` | Connect wirelessly over ADB | `adb connect 192.168.1.10:5555` |
| `adb install <app>.apk` | Install an APK to the device | `adb install myapp.apk` |
| `adb logcat` | Stream device logs to host terminal | Used for debugging IoT apps |
| `fastboot flashall -w` | Flashes boot image (for NXP boards) | Erases userdata + caches |
| `setup.sh` | Linux tool for flashing NXP boards | Bundled with the image ZIP |
| Boot Partition Size | Partition allocated to OS image | **~512 MB** |
| `wpa_supplicant.conf` | Wi-Fi credentials file (pre-baked) | Placed in SD card boot partition |
| ADB Default Port | TCP port for ADB communication | **5555** |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The installation of Android Things (and similar embedded OS like **Raspbian OS**, **Ubuntu Core**, or **FreeRTOS**) underpins:
- **Smart Home Devices:** Smart thermostats, security cameras.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Factory floor sensor hubs.
- **Retail IoT:** Interactive kiosks and digital signage.
- **Edge Computing Gateways:** Bridging local sensors to cloud platforms (Firebase, AWS IoT).
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Installation Procedure (Raspberry Pi 3 ‚Äî KTU Lab Standard)

### 3.1 Phase 1: Setting Up the Host Machine

**Step 1.1** ‚Äî Download **Android SDK Platform Tools** from the official Android developer site.

**Step 1.2** ‚Äî Extract the ZIP and add the `platform-tools/` directory to the system `PATH` environment variable.

**Step 1.3** ‚Äî Verify installation using the command:

$$
\text{Verification:} \quad \texttt{adb version} \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Expected Output: Android Debug Bridge version 1.0.xx}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** The system `PATH` allows the host OS to recognize `adb` and `fastboot` commands from any directory in the terminal, mimicking globally installed binaries.

---

### 3.2 Phase 2: Acquiring the Android Things Image

**Step 2.1** ‚Äî Sign in to the **Android Things Console** (archive versions are still hosted on Google's IoT archives).

**Step 2.2** ‚Äî Select the target hardware: **Raspberry Pi 3** from the dropdown menu.

**Step 2.3** ‚Äî Select the Android Things OS version (e.g., **1.0.11** or later).

**Step 2.4** ‚Äî Download the resulting ZIP, which contains:
- `iots_raspberrypi3.img` ‚Äî The bootable system image
- `factory_params.json` ‚Äî Configuration metadata

---

### 3.3 Phase 3: Flashing the Image to MicroSD

**Step 3.1** ‚Äî Insert the MicroSD card into the host PC using a card reader.

**Step 3.2** ‚Äî Open **Etcher** (or use the Linux `dd` command):

$$
\texttt{sudo dd if=iots\_raspberrypi3.img of=/dev/sdX bs=4M status=progress}
$$

Where:
- `if` = input file (the image)
- `of` = output file (the SD card device node)
- `bs=4M` = block size of 4 Megabytes (optimizes write speed)

**Step 3.3** ‚Äî Wait for the write to complete (typically **3‚Äì7 minutes** depending on card speed class).

---

### 3.4 Phase 4: Configuring Wi-Fi (Pre-Boot Method)

**Step 4.1** ‚Äî Re-insert the SD card into the host PC. The `boot` partition is now visible.

**Step 4.2** ‚Äî Create a file named `wpa_supplicant.conf` in the boot partition with the following content:

```text
network={
    ssid="YourWiFiName"
    psk="YourWiFiPassword"
    key_mgmt=WPA-PSK
}
```

**Step 4.3** ‚Äî Eject the SD card safely, insert it into the Raspberry Pi 3, and power it on.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logic:** Android Things reads this file *only on the first boot*. After successful Wi-Fi association, the file is internally moved to `/data/misc/wifi/`, and subsequent boots connect automatically.

---

### 3.5 Phase 5: Establishing ADB Connection

**Step 5.1** ‚Äî Wait approximately **30‚Äì60 seconds** for the board to fully boot. The onboard LED will stabilize.

**Step 5.2** ‚Äî Connect the Raspberry Pi to the host PC via a **micro-USB cable** (connected to the *data* port, not the power-only port).

**Step 5.3** ‚Äî On the host terminal, run:

$$
\texttt{adb devices}
$$

**Expected Output:**

$$
\texttt{List of devices attached} \quad \texttt{1234567890ABCDEF \quad device}
$$

**Step 5.4** ‚Äî For wireless ADB (over the same Wi-Fi network):

$$
\texttt{adb connect <RPi\_IP\_Address>:5555}
$$

---

### 3.6 Phase 6: Deploying a Test Application

**Step 6.1** ‚Äî Build your custom IoT app in **Android Studio** (targeting API 27+ for Android Things compatibility).

**Step 6.2** ‚Äî Generate the signed/un-signed APK (`app-debug.apk`).

**Step 6.3** ‚Äî Install it onto the device:

$$
\texttt{adb install -r app-debug.apk}
$$

The `-r` flag allows **reinstallation** and keeps existing user data.

**Step 6.4** ‚Äî Launch the app's main activity:

$$
\texttt{adb shell am start -n com.example.iot/.MainActivity}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** The `am start` command invokes the **Activity Manager** inside the Android Things shell, which is functionally identical to tapping an app icon on a regular Android device.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Installation Workflow (Mermaid)

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2023]**
**List any three hardware requirements for installing Android Things on a Raspberry Pi 3.**

**Model Answer:**

The three primary hardware requirements are:

1. **Raspberry Pi 3 Model B** development board (the supported SoM for Android Things).
2. **MicroSD Card** of minimum **8 GB** capacity (Class 10 recommended) to host the bootable OS image.
3. **5V / 2.5A Micro-USB Power Supply** to provide stable power to the board during boot and operation.

*(Additional supporting items: USB data cable, HDMI monitor for debugging, host PC with USB ports.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2022]**
**What is the role of the `wpa_supplicant.conf` file during Android Things installation?**

**Model Answer:**

The `wpa_supplicant.conf` file is a **plain-text configuration file** placed in the **boot partition** of the MicroSD card **before** the first boot. Its role is to:

- **Pre-configure Wi-Fi credentials** (SSID, password, and security protocol) so the Raspberry Pi 3 automatically connects to the specified wireless network upon first power-on.

This eliminates the need for a wired Ethernet connection or a serial console to input Wi-Fi details, simplifying the provisioning workflow for headless IoT deployments.

---

### 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3a. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024 Model Paper]**
**Explain the step-by-step procedure to install Android Things on a Raspberry Pi 3. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The installation of Android Things on a Raspberry Pi 3 follows a structured six-phase procedure:

**Phase 1 ‚Äî Host Environment Setup**
- Download the **Android SDK Platform Tools** ZIP from the official Android developer portal.
- Extract and add the `platform-tools/` directory to the host's system `PATH` variable.
- Verify using `adb version` and `fastboot --version` commands.

**Phase 2 ‚Äî Image Acquisition**
- Log in to the **Android Things Console** using a Google account.
- Select **Raspberry Pi 3** as the target hardware and the desired Android Things version.
- Download the image bundle ZIP file containing `iots_raspberrypi3.img`.

**Phase 3 ‚Äî Flashing the MicroSD Card**
- Insert a blank **8 GB+ MicroSD card** into the host PC.
- Use **Etcher** (GUI) or the `dd` command (CLI) to write the `.img` file onto the SD card:
  $$\texttt{dd if=iots\_raspberrypi3.img of=/dev/sdX bs=4M}$$
- Safely eject the card after the write completes.

**Phase 4 ‚Äî Pre-Boot Wi-Fi Configuration**
- Re-mount the SD card's **boot partition** (visible on Windows/macOS/Linux).
- Create a file `wpa_supplicant.conf` with the target SSID and PSK credentials.
- Safely eject the card.

**Phase 5 ‚Äî First Boot**
- Insert the SD card into the Raspberry Pi 3.
- Connect the **5V/2.5A power supply**; the red LED turns on, and the green LED blinks (indicating SD card access).
- Wait **30‚Äì60 seconds** for the Wi-Fi association to complete.

**Phase 6 ‚Äî ADB Connection Verification**
- Connect the board to the host PC via a **micro-USB data cable**.
- Run `adb devices`; the board's serial number should appear with the status `device`.

---

**Q3b. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024 Model Paper]**
**Describe the deployment of a custom Android Things application using ADB. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Once Android Things is installed and ADB is functional, deploying a custom application involves the following steps:

**Step 1 ‚Äî App Development**
- Open **Android Studio** and create a new project with the **API 27+** target (mandatory for Android Things).
- Add the `com.google.android.things:things` dependency in `build.gradle`:
  $$\texttt{implementation 'com.google.android.things:things:<latest>'}`
- Declare the **launcher activity** in `AndroidManifest.xml` with the intent filter:
  $$\texttt{<category android:name="android.intent.category.IOT\_LAUNCHER"/>}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Building the APK**
- Use **Build ‚Üí Build Bundle(s) / APK(s) ‚Üí Build APK(s)** in Android Studio.
- Locate the generated `app-debug.apk` in the `app/build/outputs/apk/debug/` directory.

**Step 3 ‚Äî Installing the APK via ADB**
- Connect the device using `adb connect <IP>:5555` (wireless) or via USB.
- Install the APK:
  $$\texttt{adb install -r app-debug.apk}$$
  The `-r` flag allows reinstalling over an existing version while preserving app data.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Launching the App**
- Use the Activity Manager to start the main activity:
  $$\texttt{adb shell am start -n com.example.iot/.MainActivity}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Real-Time Debugging**
- View live logs from the device using:
  $$\texttt{adb logcat \vert \text{s } com.example.iot}$$
  This filters logs to only show messages from the deployed app ‚Äî useful for debugging GPIO sensor interactions.

**Step 6 ‚Äî GPIO Peripheral Interaction (IoT-Specific)**
- Use the **Peripheral I/O APIs** (`PeripheralManagerService`) to interface with sensors/actuators connected to the board's GPIO pins, fulfilling the IoT-specific nature of the deployment.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks ‚Äî Alternative Choice)**

**Q4a. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]**
**Compare the installation processes of Android Things on Raspberry Pi 3 and NXP Pico i.MX7D. Highlight at least four differences. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Raspberry Pi 3 | NXP Pico i.MX7D |
|---|---|---|
| **Flashing Method** | Image written to MicroSD card using Etcher / `dd` | Image flashed via `fastboot` using `setup.sh` script |
| **Boot Medium** | Removable MicroSD card | Internal eMMC flash memory |
| **Initial Connection** | USB-to-Serial or direct HDMI for first boot | Requires `fastboot` USB connection to host |
| **Power Source** | 5V/2.5A micro-USB | Powered via the same USB cable used for flashing |
| **Wi-Fi Setup** | `wpa_supplicant.conf` pre-baked in boot partition | Configured post-flash via serial console or `fastboot` |
| **Board Reset** | Power cycle (no hard reset) | `fastboot erase userdata` for factory reset |

**Key Differences Summarized:**

1. **Storage Medium:** RPi3 boots from an *external* SD card; Pico boots from *internal eMMC*.
2. **Flashing Tool:** RPi3 uses generic image writers (Etcher); Pico uses Google's bundled `setup.sh` with `fastboot`.
3. **Provisioning Flow:** RPi3 supports headless Wi-Fi configuration *pre-boot*; Pico often requires a *post-flash* serial console for the first-time setup.
4. **Reset Capability:** Pico supports command-line factory reset; RPi3 requires a re-flash of the SD card.

---

**Q4b. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]**
**Explain the role of ADB in the Android Things ecosystem. List at least five ADB commands used during installation and deployment. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of ADB (Android Debug Bridge):**
ADB is a **command-line tool** that acts as a bridge between the **host development machine** and the **target Android Things device**. It enables file transfers, app installations, shell command execution, and real-time log streaming ‚Äî all critical for IoT development where physical access to the device (e.g., a deployed weather station) is limited.

**Five Essential ADB Commands:**

1. **`adb devices`** ‚Äî Lists all Android Things devices currently connected to the host via USB or Wi-Fi. Confirms successful device detection.

2. **`adb connect <IP>:5555`** ‚Äî Establishes a wireless ADB session with the IoT board over TCP/IP on port 5555, removing the need for a physical USB tether.

3. **`adb install -r <app>.apk`** ‚Äî Installs a custom APK onto the device. The `-r` flag allows reinstalling while preserving user data.

4. **`adb shell am start -n <package>/<activity>`** ‚Äî Launches a specific activity (app screen) on the device from the host terminal.

5. **`adb logcat | grep <tag>`** ‚Äî Streams system and app-specific log messages from the device, enabling real-time debugging of GPIO callbacks and sensor handlers.

**Additional Commands (for high-yield KTU answers):**
- `adb push <local> <remote>` ‚Äî Copy files from host to device.
- `adb pull <remote> <local>` ‚Äî Copy files from device to host.
- `adb reboot` ‚Äî Remotely reboot the IoT board.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Brillo" with "Android Things" as the same name** ‚Äî Brillo was the *codename*; the final product is officially called **Android Things**. Examiners deduct marks for outdated terminology.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the `dd` block size (`bs=4M`)** is a frequent mark-loss point in 7-mark installation questions. Always state the optimization parameter.
> 3. **Confusing `adb` (runtime communication) with `fastboot` (bootloader flashing)** ‚Äî Students often mix these up. Remember: `fastboot` works *before* the OS boots; `adb` works *after*.
> 4. **Omitting the `wpa_supplicant.conf` step** when explaining headless installation ‚Äî this is a top-3 expected answer in KTU question banks.
> 5. **Writing `192.168.0.1:5555` without explaining port 5555** ‚Äî the default ADB port is a high-yield fact worth 1 mark standalone.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **‚ö° Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ‚úÖ **Android Things** = Google's Android-based OS for embedded IoT devices (formerly *Brillo*).
- ‚úÖ Supported boards: **Raspberry Pi 3 Model B**, **NXP i.MX7D Pico**, **NXP i.MX6UL Pico**, **Intel Edison**.
- ‚úÖ Required power for RPi3: **5V / 2.5A** via micro-USB.
- ‚úÖ Minimum MicroSD size: **8 GB, Class 10**.
- ‚úÖ Default ADB TCP port: **5555**.
- ‚úÖ Pre-boot Wi-Fi config file: `wpa_supplicant.conf` placed in the **boot partition**.
- ‚úÖ Flashing tools: **Etcher** (RPi3 GUI) / **`dd` command** (RPi3 CLI) / **`setup.sh` + `fastboot`** (NXP boards).
- ‚úÖ `dd` optimal block size: **4M** (`bs=4M`).
- ‚úÖ **ADB** = runtime communication; **Fastboot** = bootloader-level flashing.
- ‚úÖ `adb install -r` allows **reinstallation** with data preservation.
- ‚úÖ Android Things apps must target **API 27+** and declare the `IOT_LAUNCHER` intent filter.
- ‚úÖ Real-time debugging uses **`adb logcat`** with tag-based filtering.
- ‚úÖ The **Android Things Console** was used for official image distribution (now archived/deprecated for new registrations).
- ‚úÖ **GPIO access** in apps is done via `PeripheralManagerService` ‚Äî a key IoT-specific API.
- ‚úÖ Wireless deployment flow: `adb connect <Board_IP>:5555` ‚Üí `adb install` ‚Üí `adb shell am start`.
- ‚úÖ Always verify with `adb devices` before any install/push/logcat operation.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Verification of Android Things Installation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT604 - MOBILE APPLICATIONS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Verification of Android Things Installation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Android Things** (formerly *Brillo*) is an **Android-based operating system** developed by **Google** specifically for **Internet of Things (IoT)** and embedded devices. It extends the core Android framework with additional APIs (called the **Things Support Library**) that allow developers to integrate with sensors and actuators on supported hardware platforms such as the **Raspberry Pi 3, NXP i.MX7D, and Intel Edison**.

**Verification of Android Things Installation** is the systematic process of confirming that:
- The Android Things image has been **correctly flashed** onto the target IoT hardware.
- The **Android Things Console** has been registered and the device is associated with a valid **Product**.
- The **Weave/Cloud IoT** communication channel is functional.
- The **ADB (Android Debug Bridge)** connection between the host development machine and the target device is established.
- All **peripheral interfaces** (GPIO, I¬≤C, SPI, UART, PWM) and built-in sensors are detectable and operational.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Verification is *not* optional in industrial IoT deployment. According to the PBCIT604 syllabus, students must be able to describe and execute the **post-flashing verification workflow**, including device boot, ADB handshake, peripheral enumeration, and Hello World app deployment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **Android Things installation verification** like **the inspection of a newly constructed smart home before the owner moves in**:

| Smart Home Analogy | Android Things Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Foundation, walls, roof construction | Flashing the Android Things image onto the SD card |
| Electrical wiring check | Verifying GPIO, I¬≤C, SPI, UART pin mappings |
| Plumbing and water flow test | Running the Hello World sample app |
| Wi-Fi router activation & speed test | Connecting the device to **Google Cloud IoT** |
| Final handover checklist | Running `adb devices` and `adb logcat` to confirm everything is green |

Just as a homebuyer would not accept the keys without testing every switch and tap, an IoT developer must not deploy an Android Things device into the field without **end-to-end verification**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - **IoT Device** ‚Äì Hardware running the Android Things image (e.g., Raspberry Pi 3).
> - **Product** ‚Äì A logical grouping registered in the Android Things Console representing a device model.
> - **Peripheral I/O** ‚Äì The set of low-level interfaces (GPIO, I¬≤C, SPI, UART, PWM) used for hardware communication.
> - **Things Support Library** ‚Äì `com.google.android.things:things` ‚Äì The official library providing peripheral access APIs.

## 1.3 Standard Verification Metrics

The following **standard metrics** are used to confirm a successful Android Things installation:

- **Boot Time** ‚Äì typically **30 to 60 seconds** for Raspberry Pi 3.
- **ADB Handshake Latency** ‚Äì should be **< 2 seconds** on a local USB connection.
- **Console Association Status** ‚Äì must display **"Connected"** in the Android Things Console.
- **Peripheral Enumeration Count** ‚Äì All declared peripherals (in `BoardDesc`/`PeripheralManagerService`) must report as **OPEN** without `IOException`.
- **Hello World Log Tag** ‚Äì `HelloThings` must appear in `adb logcat` output.

All of the above are routinely tested in KTU lab exams, and students are expected to **record these values in their practical record**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why Verification is Required

Android Things deployment follows a **three-stage pipeline** that must be verified at each transition:

### Stage 1 ‚Äî Image Preparation
The host machine downloads a **system image** (`.img` file) and a **bootloader** image. These must be verified using **SHA-256 checksum validation** to prevent silent corruption.

### Stage 2 ‚Äî Flashing the Target Device
The images are written to a **microSD card** (for Raspberry Pi 3) using tools like `dd`, **Etcher**, or **Win32DiskImager**. The flash process must complete without I/O errors.

### Stage 3 ‚Äî Post-Boot Configuration
The device connects to the Android Things Console, authenticates using a **device key** generated per Product, and pulls down the user application via OTA (Over-The-Air) delivery.

> [!NOTE]
> Verification is mandatory at **all three stages** because Android Things devices typically have **no display** in production deployments. Without rigorous verification, an undetected failure at any stage will silently brick the device in the field.

## 2.2 Verification Methodology ‚Äî Structured Logic

The verification process can be decomposed into the following seven logical steps:

1. **Pre-Flash Checks** ‚Äî Verify the integrity of the downloaded `.img` file using `sha256sum`; verify the SD card capacity is at least **8 GB**; back up existing data.
2. **Flash Execution** ‚Äî Use `dd` or Etcher to write the image; safely eject the SD card.
3. **Hardware Boot Check** ‚Äî Insert the SD card into the target, power it on, and observe the **green ACT LED** pattern on the Raspberry Pi (a steady blink indicates successful boot).
4. **ADB Connection Check** ‚Äî Connect the device via USB (or Ethernet for headless setups); run `adb devices` to confirm the device appears in the device list with the status `device` (not `unauthorized` or `offline`).
5. **Console Registration Check** ‚Äî Log in to the [Android Things Console](https://partner.android.com/things/console); confirm that the device shows up as **Connected** under the registered Product.
6. **Peripheral Enumeration** ‚Äî Run the **Hello Things** sample (or any sample using `PeripheralManagerService`) to list all detected GPIO, I¬≤C, SPI, and UART buses.
7. **Application Deployment Check** ‚Äî Push a sample app using `adb install` and verify it appears on the **Launcher** screen if a display is attached.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Verification Step | Command / Tool | Expected Output | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Image Integrity | `sha256sum android-things-image.zip` | Matches official SHA-256 | Detect corrupt downloads |
| SD Card Flash | `dd if=image.img of=/dev/sdX bs=4M status=progress` | Bytes written = image size | Write system image |
| Device Discovery | `adb devices` | List of devices with `device` status | Confirm USB/Network ADB |
| Log Capture | `adb logcat -d` | Boot logs without `FATAL EXCEPTION` | Detect runtime errors |
| Reboot | `adb reboot` | Device restarts cleanly | Test firmware stability |
| Shell Access | `adb shell` | `$` prompt appears | Confirm root access |
| Peripheral Test | `PeripheralManagerService.getUartList()` | Non-empty list of buses | Verify hardware detection |
| Console Check | Console UI | "Connected" status | Confirm cloud handshake |
| App Install | `adb install -r app.apk` | `Success` | Confirm APK deploy |
| Wi-Fi / Ethernet | `adb shell ifconfig wlan0` | Valid IP address | Network verification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU 14-mark questions**, students are expected to write at least **8‚Äì10 distinct verification commands** in tabular form. Avoid repeating commands like `adb devices` more than once.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Android Things verification is a **mandatory quality gate** in the following real-world engineering workflows:

- **Industrial Automation** ‚Äî Factory sensor gateways running Android Things must be verified before being placed on the production line to prevent costly recalls.
- **Smart Retail** ‚Äî Digital signage and inventory robots use Android Things; a failure during installation can halt checkout operations.
- **Smart Home Hubs** ‚Äî Devices such as the original Google Home prototypes were based on Android Things; verification ensures cloud connectivity.
- **Academic & Research IoT** ‚Äî Engineering students in KTU-affiliated colleges use Android Things for capstone projects on **environmental monitoring**, **agriculture automation**, and **health wearables**.

## 2.5 Common Failure Modes (KTU Exam Favorite)

The most common points of failure during Android Things installation are:

- **Corrupted image** ‚Üí `Fastboot` or boot loop on the device.
- **Wrong baud rate** on serial console ‚Üí No boot logs visible.
- **Insufficient SD card speed** (Class 4 or lower) ‚Üí Slow boot and random crashes.
- **Firewall blocking port 5555** ‚Üí `adb devices` returns `offline`.
- **Outdated `platform-tools`** ‚Üí `adb` cannot recognize the device.
- **Missing OEM USB drivers** (on Windows) ‚Üí Device never appears in device manager.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Process & Analytical Matrix

This section presents the **complete procedural verification workflow** in a strict step-by-step format suitable for KTU 14-mark descriptive questions. Each step includes the **trigger condition**, the **command sequence**, the **expected output**, and the **failure analysis**.

## 3.1 Step 1 ‚Äî Host Machine Preparation

**Trigger:** Before downloading the Android Things image.

**Command Sequence:**

```bash
# Step 1.1 ‚Äî Verify available disk space
df -h /tmp

# Step 1.2 ‚Äî Verify ADB is installed
adb version
```

**Expected Output:**

- Available disk space ‚â• **4 GB** (image size + workspace).
- `adb version` returns `Android Debug Bridge version 1.0.41` or later.

**Failure Analysis:**

- If disk space is insufficient, the image download will abort mid-way and the resulting file will be **truncated and unusable**.
- If `adb` is missing, install the **Android SDK Platform Tools** before proceeding.

## 3.2 Step 2 ‚Äî Image Download and SHA-256 Verification

**Trigger:** Image has finished downloading.

**Command Sequence:**

```bash
# Step 2.1 ‚Äî Compute the SHA-256 hash
sha256sum androidthings_rpi3_devpreview_zip

# Step 2.2 ‚Äî Compare with the official hash published by Google
```

**Expected Output:**

- The computed hash must **exactly match** the official hash published on the Android Things download page.
- Any mismatch indicates **bit-level corruption** that will lead to a bricked device.

**Analytical Note:** A **single flipped bit** in the bootloader region of the image can prevent the device from booting. SHA-256 verification is therefore **non-negotiable** in industrial deployments.

## 3.3 Step 3 ‚Äî Flashing the microSD Card

**Trigger:** Image verification complete.

**Command Sequence (Linux):**

```bash
# Step 3.1 ‚Äî Identify the SD card device node
lsblk

# Step 3.2 ‚Äî Unmount the card if mounted
umount /dev/sdX1

# Step 3.3 ‚Äî Flash the image
sudo dd if=androidthings_rpi3.img of=/dev/sdX bs=4M status=progress conv=fsync
```

**Expected Output:**

- The progress bar reaches **100%** and `dd` exits with no I/O errors.
- The total bytes written equals the image size (typically **~480 MB** for preview builds).

**Failure Analysis:**

- If `dd` reports `No space left on device`, the SD card is too small. Use a card of at least **8 GB**.
- If the write completes but the device does not boot, the card may be a **counterfeit low-speed card**. Replace it with a Class 10 or UHS-I card from a reputable brand.

## 3.4 Step 4 ‚Äî Hardware Boot Check

**Trigger:** SD card inserted and device powered on.

**Observation Procedure:**

1. Insert the flashed SD card into the Raspberry Pi 3.
2. Connect the **5V / 2.5A** power supply.
3. Observe the **green ACT LED** on the board.

**Expected Output:**

- The green LED should **blink irregularly** for the first **30‚Äì60 seconds**, indicating kernel and system server initialization.
- The LED should then settle into a **heartbeat pattern** (regular short blinks) once the system is fully booted.

**Failure Analysis:**

- **No LED activity** ‚Üí Power supply insufficient or SD card unreadable.
- **Solid green LED** ‚Üí Kernel panic; reflash the card.
- **Repeated blink pattern of 3 long + 3 short** ‚Üí `start.elf` or bootloader corruption.

## 3.5 Step 5 ‚Äî ADB Connection Establishment

**Trigger:** Device has fully booted.

**Command Sequence:**

```bash
# Step 5.1 ‚Äî Connect via USB (default) or via TCP/IP for headless setup
adb connect <device_ip>:5555

# Step 5.2 ‚Äî List connected devices
adb devices

# Step 5.3 ‚Äî Open an interactive shell
adb shell
```

**Expected Output:**

- `adb devices` should return:

```
List of devices attached
192.168.1.42:5555    device
```

- `adb shell` should drop the user into a `$` prompt.

**Failure Analysis:**

- Status `unauthorized` ‚Üí Tap **"Always allow from this computer"** on the device screen (if a display is attached) or re-add the ADB key.
- Status `offline` ‚Üí The ADB daemon on the device is not running; reboot and retry.
- Device not listed ‚Üí USB cable is charge-only, or the OEM driver is missing on Windows.

## 3.6 Step 6 ‚Äî Console Registration Verification

**Trigger:** ADB is functional.

**Procedure:**

1. Open a browser and navigate to **https://partner.android.com/things/console**.
2. Sign in with the Google account that owns the **Product**.
3. Select the **Product** created earlier.
4. Locate the **Device ID** (obtained via `adb shell settings get global device_id` on the device).
5. Confirm the device appears in the **Devices** list with status **"Connected"**.

**Expected Output:**

- A green **"Connected"** indicator next to the device entry.
- The **Last Activity** timestamp should be within the last few minutes.

**Failure Analysis:**

- If status is **"Disconnected"**, the device cannot reach `cloud-iot.googleapis.com` on port **443**. Check firewall rules and DNS resolution.
- If the device is not listed at all, the **Product Key** has not been flashed onto the device. Re-burn the image with the correct key.

## 3.7 Step 7 ‚Äî Peripheral Enumeration via Hello Things Sample

**Trigger:** All prior steps have passed.

**Command Sequence:**

```bash
# Step 7.1 ‚Äî Build the sample app
./gradlew :hello-things:installDebug

# Step 7.2 ‚Äî Push and install the APK
adb install -r app/build/outputs/apk/debug/app-debug.apk

# Step 7.3 ‚Äî Launch the activity
adb shell am start -n com.example.iot.hellothings/.MainActivity

# Step 7.4 ‚Äî Capture the logs
adb logcat -s HelloThings
```

**Expected Output:**

- `adb logcat` should display the list of available peripherals, for example:

```
HelloThings: GPIO: [BCM6, BCM16, BCM17, BCM22, BCM23, BCM24, BCM25, BCM26, BCM27]
HelloThings: I2C: [/dev/i2c-1]
HelloThings: SPI: [/dev/spidev0.0, /dev/spidev0.1]
HelloThings: UART: [/dev/ttyAMA0, /dev/ttyS0]
HelloThings: PWM: [PWM0, PWM1]
```

**Failure Analysis:**

- Empty peripheral list ‚Üí The Things Support Library is not loaded; verify the manifest contains `<uses-library android:name="com.google.android.things"/>`.
- `IOException: Permission denied` ‚Üí The app is missing the `android.permission.ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION` permission required on Android Things for some peripheral operations.

## 3.8 Step 8 ‚Äî Network Connectivity Verification

**Trigger:** All peripheral tests pass.

**Command Sequence:**

```bash
# Step 8.1 ‚Äî Display network interfaces
adb shell ifconfig

# Step 8.2 ‚Äî Ping a public DNS server
adb shell ping -c 4 8.8.8.8

# Step 8.3 ‚Äî Verify DNS resolution
adb shell nslookup google.com
```

**Expected Output:**

- `wlan0` (or `eth0`) must have a valid **IPv4 address** in the local subnet.
- All four ping packets must return with **0% packet loss**.
- DNS resolution must return a valid IP address.

**Failure Analysis:**

- `ping: network is unreachable` ‚Üí The interface is not configured. Run `adb shell ifconfig wlan0 up` and reconnect to Wi-Fi via the console or `am start` of the setup activity.
- DNS failure despite valid IP ‚Üí The DNS server is unreachable. Reconfigure the DNS via `setprop net.dns1 8.8.8.8`.

## 3.9 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Verification Outcomes

The following analytical matrix maps each verification step to its **pass criterion**, **failure symptom**, and **recommended corrective action**. This table is a **high-yield KTU exam artifact**.

| # | Verification Step | Pass Criterion | Failure Symptom | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SHA-256 of image | Matches official hash | Hash mismatch | Re-download image |
| 2 | SD card flash | `dd` completes with 0 errors | I/O error | Replace SD card |
| 3 | Hardware boot | Green LED heartbeat | Solid / no LED | Check power & card |
| 4 | ADB device list | Device shown as `device` | `offline` / `unauthorized` | Re-authorize / reboot |
| 5 | Console association | Status = Connected | Disconnected | Check network & key |
| 6 | Peripheral list | Non-empty list | Empty list | Check manifest |
| 7 | Network ping | 0% loss | 100% loss | Reconfigure interface |
| 8 | Sample app launch | Activity appears in logcat | `FATAL EXCEPTION` | Fix app code |
| 9 | OTA app delivery | App auto-installed | App missing | Re-trigger OTA push |
| 10 | Final reboot | Clean boot | Boot loop | Re-flash image |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU viva voce, examiners frequently ask: *"What is the first thing you do after flashing the SD card?"* The correct answer is **"Insert it into the device, power it on, and observe the LED boot pattern"**, followed by **"Connect via ADB and verify the device appears in the console"**.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 ‚Äî End-to-End Android Things Verification Flow

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **complete sequential verification workflow** that an engineer follows from the moment the image is downloaded to the moment the device is declared "production-ready."

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Download Android Things Image] --> B[Verify SHA-256 Hash]
    B --> C{Hash Matches?}
    C -- No --> B1[Re-download Image] --> B
    C -- Yes --> D[Flash Image to microSD Card]
    D --> E[Insert SD Card into Target Device]
    E --> F[Power On Device]
    F --> G{LED Heartbeat Pattern?}
    G -- No --> G1[Check Power Supply and SD Card] --> D
    G -- Yes --> H[Connect Host PC to Device via USB or Ethernet]
    H --> I[Run adb devices]
    I --> J{Device Listed as device?}
    J -- No --> J1[Re-authorize ADB or Install OEM Drivers] --> H
    J -- Yes --> K[Open Android Things Console]
    K --> L{Device Status = Connected?}
    L -- No --> L1[Verify Network and Product Key] --> H
    L -- Yes --> M[Deploy Hello Things Sample]
    M --> N[Enumerate Peripherals via PeripheralManagerService]
    N --> O{All Peripherals Detected?}
    O -- No --> O1[Check Manifest and Wiring] --> M
    O -- Yes --> P[Verify Network Connectivity via ping]
    P --> Q{Ping Successful?}
    Q -- No --> Q1[Reconfigure Network Interface] --> P
    Q -- Yes --> R[Verify OTA App Delivery]
    R --> S{App Auto-Installed?}
    S -- No --> S1[Trigger OTA Push from Console] --> R
    S -- Yes --> T[Final Reboot Test]
    T --> U{Device Boots Cleanly?}
    U -- No --> U1[Reflash Image] --> D
    U -- Yes --> V[End: Device Production-Ready]
```
