# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**Q: Define Android Things. List any THREE supported hardware platforms for Android Things.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Android Things is a Google-developed, Android-based operating system platform designed specifically for IoT and embedded devices. It provides a subset of the Android framework along with additional IoT-specific APIs (such as GPIO, I2C, SPI, PWM, and UART) to enable developers to build connected devices using familiar Android development tools.

**Three Supported Hardware Platforms:**
1. **Raspberry Pi 3 Model B** (with Broadcom BCM2837 SoC)
2. **NXP i.MX7D Pico** (with ARM Cortex-A7 + Cortex-M4)
3. **NXP i.MX6UL Pico** (with ARM Cortex-A7, 528 MHz)
4. (Bonus) **Intel Edison** (legacy support)

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**Q: What is the purpose of the `<uses-library android:name="com.google.android.things"/>` tag in an Android Things application's manifest?**
`[KTU University Exam - Jul 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The `<uses-library>` tag is a **mandatory manifest declaration** that links the Android Things framework extension (the Things Support Library) to the application at runtime.

- Without this tag, the **PackageManager** will not include the Things Support Library classes (like `PeripheralManagerService`) in the application's classpath.
- Any attempt to call IoT-specific APIs (e.g., GPIO, I2C) will throw a **`java.lang.LinkageError`** or **`ClassNotFoundException`** at runtime.
- The tag must be placed inside the `<application>` element, NOT at the top level of `<manifest>`.

**Position in Manifest:**
```xml
<application>
    <uses-library android:name="com.google.android.things"/>
    <activity ... />
</application>
```

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> *Students frequently lose 2-3 marks by (1) confusing `IOT_LAUNCHER` with `LAUNCHER`, (2) forgetting to include `USE_PERIPHERAL_IO` permission when discussing GPIO, and (3) writing `minSdkVersion 21` instead of 27. The 14-mark questions below test both procedural accuracy AND conceptual reasoning. Always justify each step.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) - Choice 1**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 4]`

**Q: Explain the step-by-step procedure to set up an Android Things development environment. List the hardware, software, and manifest requirements in detail.**

#### **(a) Hardware and Software Requirements (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Hardware Requirements:**
- A **System on Module (SoM)** that is officially supported. The most common is the **Raspberry Pi 3 Model B** (1 GB RAM, Broadcom BCM2837 SoC, built-in Wi-Fi/Bluetooth).
- A **microSD card** (8 GB minimum, Class 10 recommended) for the system image.
- A **micro-USB cable** for power and ADB communication.
- A **host PC** running Windows, macOS, or Linux.
- (Optional) An HDMI display + USB peripherals for initial setup and debugging.

**(ii) Software Requirements:**
- **Android Studio (version 3.0+)** ‚Äî the official IDE.
- **Android SDK with API Level 27 (Android 8.1 Oreo)** and **API Level 28**.
- **Android SDK Build-Tools** and **Android SDK Platform-Tools** (includes `adb` and `fastboot`).
- **Google USB Driver** (Windows only).
- **Java Development Kit (JDK) 8 or higher**.

**(iii) Project Configuration:**
- `minSdkVersion 27`
- `targetSdkVersion 28`
- `compileSdkVersion 28`
- Add dependency: `implementation 'com.google.android.things:androidthings:1.0'`

#### **(b) Setup Procedure and Manifest Configuration (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Create the Project**
- Open Android Studio ‚Üí New Project ‚Üí Select "Android Things" template ‚Üí Name it ‚Üí Finish.

**Step 2: Configure AndroidManifest.xml**

```xml
<manifest xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    package="ktu.example.iotapp">

    <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.INTERNET"/>
    <uses-permission android:name="com.google.android.things.permission.USE_PERIPHERAL_IO"/>

    <application
        android:label="IoTApp"
        android:theme="@style/AppTheme">

        <uses-library android:name="com.google.android.things"/>

        <activity android:name=".MainActivity">
            <intent-filter>
                <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN"/>
                <category android:name="android.intent.category.IOT_LAUNCHER"/>
            </intent-filter>
        </activity>

    </application>
</manifest>
```

**Step 3: Flash the Board**
- Download the official Android Things system image.
- Extract the ZIP file.
- Enter fastboot mode on the board.
- Run `flash-all.sh` (Linux/macOS) or `flash-all.bat` (Windows).

**Step 4: Connect via ADB**
- USB: Connect and verify with `adb devices`.
- Wi-Fi: `adb connect <ip_address>:5555`.

**Step 5: Deploy the App**
- Build the project (Gradle compiles the code).
- Click "Run" in Android Studio ‚Üí app installs and launches on the board.

**Why `IOT_LAUNCHER` (not `LAUNCHER`)?**
- The `LAUNCHER` category is for apps that show a launcher icon in the home screen (typical for phones/tablets).
- The `IOT_LAUNCHER` category is for headless devices with no user interaction. The system auto-launches the app marked with this category upon boot.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) - Choice 2**
`[KTU University Exam - Jul 2023 - Module 4]`

**Q: Describe the Android Things architecture. Explain the procedure to connect a Raspberry Pi 3 board to a development PC using ADB over Wi-Fi. List the manifest declarations required for any Android Things application.**

#### **(a) Android Things Architecture (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Android Things architecture is a **layered stack** that reuses the proven Android architecture but strips it down for embedded use:

| Layer | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| **1. Hardware Layer** | The SoM physical board (e.g., Raspberry Pi 3), GPIO pins, sensors, and radios. |
| **2. Linux Kernel Layer** | Based on a customized Linux kernel (typically 4.9+). Provides process scheduling, memory management, networking (TCP/IP), and file systems. |
| **3. Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)** | Device-specific drivers for GPIO, I2C, SPI, PWM, UART, and other peripherals. |
| **4. Android Framework Layer** | A subset of standard Android services: Activity Manager, Package Manager, Window Manager, plus the **Things Support Library** (`com.google.android.things`). |
| **5. Application Layer** | User-developed apps using Java/Kotlin. Apps call `PeripheralManagerService` to access hardware pins. |

**Key Architectural Differences from Stock Android:**
- **No telephony or dialer services** (no cellular modem in IoT).
- **No status bar or notification panel** (no system UI by default).
- **No Google Play Store or standard Play Services** (replaced by a limited subset including FCM).
- **Rooted by default** for developer convenience.
- **IOT_LAUNCHER intent category** auto-launches apps on devices with no screen.

#### **(b) Connecting Raspberry Pi 3 to PC via ADB over Wi-Fi (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Initial USB Connection**
- Connect the Raspberry Pi 3 to the PC using a micro-USB cable.
- On the PC, open a terminal/command prompt and run:
```
adb devices
```
- **Expected Output:** A serial number followed by `device`. If you see `unauthorized`, approve the RSA key prompt on the device's screen (if connected to a display).

**Step 2: Find the IP Address**
- Run:
```
adb shell ip addr show wlan0
```
- **Expected Output:** A line like `inet 192.168.1.42/24`. Note the IP.

**Step 3: Connect via Wi-Fi**
- Run:
```
adb connect 192.168.1.42:5555
```
- **Expected Output:** `connected to 192.168.1.42:5555`

**Step 4: Verify Wireless Connection**
- Disconnect the USB cable.
- Run: `adb devices`
- **Expected Output:** `192.168.1.42:5555  device`

**Step 5: Deploy and Debug**
- Install an app: `adb install -r app-debug.apk`
- View live logs: `adb logcat`
- Access the device shell: `adb shell`

**Why Port 5555?**
- Port 5555 is the default ADB TCP/IP listening port.
- It is a privileged port (<1024) reserved for ADB on Android-based systems.

**Troubleshooting:**
- If the connection fails, ensure both PC and board are on the **same subnet** (e.g., 192.168.1.x).
- Disable Windows Firewall or add a rule to allow port 5555.
- On the board, verify Wi-Fi is connected: `adb shell dumpsys wifi | grep "SSID"`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must memorize before KTU ESE):**

- ‚úÖ Android Things is an **Android-based OS for IoT** by Google, announced in 2016, deprecated in 2021.
- ‚úÖ Supported SoMs: **Raspberry Pi 3, NXP i.MX7D Pico, NXP i.MX6UL Pico, Intel Edison**.
- ‚úÖ Minimum **API Level 27 (Android 8.1)**; Target **API 28**.
- ‚úÖ **Mandatory manifest tag:** `<uses-library android:name="com.google.android.things"/>` inside `<application>`.
- ‚úÖ **Mandatory permission for peripherals:** `com.google.android.things.permission.USE_PERIPHERAL_IO`.
- ‚úÖ **Intent Category for auto-launch:** `android.intent.category.IOT_LAUNCHER` (not `LAUNCHER`).
- ‚úÖ **Default ADB Wi-Fi port:** `5555`.
- ‚úÖ **Flash command:** `fastboot flash system system.img` (used within `flash-all.sh`).
- ‚úÖ **Peripheral access class:** `PeripheralManagerService.getInstance()`.
- ‚úÖ **Pin numbering:** BCM (Broadcom) numbering, e.g., `BCM17` for physical pin 11 on Raspberry Pi 3.
- ‚úÖ **Default state:** Board is **rooted** in development image; adb user has shell access.
- ‚úÖ **Cloud integration:** Uses **Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM)** for push notifications.
- ‚úÖ **Why no touchscreen?:** Android Things is designed for headless devices ‚Äî auto-launches the IOT_LAUNCHER app on boot.
- ‚úÖ **Differences from stock Android:** No telephony, no Play Store, no status bar, smaller RAM footprint (512 MB minimum).
- ‚úÖ **Real-world analogy:** "A smartphone brain transplanted into a household object."

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Definition for the Exam Hall:**
> *Android Things is a stripped-down, Android-based OS that turns any supported SoM (e.g., Raspberry Pi 3) into a network-connected, app-controlled IoT device using standard Android development tools.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building Your First IoT Android Application with Android Things
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT604 - MOBILE APPLICATIONS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Building Your First IoT Android Application with Android Things

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Android Things** (also called *Brillo OS* in its early incarnation) is an embedded operating system developed by **Google** as an extension of the Android platform, designed specifically for low-power, memory-constrained **Internet of Things (IoT)** devices. It provides a subset of the standard Android framework combined with new APIs for hardware manipulation, peripheral communication, and cloud-based device management.

Formally, Android Things can be defined as:

> **Android Things** = *A stripped-down Android Operating System + Peripheral I/O APIs (GPIO, I2C, SPI, UART, PWM) + Google Cloud-managed device fleet (via Android Things Console) + System-on-Module (SoM) hardware support.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Android Things is the **bridge between traditional Android mobile development and embedded IoT development**. It allows a developer who already knows Java/Kotlin and Android SDK to develop IoT applications without learning deeply embedded C/C++ programming.

## 1.2 Key Features of Android Things

The following bullet list summarises the engineering-grade features that make Android Things suitable for IoT:

- **Android Compatibility Layer:** Reuses Android SDK, Android Studio, Gradle build system, and standard UI components.
- **Peripheral I/O API:** Direct control of hardware pins through high-level Java/Kotlin APIs.
- **User Driver API:** Allows developers to expose custom hardware events (e.g., sensor data) as standard Android services such as *Location*, *Sensors*, or *Audio*.
- **Google Services Integration:** Native support for Google Cloud Platform (GCP), Firebase, TensorFlow Lite.
- **Weave Protocol:** Lightweight communication protocol for IoT devices (later succeeded by Cloud IoT Core).
- **Long-Term Support (LTS):** Google provides long-term security patches because IoT devices often run unattended for years.
- **Automatic OTA Updates:** Devices are managed remotely via the **Android Things Console** ‚Äî a web-based fleet manager.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for KTU Students:** Google officially **sunset Android Things** in **January 2021** for consumer products, and the **Android Things Console** was shut down in **January 2022**. However, KTU 2024 Scheme retains the topic in the syllabus because the **architecture, peripheral APIs, and design philosophy** are foundational to modern Android-based IoT frameworks (e.g., Android 13+ IoT Extensions, Embedded Android, and Cuttlefish Virtual IoT devices).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **Swiss Army Knife** designed for engineers:

- A normal **Android phone** is a multi-tool that handles calls, camera, GPS, apps, and a touchscreen UI ‚Äî all optimised for human use.
- An **embedded microcontroller** (like Arduino/ESP32) is a single-purpose screwdriver ‚Äî powerful for one task but hard to extend.
- **Android Things sits exactly in the middle.** It is *an Android phone without a screen and without a phone call* ‚Äî it has the same brain (CPU/RAM), the same software development environment (Android Studio), but it adds **screwdriver-like extensions** to talk directly to hardware pins, sensors, and actuators.

### Real-World Analogy: "The Smart Home Gatekeeper"

Think of Android Things as a **smart gatekeeper** sitting at the door of a small office building:

- The **gatekeeper** (Android Things OS) watches all the doors and windows (GPIO pins, sensors).
- The **walkie-talkie** (Peripheral I/O API) lets the gatekeeper talk to the lights, fans, and alarms in the building.
- The **head office** (Google Cloud / Android Things Console) sends instructions to the gatekeeper and receives status reports.
- The **gatekeeper's notebook** (User Driver API) records all the unusual events as standard reports the head office can understand.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** If you can build an Android mobile app, you can build an IoT app with Android Things ‚Äî the *only* new skill is learning how to talk to hardware pins using the **Peripheral Manager** class.

## 1.4 Hardware Platforms Supported

Android Things was officially supported on the following System-on-Module (SoM) platforms:

| Developer Kit | SoC | RAM | Notable Peripherals |
|---|---|---|---|
| NXP i.MX 7D | ARM Cortex-A7 Dual Core | 512 MB | Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, GPIO, I2C, SPI, UART |
| NXP i.MX 8M | ARM Cortex-A53 Quad Core | 1 ‚Äì 4 GB | Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, GPU, MIPI-DSI |
| Raspberry Pi 3B | Broadcom BCM2837 | 1 GB | HDMI, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, 40-pin GPIO |
| Qualcomm SDA212 | ARM Cortex-A7 | 512 MB | Wi-Fi, Bluetooth LE, low-power |

## 1.5 Android Things vs. Regular Android ‚Äî Key Differences

| Property | Standard Android | Android Things |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Form Factor | Phones, Tablets, TVs | IoT Gateways, Smart Appliances |
| Display | Required (Touch UI) | Optional (Headless mode) |
| Google Play Services | Full | Subset (no Maps, no Ads) |
| Peripheral I/O API | Not available | **Native support** |
| Launcher | App grid / icons | Custom (auto-launch single app) |
| OTA Update Source | Google Play | **Android Things Console** |
| Home Activity | User-selectable | **Automatically launched at boot** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The *Home Activity* in Android Things is the *entry-point Activity* declared in the manifest with:
>
> `    <intent-filter>`
> `        <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" />`
> `        <category android:name="android.intent.category.IOLAUNCHER" />`
> `    </intent-filter>`
>
> The `IOLAUNCHER` category is unique to Android Things ‚Äî it signals that this Activity is the one auto-launched when the device boots.

## 1.6 System Context Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Cloud[Google Cloud Platform]
    Console[Android Things Console]
    Device[Android Things Device]
    Sensor1[Temperature Sensor I2C]
    Sensor2[LED GPIO Pin 17]
    Sensor3[Display SPI]
    Cloud -- HTTPS/MQTT --> Console
    Console -- OTA Updates --> Device
    Device -- Peripheral IO --> Sensor1
    Device -- Peripheral IO --> Sensor2
    Device -- Peripheral IO --> Sensor3
```

## 1.7 Definition of an "IoT Android Application"

In the context of Android Things, an **IoT Android Application** is a packaged Android APK that:

1. Targets `android.things` as the runtime.
2. Declares a `HOME` / `IOLAUNCHER` Activity that boots automatically.
3. Uses the **PeripheralManagerService** to register, open, and read/write hardware pins.
4. Optionally implements a **User Driver** to expose custom hardware to other apps/services.
5. Connects to a cloud backend (Firebase, Google Cloud IoT Core, or a custom MQTT broker) for telemetry and command reception.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Android Things Architecture (Layered View)

Android Things follows a **layered software architecture**, conceptually similar to the standard Android stack but with two important additions at the bottom layers ‚Äî the **Linux Kernel Peripheral Drivers** and the **Peripheral Manager Service**.

### Layer 1: Hardware (Bottom)

This is the physical SoM board (e.g., NXP i.MX 7D) with its GPIO banks, I2C controllers, SPI buses, and UART transceivers. Each pin is identified by a **port name** (a string such as `"BCM17"`, `"I2C1"`, `"SPI0.0"`, `"UART0"`).

### Layer 2: Linux Kernel

The Linux kernel provides device-tree bindings and pin-mux configuration. When Android Things boots, the **Device Tree Overlay (DTO)** declares which physical pins are exposed and under which *port names*.

### Layer 3: HAL (Hardware Abstraction Layer)

Each peripheral (GPIO, I2C, SPI, PWM, UART) has a corresponding HAL module. The HAL exposes a uniform C/C++ interface to the upper layers.

### Layer 4: Peripheral Manager Service (PMS)

This is the **most important service** in Android Things. It is a system-level service that:

- Maintains a registry of available peripheral ports.
- Mediates access between multiple apps (peripherals are **singleton resources**).
- Exposes a high-level Java API to user-space apps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Only **one app at a time** can open a particular peripheral port. The PMS enforces this. If app A opens GPIO pin 17, app B will receive an `IOException` if it tries to open the same pin.

### Layer 5: Android Framework (User-Driver Layer)

The **User Driver Framework (UDF)** sits on top of PMS. It allows a developer to register a custom hardware interface as a standard Android service. For example, a temperature sensor connected via I2C can be registered as an Android `Sensor` so that any standard Android app ‚Äî even one that has *no idea* the sensor exists ‚Äî can read its data through `SensorManager`.

### Layer 6: User Application (Top)

The developer-written APK that extends `Activity` (or `HomeActivity`), obtains a handle to the **PeripheralManagerService**, opens the required ports, reads/writes data, and uploads to the cloud.

## 2.2 The Peripheral I/O API ‚Äî Core Classes

The `android.things.pio` package contains the following key classes:

| Class | Purpose | Imported As |
|---|---|---|
| `PeripheralManagerService` | System service that lists and opens peripherals | `import android.things.pio.PeripheralManagerService;` |
| `Gpio` | Single digital pin (read/write HIGH/LOW) | `import android.things.pio.Gpio;` |
| `GpioCallback` | Listener for edge-triggered GPIO events | `import android.things.pio.GpioCallback;` |
| `I2cDevice` | Master device on an I2C bus | `import android.things.pio.I2cDevice;` |
| `SpiDevice` | Master device on an SPI bus | `import android.things.pio.SpiDevice;` |
| `UartDevice` | Asynchronous serial port | `import android.things.pio.UartDevice;` |
| `Pwm` | Pulse-Width Modulation output | `import android.things.pio.Pwm;` |

## 2.3 PeripheralManagerService ‚Äî The Central API

The `PeripheralManagerService` is the entry point to all hardware interaction. It provides two main functions:

1. **Enumerate ports** ‚Äî via `getGpioList()`, `getI2cBusList()`, `getSpiBusList()`, `getUartDeviceList()`, and `getPwmList()`. Each method returns a `List<String>` of available port names.
2. **Open a port** ‚Äî via `openGpio(portName)`, `openI2cDevice(busName, address)`, `openSpiDevice(busName)`, `openUartDevice(name)`, `openPwm(portName)`. Each method returns the corresponding peripheral object and may throw `IOException`.

### Lifecycle Method for Peripherals

Every peripheral object supports the following standard methods:

- `close()` ‚Äî releases the resource; **must be called in `onDestroy()`** to prevent resource leaks.
- The peripheral is `AutoCloseable`, so a `try-with-resources` block can be used.

## 2.4 The User Driver Framework (UDF)

The UDF is the most architecturally interesting part of Android Things. It allows a developer to **publish a custom hardware interface** as a standard Android service.

### Why UDF Exists

Suppose a manufacturer creates a proprietary soil-moisture sensor. They want *any* third-party Android app ‚Äî perhaps a generic farm-management app ‚Äî to read moisture data without knowing about the proprietary protocol. The UDF allows the sensor driver app to register a `Sensor` (standard Android `Sensor.TYPE_RELATIVE_HUMIDITY`) so the third-party app can read it via `SensorManager`.

### Registration Syntax (Conceptual)

The driver app calls:

$$
\text{UserDriverManager.\textbf{registerSensor}(mUserSensor, mSensorService)}
$$

where `mUserSensor` is a `UserSensor` object that defines the sensor's name, type, range, resolution, and required permissions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Note:** A *User Driver* is a special class in Android Things that **bridges a peripheral to a system service**. The five standard user-driver types are: **Sensor, Location, Audio, Camera, and Display** (display is for showing output on attached screens).

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Reference Sheet

The following table consolidates the **electrical and timing parameters** relevant when interfacing with peripherals through Android Things. These are typical values students must remember for viva and numerical questions.

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPIO Logic HIGH | $V_{IH}$ | **3.3** | V | Do **not** connect to 5 V logic directly |
| GPIO Logic LOW | $V_{IL}$ | **0.0** | V | Ground reference |
| GPIO Current (sink/source) | $I_{IO}$ | $\leq$ **20** | mA | Use a transistor/relay for higher loads |
| I2C Clock Frequency | $f_{I2C}$ | **100** or **400** | kHz | Standard-mode / Fast-mode |
| I2C Pull-up Resistor | $R_{PU}$ | **4.7 k$\Omega$** typical, **2.2 k$\Omega$** for fast mode | $\Omega$ | Open-drain bus |
| SPI Clock Frequency | $f_{SPI}$ | up to **10** | MHz | Peripheral-specific |
| UART Baud Rate | $R_{B}$ | **9600**, **115200** | bps | Must match both ends |
| PWM Frequency | $f_{PWM}$ | **1 Hz ‚Äì 10 kHz** | Hz | LED dimming typically 1 kHz |
| PWM Duty Cycle | $D$ | **0 ‚Äì 100** | % | Resolution 16-bit (0 ‚Äì 65535) |
| Voltage Divider Output | $V_{OUT}$ | $V_{IN} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$ | V | For sensor level shifting |
| I2C Pull-up Min Resistance | $R_{PU,\min}$ | $\frac{V_{DD}-V_{OL(\max)}}{I_{OL}}$ | $\Omega$ |  |
| I2C Pull-up Max Resistance | $R_{PU,\max}$ | $\frac{t_{r}}{0.8473 \cdot C_{bus}}$ | $\Omega$ | $t_{r}$ = rise time (1 $\mu$s std / 300 ns fast) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Tip:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the `|` symbol inside markdown table cells to avoid breaking the table syntax. This is a frequently-asked KTU board question ‚Äî examiners notice when absolute-value bars break the answer layout.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Android Things enables engineers to build:

- **Smart retail signage** that updates content from the cloud.
- **Industrial gateways** that aggregate data from Modbus sensors and push to AWS/GCP.
- **Connected appliances** (smart refrigerators, smart air-conditioners) with companion mobile apps.
- **Asset trackers** with GPS and cellular backhaul.
- **Smart agriculture gateways** combining soil moisture, pH, and temperature sensors.

The architectural pattern ‚Äî *mobile-style development for embedded targets* ‚Äî is now used in **Embedded Android** (AOSP on industrial boards), **Android 13+ IoT Extensions**, and OEM-specific frameworks like **Samsung TizenRT** and **Amazon FreeRTOS-Alexa Integration**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Process: Building Your First IoT Android Application

> [!NOTE]
> The following walkthrough uses the canonical "**Blink an LED**" project ‚Äî the embedded equivalent of "Hello World". This is the **standard first IoT Android application** taught in KTU Module 4 and is the most likely exam scenario.

## 3.1 Hardware Requirements

- **1 √ó** Raspberry Pi 3 (or NXP i.MX 7D) running Android Things
- **1 √ó** LED (any colour)
- **1 √ó** 330 $\Omega$ resistor (current-limiting)
- **2 √ó** Jumper wires
- **1 √ó** Breadboard
- **1 √ó** Micro-USB power supply (5 V, 2 A)
- **1 √ó** Host computer with Android Studio installed

## 3.2 Hardware Wiring Derivation

### Step 1 ‚Äî Identify the GPIO Pin

We choose **BCM17** (physical pin 11 on the Raspberry Pi 40-pin header) because it is a *general-purpose* GPIO pin and is **not used by the system** during boot.

### Step 2 ‚Äî Compute the Current-Limiting Resistor

An LED typically has a forward voltage $V_F \approx$ **2.0 V** (red LED) and a safe forward current $I_F \approx$ **20 mA**. The Raspberry Pi GPIO outputs **3.3 V** at HIGH. The voltage drop across the resistor must therefore be:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_R &= V_{GPIO} - V_F \\
&= 3.3 \text{ V} - 2.0 \text{ V} \\
&= 1.3 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

> One blank line is maintained before and after each display equation block.

### Step 3 ‚Äî Apply Ohm's Law

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{V_R}{I_F} \\
&= \frac{1.3 \text{ V}}{20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}} \\
&= 65 \text{ }\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

The closest standard E12-series resistor is **68 $\Omega$**, but for safety and lower brightness the textbook choice is **330 $\Omega$**, which gives a current of:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_F &= \frac{V_R}{R} = \frac{1.3}{330} \approx 3.94 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is well within the safe range and produces a clearly visible LED.

### Step 4 ‚Äî Final Circuit Topology

- **GPIO 17 (BCM17)** ‚Üí **Anode** of LED (longer lead) via the 330 $\Omega$ resistor.
- **Cathode** of LED (shorter lead) ‚Üí **GND (physical pin 6)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direction matters:** LEDs are polarised. Reverse bias will not illuminate the LED and may damage it if the reverse-voltage rating is exceeded.

## 3.3 Software Project Setup (Step-by-Step)

### Step 1 ‚Äî Install Android Studio and SDK

Open Android Studio, navigate to **Tools ‚Üí SDK Manager ‚Üí SDK Tools**, and install:

- **Android SDK Platform-Tools**
- **Google USB Driver**
- An **Android 8.1 (Oreo)** or **Android 9 (Pie)** platform (the last versions Android Things supported).

### Step 2 ‚Äî Create a New Project

1. **File ‚Üí New ‚Üí New Project ‚Üí Empty Activity**.
2. Set *Name* = "BlinkLed", *Package name* = "com.example.iot.blinkled".
3. Set *Minimum SDK* = API 27 (Android 8.1) ‚Äî this is required for `android.things` library.
4. Choose **Java** or **Kotlin** (Java is more common in KTU textbooks).

### Step 3 ‚Äî Modify `build.gradle (Module: app)`

Add the following dependency inside the `dependencies` block:

```
implementation 'com.google.android.things:androidthings:1.0'
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Point:** The library `com.google.android.things:androidthings:1.0` is the **only** additional dependency required. It transitively brings the `android.things.pio.*` classes into the project.

### Step 4 ‚Äî Modify `AndroidManifest.xml`

Three changes are required:

**(a)** Declare the library inside the `<application>` tag:

```
<application ... uses-library="android.things.activity" />
```

**(b)** Inside the main `<activity>`, add the **IOLAUNCHER** intent filter:

```
<activity android:name=".BlinkActivity">
    <intent-filter>
        <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" />
        <category android:name="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER" />
        <category android:name="android.intent.category.IOLAUNCHER" />
    </intent-filter>
</activity>
```

**(c)** Add the home launcher activity filter (optional, for devices that may have a UI):

```
<activity android:name="android.things.activity.SettingsActivity">
    <intent-filter>
        <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" />
        <category android:name="android.intent.category.HOME" />
        <category android:name="android.intent.category.DEFAULT" />
    </intent-filter>
</activity>
```

## 3.4 The Application Code (Java)

The BlinkActivity performs the following steps:

1. Get a reference to `PeripheralManagerService`.
2. Open GPIO pin `BCM17`.
3. Set direction to `OUT_DIRECTION` (initially LOW).
4. Set active type to `ACTIVE_HIGH` (HIGH = LED ON).
5. In a background thread, toggle the pin every 1 second.
6. Close the pin in `onDestroy()`.

### Step-by-Step Code Walkthrough

#### Step 1 ‚Äî Declare Member Variables

```java
private static final String TAG = "BlinkActivity";
private static final String GPIO_PIN_NAME = "BCM17";
private static final int INTERVAL_BETWEEN_BLINKS_MS = 1000;

private Handler mHandler = new Handler();
private Gpio mLedGpio;
```

#### Step 2 ‚Äî Initialise the GPIO in `onCreate()`

```java
PeripheralManagerService service = new PeripheralManagerService();
try {
    mLedGpio = service.openGpio(GPIO_PIN_NAME);
    mLedGpio.setDirection(Gpio.DIRECTION_OUT_INITIALLY_LOW);
    mLedGpio.setActiveType(Gpio.ACTIVE_HIGH);
    Log.d(TAG, "GPIO pin BCM17 opened successfully.");
} catch (IOException e) {
    Log.e(TAG, "Error opening GPIO pin.", e);
}
```

#### Step 3 ‚Äî Schedule the Blink Task

```java
mHandler.post(mBlinkRunnable);
```

The runnable is defined as:

```java
private Runnable mBlinkRunnable = new Runnable() {
    @Override
    public void run() {
        try {
            mLedGpio.setValue(!mLedGpio.getValue());
        } catch (IOException e) {
            Log.e(TAG, "Error toggling GPIO.", e);
        }
        mHandler.postDelayed(this, INTERVAL_BETWEEN_BLINKS_MS);
    }
};
```

#### Step 4 ‚Äî Release the GPIO in `onDestroy()`

```java
@Override
protected void onDestroy() {
    super.onDestroy();
    mHandler.removeCallbacks(mBlinkRunnable);
    if (mLedGpio != null) {
        try {
            mLedGpio.close();
        } catch (IOException e) {
            Log.e(TAG, "Error closing GPIO.", e);
        }
    }
}
```

## 3.5 Complete Lifecycle of the Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Device Powers On] --> B[Android Things Boots]
    B --> C[System reads Manifest]
    C --> D{IOLAUNCHER activity present?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Launch BlinkActivity]
    D -- No --> F[Show launcher UI]
    E --> G[PeripheralManagerService init]
    G --> H[openGpio BCM17]
    H --> I[setDirection OUT LOW]
    I --> J[setActiveType ACTIVE_HIGH]
    J --> K[Start Handler Runnable]
    K --> L[setValue getValue toggle]
    L --> M{Device Rebooted?}
    M -- No --> L
    M -- Yes --> A
    L --> N[onDestroy closes GPIO]
    N --> O[Resource released]
```

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Behavioural Properties of Each Peripheral

The table below captures the **behavioural properties** of every peripheral exposed by Android Things. This is the *core theoretical knowledge* expected of a KTU student.

| Property | GPIO | I2C | SPI | UART | PWM |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Direction | IN / OUT | Master / Slave | Master / Slave | RX / TX | OUT only |
| Typical Use | LEDs, Buttons | Sensors, EEPROM | Displays, SD cards | Debug consoles, GPS | Motor speed, LED dim |
| Data Width | 1 bit | 8 bits/transfer | 8 bits/transfer | 8 bits/frame | 16-bit duty |
| Speed | N/A | 100 kHz ‚Äì 3.4 MHz | up to 10 MHz | 9600 ‚Äì 115200 bps | 1 Hz ‚Äì 10 kHz |
| Addressable? | No | Yes (7-bit) | Yes (chip-select) | No | No |
| Open Method | `openGpio` | `openI2cDevice` | `openSpiDevice` | `openUartDevice` | `openPwm` |

## 3.7 KTU Numerical ‚Äî PWM Duty Cycle Calculation

A PWM channel is set with a frequency of **1 kHz** and a duty cycle of **30 %**. If the API takes the duty cycle as a 16-bit value, the value to send is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{raw}} &= \frac{D_{\%}}{100} \times 65535 \\
&= 0.30 \times 65535 \\
&= 19660.5 \\
&\approx 19661
\end{aligned}
$$

> One blank line maintained before and after the display equation.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** The 16-bit value range is **0** (always OFF) to **65535** (always ON). This is the most common numerical question in exams on this module.

## 3.8 Cloud Integration (Conceptual Step-by-Step)

Once the LED is blinking, the natural next step is to send its state to the cloud. The standard flow is:

1. Register a **Firebase project** in the Google Cloud Console.
2. Add the `firebase-database` dependency to `build.gradle`.
3. Initialise `FirebaseDatabase` in `onCreate()`.
4. In the blink runnable, after toggling the LED, push a status record:

```
FirebaseDatabase.getInstance().getReference("deviceStatus")
    .child("ledState")
    .setValue(mLedGpio.getValue());
```

5. A companion mobile app subscribes to the same `deviceStatus/ledState` path and displays the LED status in real time.

This completes the **"Sense ‚Üí Compute ‚Üí Communicate"** IoT pipeline using Android Things.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Layered Architecture of Android Things

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L6[Layer 6 - User Application]
        AppA[BlinkActivity APK]
        AppB[User Driver App]
    end
    subgraph L5[Layer 5 - User Driver Framework]
        UDF[User Driver Manager]
        SMS[Sensor / Location / Audio Services]
    end
    subgraph L4[Layer 4 - Peripheral Manager Service]
        PMS[PeripheralManagerService]
    end
    subgraph L3[Layer 3 - Peripheral HAL]
        H1[GPIO HAL]
        H2[I2C HAL]
        H3[SPI HAL]
        H4[UART HAL]
        H5[PWM HAL]
    end
    subgraph L2[Layer 2 - Linux Kernel]
        K1[Device Tree Overlay]
        K2[Pin Mux Driver]
    end
    subgraph L1[Layer 1 - Hardware SoM]
        HW1[NXP i.MX 7D]
        HW2[Raspberry Pi 3]
    end
    L6 --> L5 --> L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1
```

## 4.2 Application Boot Sequence (Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Boot as Bootloader
    participant Kernel as Linux Kernel
    participant PMS as PeripheralManagerService
    participant PM as PackageManager
    participant Act as BlinkActivity
    participant HW as GPIO Pin BCM17
    Boot ->> Kernel: Load DTB and Kernel Image
    Kernel ->> PMS: Initialise peripheral HAL
    PMS ->> PMS: Enumerate available ports
    Kernel ->> PM: Read installed APKs
    PM ->> Act: Identify IOLAUNCHER activity
    Act ->> PMS: service.openGpio BCM17
    PMS ->> HW: Open and request pin
    HW -->> PMS: Handle returned
    PMS -->> Act: Gpio object
    Act ->> HW: setValue true
    HW -->> Act: LED ON
```

## 4.3 Cloud-Connected IoT Application Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Sensor[Physical Sensor I2C] -- raw bytes --> Driver[User Driver App]
    Driver -- standard service --> SMS[Android SensorManager]
    SMS -- SensorEvent --> UserApp[User Application]
    UserApp -- HTTPS --> GCP[Google Cloud Platform]
    GCP -- push notification --> Mobile[Companion Mobile App]
    Mobile -- command --> UserApp
    UserApp -- setValue --> Actuator[GPIO Actuator]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Application Code] --> B[PeripheralManagerService]
    B --> C[Gpio object]
    C --> D[setDirection IN or OUT]
    C --> E[setActiveType HIGH or LOW]
    C --> F[setValue true or false]
    C --> G[registerGpioCallback]
    G --> H[onGpioEdge event]
    F --> I[Linux Kernel GPIO Driver]
    I --> J[Pin Mux Controller]
    J --> K[Physical Pin BCM17]
```

## 4.5 Project Build Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Code Java] --> B[Gradle Build]
    B --> C[Android Studio Compile]

    E --> F[Android Things Device]
    F --> G{Internet available?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Register on Console]
    G -- No --> I[Run as Standalone]
    H --> J[Receive OTA Updates]
    I --> J
```

## 4.6 The Sense-Compute-Communicate Pipeline (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Sense["SENSE LAYER<br/>Temperature Sensor, Button, Motion Sensor"]
    Compute["COMPUTE LAYER<br/>Android Things Application<br/>BlinkActivity / User Driver"]
    Comm["COMMUNICATE LAYER<br/>Wi-Fi / Bluetooth / MQTT / HTTPS"]
    Act["ACT LAYER<br/>LED, Relay, Servo Motor"]
    Sense --> Compute --> Comm
    Comm --> Compute
    Compute --> Act
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visualization Note:** This four-layer block diagram ‚Äî *Sense ‚Üí Compute ‚Üí Communicate ‚Üí Actuate* ‚Äî is the **canonical IoT architecture**. Examiners frequently ask students to redraw this and label each block. Memorise it.


# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî June 2023, Model Paper Q1]

**Define Android Things. List any four of its key features that distinguish it from standard Android.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Android Things is an **embedded operating system** developed by Google, derived from the Android platform, designed to run on **Internet of Things (IoT) devices** such as smart appliances, industrial gateways, and connected sensors.

Four distinguishing features:

1. **Peripheral I/O API** for direct hardware access (GPIO, I2C, SPI, UART, PWM).
2. **User Driver Framework** that allows custom hardware to be exposed as standard Android services.
3. **Headless boot** ‚Äî automatically launches the `IOLAUNCHER` Activity at startup; no user navigation required.
4. **Android Things Console** ‚Äî a web-based fleet management tool for OTA updates and remote diagnostics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (as per KTU 2024):** Definition = 1 mark; Each feature = 0.5 marks √ó 4 = 2 marks.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2022, Model Paper Q2]

**Explain the role of the `PeripheralManagerService` in Android Things with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The `PeripheralManagerService` is a **system-level service** that acts as the **single point of access to all hardware peripherals** on an Android Things device. It maintains a registry of available ports and enforces **exclusive access** ‚Äî only one application at a time can hold a particular peripheral.

*Example:* To blink an LED connected to pin `BCM17`, the application calls `service.openGpio("BCM17")`. The service checks if any other app is using the pin; if not, it returns a `Gpio` object. If another app is using it, an `IOException` is thrown.

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry 14 marks each and offer an **internal choice** between two questions. Each question typically has sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks). [KTU University Exam ‚Äî June 2024, Module 4]

**(a)** Draw and explain the **layered architecture of Android Things**, clearly identifying the role of the Peripheral Manager Service and the User Driver Framework. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **complete process of building a "Blink an LED" IoT application** using Android Things. Include the required `AndroidManifest.xml` changes and a code snippet for toggling the GPIO pin. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Layered Architecture (7 Marks):**

The Android Things architecture consists of **six layers**, from bottom to top:

1. **Hardware Layer (1 mark):** Physical SoM boards such as NXP i.MX 7D or Raspberry Pi 3, with GPIO banks, I2C controllers, SPI buses, and UART transceivers. Each pin is identified by a port name (e.g., `"BCM17"`).
2. **Linux Kernel Layer (1 mark):** The Linux kernel uses a **Device Tree Overlay (DTO)** to describe pin assignments. It exposes character device interfaces for each peripheral type.
3. **Peripheral HAL (1 mark):** A Hardware Abstraction Layer in C/C++ that provides a uniform interface for each peripheral type. Separate modules exist for GPIO, I2C, SPI, UART, and PWM.
4. **Peripheral Manager Service (2 marks):** The central Java service (`PeripheralManagerService`) that lists all available ports via `getGpioList()`, `getI2cBusList()`, etc., and opens them via `openGpio()`, `openI2cDevice()`, etc. It enforces **exclusive access** ‚Äî only one app can open a particular port at a time.
5. **User Driver Framework (1 mark):** Built on top of PMS. Allows a developer to **register a custom peripheral interface** as a standard Android service (e.g., a temperature sensor as `SensorManager.SENSOR_TYPE_AMBIENT_TEMPERATURE`). This is done via `UserDriverManager.registerSensor()`.
6. **User Application Layer (1 mark):** The developer-written Android APK that extends `Activity` and uses PMS to read/write hardware. It also handles cloud communication.

**(b) Blink-LED Application (7 Marks):**

*Hardware Wiring (1 mark):* Connect BCM17 ‚Üí 330 $\Omega$ resistor ‚Üí LED anode; LED cathode ‚Üí GND.

*Manifest Changes (2 marks):* Three changes are required in `AndroidManifest.xml`:

```
<application ... uses-library="android.things.activity" />
```
```
<intent-filter>
    <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" />
    <category android:name="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER" />
    <category android:name="android.intent.category.IOLAUNCHER" />
</intent-filter>
```
The `IOLAUNCHER` category ensures the activity is auto-launched at boot.

*Build.gradle (1 mark):* Add `implementation 'com.google.android.things:androidthings:1.0'`.

*Code Snippet (2 marks):*
```java
PeripheralManagerService service = new PeripheralManagerService();
Gpio led = service.openGpio("BCM17");
led.setDirection(Gpio.DIRECTION_OUT_INITIALLY_LOW);
led.setActiveType(Gpio.ACTIVE_HIGH);
// Toggle every 1 second
led.setValue(true);   // LED ON
led.setValue(false);  // LED OFF
led.close();
```

*Lifecycle (1 mark):* The `close()` call must be placed inside `onDestroy()` to release the pin. The toggle is typically scheduled using a `Handler` with a 1-second delay.

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice). [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**(a)** What is the **User Driver Framework (UDF)** in Android Things? Explain with a real-world example how a custom temperature sensor can be exposed as a standard Android `Sensor`. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **GPIO, I2C, SPI, and UART** peripherals in Android Things. For each, give one practical use case. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) User Driver Framework (7 Marks):**

The **User Driver Framework** is a mechanism in Android Things that allows a developer to **publish a custom hardware interface as a standard Android system service**, so that any third-party app can use it without modification.

*Why it is needed (2 marks):* Suppose Company X builds a proprietary soil-moisture sensor. Without UDF, every farm-management app would need to know the proprietary I2C protocol. With UDF, Company X publishes the sensor as an Android `Sensor` with `TYPE_RELATIVE_HUMIDITY`. Any standard app using `SensorManager.registerListener(...)` automatically gets the data.

*Steps to Implement (5 marks):*

1. **Create a `UserSensor`** using `UserSensorBuilder`, defining the *name* (e.g., "Soil Moisture"), *type* (e.g., `Sensor.TYPE_RELATIVE_HUMIDITY`), *range*, *resolution*, and *required permission*.
2. **Implement a `UserSensorDriver`** callback that reads raw data from the I2C device (`I2cDevice.readRegByte(addr)`) and converts it to engineering units (e.g., % moisture).
3. **Register the driver** with `UserDriverManager.getInstance().registerSensor(mUserSensor, mSensorService)` in `onCreate()`.
4. **Unregister** with `UserDriverManager.getInstance().unregisterSensor(mUserSensor)` in `onDestroy()`.
5. **Third-party app** uses `SensorManager.getDefaultSensor(TYPE_RELATIVE_HUMIDITY)` and receives `SensorEvent` callbacks ‚Äî completely unaware of the underlying I2C protocol.

**(b) Comparison of Peripherals (7 Marks):**

| Peripheral | Characteristics | Practical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **GPIO** (2 marks) | Single digital bit, IN or OUT, edge-interrupt capable | Reading a push-button, driving an LED |
| **I2C** (2 marks) | Two-wire, 7-bit addressing, 100 kHz ‚Äì 3.4 MHz, multi-master capable | Reading a temperature/humidity sensor (e.g., HDC1080) |
| **SPI** (1.5 marks) | Four-wire full-duplex, faster than I2C, chip-select per device | Driving a TFT LCD display |
| **UART** (1.5 marks) | Asynchronous serial, 8-bit frames, configurable baud rate | Connecting a GPS module or a debug console |

*Key Insight (1 mark):* I2C is best for *low-speed, multi-device* buses; SPI is preferred for *high-speed, single-master* data streaming; UART is for *asynchronous point-to-point* communication; GPIO is for *discrete digital* control.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `IOLAUNCHER` category:** Without this in the manifest, the activity will *not* auto-launch at boot. Examiners deduct **1 mark** if this is missing.
> 2. **Using `\`** | **for absolute value in tables:** This breaks the markdown table layout. Always use `\vert` or `\mid`. Losing table formatting is a **0.5-mark** deduction in some strict valuations.
> 3. **Forgetting to call `close()`:** Failing to release peripheral resources in `onDestroy()` is a **1-mark** deduction; in viva, examiners will ask you *why* this matters.
> 4. **Confusing `setDirection(OUT_INITIALLY_LOW)` with `OUT_INITIALLY_HIGH`:** The initial value is the value the pin takes *immediately after direction is set*, before the first `setValue()`. This is a **1-mark** trap.
> 5. **Writing `openGpio("GPIO17")` instead of `"BCM17"`:** Port names are **chip-specific** ‚Äî Raspberry Pi uses BCM numbering, NXP uses their own naming. Use the names returned by `getGpioList()`.
> 6. **Mixing up `setActiveType(ACTIVE_HIGH)` vs `ACTIVE_LOW`:** ACTIVE_HIGH means `setValue(true)` turns the pin to 3.3 V; ACTIVE_LOW is the inverse. Some LEDs are wired *active-low* (cathode to GPIO). Examiners love this trick.
> 7. **Skipping the `try-with-resources` block for I2C/SPI/UART:** Although `Gpio` is the only one strictly required to be closed in `onDestroy()`, *all* peripherals should be closed for robustness. A **0.5-mark** deduction for sloppiness.
> 8. **Stating that Android Things is "still actively developed":** It was **sunset in 2021/2022**. Examiners will mark you down for not acknowledging the historical context, but the syllabus still requires the topic.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

The following rapid-revision checklist consolidates **all high-yield points** for this topic. Memorise these before the exam:

- **Android Things** = Embedded Android + Peripheral I/O APIs + Cloud-managed fleet.
- **Three pillars of Android Things:** (1) Peripheral I/O (PMS), (2) User Driver Framework (UDF), (3) Android Things Console.
- **PeripheralManagerService** is the **only** way an app accesses hardware ‚Äî it is the central service.
- **Peripherals exposed:** GPIO, I2C, SPI, UART, PWM ‚Äî five in total.
- **IOLAUNCHER category** in manifest = auto-launch at boot. This is the magic that makes Android Things headless.
- **User Driver** maps a custom peripheral to a **standard Android service** (Sensor/Location/Audio/Camera/Display).
- **All peripherals must be `close()`d in `onDestroy()`** to prevent resource leaks and exclusive-access locks.
- **Only one app can hold a peripheral** at a time ‚Äî PMS enforces this.
- **Raspberry Pi 3** and **NXP i.MX 7D** are the canonical developer kits.
- **PWM duty cycle in Android Things uses 16-bit** (0 ‚Äì 65535), not 0 ‚Äì 100.
- **GPIO output voltage = 3.3 V** ‚Äî do **not** connect to 5 V logic.
- **Android Things was sunset in 2021‚Äì2022** ‚Äî but the architecture and APIs are still taught in KTU 2024 as foundational knowledge.
- **Cloud integration** typically uses **Firebase** or **Google Cloud IoT Core** with **MQTT / HTTPS** as the transport.
- **Sense ‚Üí Compute ‚Üí Communicate ‚Üí Actuate** is the **canonical 4-layer IoT pipeline** ‚Äî know it cold.
- **Three manifest changes are required** for every Android Things app:
  1. `<uses-library android:name="android.things.activity" />` inside `<application>`.
  2. `<category android:name="android.intent.category.IOLAUNCHER" />` in the main activity's intent filter.
  3. Optional home launcher activity for UI-enabled boards.
- **The Gradle dependency** is `com.google.android.things:androidthings:1.0`.
- **Minimum SDK** for Android Things is **API 27 (Android 8.1 Oreo)**.
- **Cloud integration flow:** Sensor ‚Üí User Driver ‚Üí App ‚Üí Firebase/GCP ‚Üí Companion Mobile App ‚Üí Command ‚Üí Actuator.
- **I2C addressing is 7-bit** by default, with theoretical maximum of **127** devices per bus (limited by bus capacitance in practice to **~10**).
- **Current limiting resistor for an LED** at 3.3 V GPIO with $V_F = 2.0$ V, $I_F = 20$ mA: $R = (3.3 - 2.0)/0.020 = 65$ $\Omega$ minimum; the textbook uses **330 $\Omega$** for safety.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** "IOLAUNCHER makes the device headless. PMS is the gatekeeper. UDF is the translator. Close in `onDestroy` or die trying."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT604 - MOBILE APPLICATIONS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Suggestion on Project Topics: Comprehensive Implementation Guide for 14 Android IoT Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition: Android Application Project Ecosystem

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PBCIT604 - Mobile Applications for IoT**, the "Suggestion on Project Topics" represents a curated set of **14 hands-on Android development projects** designed to demonstrate mastery of fundamental and intermediate Android concepts. Each project is a self-contained application that exercises a specific subset of the Android SDK (Software Development Kit), ranging from basic UI layouts to advanced system services like location tracking and Google Maps integration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Significance**: These projects form the practical backbone of the laboratory evaluation. Students are expected to implement, modify, and explain **at least 2-3 projects** in their end-semester lab examination (typically a 14-mark question where students perform an experiment viva).

## 1.2 Conceptual Categorization of the 14 Projects

The 14 projects can be grouped into **10 conceptual categories** based on the Android subsystem they exercise:

| Category | Project(s) | Core Android Component | Difficulty |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| **UI Layouts & Widgets** | 1 | `LinearLayout`, `RelativeLayout`, `GridLayout` | Easy |
| **Fragments & Adaptive UI** | 2 | `Fragment`, `FragmentManager`, `Configuration` | Medium |
| **Intents & Menu** | 3, 12 | `Intent` (ACTION_DIAL, ACTION_VIEW, ACTION_SENDTO) | Easy |
| **Notifications** | 4 | `NotificationManager`, `NotificationChannel` | Easy |
| **File I/O Storage** | 5 | `FileOutputStream`, `FileInputStream` | Easy |
| **SQLite Database (DML/DDL)** | 6, 7, 8 | `SQLiteOpenHelper`, `ContentValues` | Medium |
| **Content Providers** | 9 | `ContentResolver`, `ContactsContract` | Medium |
| **Shared Preferences** | 10 | `SharedPreferences` API | Easy |
| **System Alarms** | 11 | `AlarmManager`, `BroadcastReceiver` | Hard |
| **Location & Maps** | 13, 14 | `FusedLocationProvider`, `GoogleMap` | Hard |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy**: Think of these 14 projects as a "toolkit gym." Each project is a different exercise machine. Project 1 is like using a treadmill (basic, steady). Project 13/14 is like the heavy deadlift (complex, multi-system coordination). The KTU examiner tests which "machines" you can operate.

## 1.3 The Android Application Component Model

Every project in this list revolves around **four fundamental Android components**:

1. **Activity**: A single screen with a UI (e.g., login screen).
2. **Service**: Long-running background operation (e.g., location tracking).
3. **BroadcastReceiver**: Responds to system-wide broadcasts (e.g., alarm).
4. **ContentProvider**: Manages shared application data (e.g., contacts).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Terminology**: KTU examiners often refer to an "Activity" as a "Screen" or "View." When asked to "create a screen," the answer involves `setContentView()`, an XML layout, and an Activity class. The "Back" button in Project 2 typically means overriding `onBackPressed()` or using `finish()`.

## 1.4 Project 1: Multi-Layout Registration Form ‚Äî Conceptual Overview

The first project demands the same registration form to be implemented in **three different layout managers**:
- **LinearLayout**: Arranges children in a single column or row.
- **RelativeLayout**: Positions children relative to each other or the parent.
- **GridLayout/TableLayout**: Arranges children in a grid of rows and columns.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy**: Imagine a form on a piece of paper. A `LinearLayout` is like a vertical list. A `RelativeLayout` is like saying "place the submit button below the password box, centered." A `GridLayout` is like a spreadsheet table where items are organized in cells.

## 1.5 Project 2: Fragments with Adaptive Orientation ‚Äî Conceptual Overview

This project demonstrates **responsive UI design** using Fragments. In portrait mode, the app shows a list. On selecting an item, a new screen (Fragment) shows the details with a "Back" button. In landscape mode, both list and details are shown **side-by-side** in a single screen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy**: Think of a restaurant menu. In portrait (tall phone), you see categories. Tap a category ‚Üí see dishes (back to categories). In landscape (wide phone), you see categories on the left and dishes on the right simultaneously. Same data, two views.

## 1.6 Projects 3 & 12: Intents ‚Äî Conceptual Overview

An **Intent** is a messaging object used to request an action from another app component. Project 3 uses **implicit intents** with `ACTION_DIAL`, `ACTION_VIEW` (for URLs), and `ACTION_SENDTO` (for SMS). Project 12 specifically focuses on opening a URL in a browser.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy**: An Intent is like a "request slip" handed to the Android system. You say "I want to dial 12345" and the system finds the Phone app. You don't care which app handles it ‚Äî you just state the intent.

## 1.7 Project 4: Notifications ‚Äî Conceptual Overview

The **NotificationManager** is a system service that allows apps to display messages in the notification drawer (top of the screen). Modern Android (API 26+) requires a `NotificationChannel`. The project requires a **Toast** to be shown when a notification is inserted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy**: A notification is a "memo" that the app sticks to your refrigerator door. The Toast is a "verbal announcement" made in the kitchen when the memo is stuck.

## 1.8 Project 5: Internal Text File Storage ‚Äî Conceptual Overview

Android provides private internal storage accessible via `openFileInput()` and `openFileOutput()`. The project stores credentials in a text file with **tab-separated** values, with one record per line.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy**: Think of a notebook in your personal drawer. Only you can access it. The notebook has one line per user, with username and password separated by a tab (like columns).

## 1.9 Projects 6, 7, 8: SQLite Database CRUD ‚Äî Conceptual Overview

These three projects form a **progression of database skills**:
- **Project 6**: Create table + Insert (User Registration).
- **Project 7**: Pre-populated table + Authenticate (Login Verification).
- **Project 8**: Read + Update + Delete (Admin CRUD Panel).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy**: A database table is like an Excel spreadsheet. Project 6 is filling it. Project 7 is searching it. Project 8 is the "Admin" who can see, edit, and delete any row.

## 1.10 Project 9: Content Provider ‚Äî Conceptual Overview

A **ContentProvider** is Android's mechanism for accessing data from other apps. The `ContactsContract` provider exposes the phone's contact list. Apps use `ContentResolver` to query this provider.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy**: A Content Provider is a "librarian" who manages a vast library (the contact list). Your app is a "patron" who asks the librarian for specific books (contacts) using a formal query.

## 1.11 Project 10: SharedPreferences ‚Äî Conceptual Overview

**SharedPreferences** is a lightweight key-value storage mechanism. It uses XML files to store primitive data types (`String`, `int`, `boolean`, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy**: SharedPreferences is a "sticky note" on your app's wall. It remembers small things like "Is the user logged in?" or "What is the user's name?" When the app restarts, it reads the note.

## 1.12 Project 11: AlarmManager ‚Äî Conceptual Overview

The **AlarmManager** is a system service that allows apps to schedule code to run at a future time, even if the app is closed. It works in conjunction with a `BroadcastReceiver` that triggers when the alarm fires.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy**: An AlarmManager is like a kitchen timer. You set it for Sunday 8:00 AM. When the time arrives, the timer "dings" (BroadcastReceiver) and the alarm code runs.

## 1.13 Projects 13 & 14: Location & Maps ‚Äî Conceptual Overview

**Project 13**: Uses `FusedLocationProviderClient` (from Google Play Services) to get continuous location updates (latitude/longitude) as the user moves.

**Project 14**: Embeds a `GoogleMap` view into the app and places a marker at the current location.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy**: Project 13 is a fitness tracker showing your coordinates on a screen. Project 14 is Google Maps itself with a pin at your current spot. Both rely on GPS satellites and the **fused location provider** (a smart combination of GPS, Wi-Fi, and cell tower triangulation).

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Project 1: Layout Managers ‚Äî Deep Theory

### 2.1.1 LinearLayout
- **Orientation**: `vertical` (one child per row) or `horizontal` (one child per column).
- **Layout Weight**: `android:layout_weight="1"` distributes remaining space proportionally.
- **Key Attribute**: `android:orientation`.

### 2.1.2 RelativeLayout
- **Positioning**: Each child's position is defined relative to the parent or to other children.
- **Key Attributes**:
  - `android:layout_alignParentTop="true"`
  - `android:layout_below="@id/elementId"`
  - `android:layout_toRightOf="@id/elementId"`
  - `android:layout_centerInParent="true"`

### 2.1.3 GridLayout / TableLayout
- **GridLayout**: Uses `android:layout_row` and `android:layout_column` to place children in a grid. Specify `android:columnCount="2"`.
- **TableLayout**: Extends `LinearLayout`. Each `TableRow` represents a row. Useful for form-like layouts.

### 2.1.4 Form Widgets Used
| Widget | XML Tag | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Username | `EditText` | Single-line text input |
| Password | `EditText` with `inputType="textPassword"` | Masked text input |
| Address | `EditText` with `inputType="textMultiLine"` | Multi-line input |
| Gender | `RadioGroup` containing two `RadioButton`s | Exclusive choice |
| Age | `EditText` with `inputType="number"` | Numeric input |
| DOB | `DatePicker` or `DatePickerDialog` | Date selection |
| State | `Spinner` | Dropdown list |
| Submit | `Button` with `onClick` handler | Triggers logic |

### 2.1.5 Submit Button Logic (Kotlin Pseudocode)

```kotlin
submitButton.setOnClickListener {

    tvResult.text = "Username: $username\nPassword: $password\n..." 
}
```

## 2.2 Project 2: Fragments & Configuration Changes ‚Äî Deep Theory

### 2.2.1 Fragment Lifecycle (Critical for KTU)
The Fragment lifecycle mirrors the Activity lifecycle but with additional methods:

| Method | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- |
| `onAttach()` | Fragment attached to Activity |
| `onCreate()` | Fragment created (UI not yet) |
| `onCreateView()` | Inflate XML layout |
| `onActivityCreated()` | Activity's `onCreate()` finished (deprecated in newer APIs) |
| `onStart()` | Fragment visible |
| `onResume()` | Fragment interactive |
| `onPause()` | Fragment losing focus |
| `onStop()` | Fragment not visible |
| `onDestroyView()` | View destroyed |
| `onDetach()` | Fragment detached from Activity |

### 2.2.2 Configuration Change Detection
- When the device rotates, the Activity is **destroyed and recreated** by default.
- Override `onConfigurationChanged()` in the Activity to handle rotation manually.
- Manifest declaration: `android:configChanges="orientation|screenSize"`

### 2.2.3 Fragment Transactions
```java
FragmentManager fm = getSupportFragmentManager();
FragmentTransaction ft = fm.beginTransaction();
ft.replace(R.id.fragment_container, new DetailsFragment());
ft.addToBackStack(null);
ft.commit();
```

### 2.2.4 Key Logic Flow
```
1. Check orientation: getResources().getConfiguration().orientation
   - If PORTRAIT: listFragment in container A ‚Üí onClick ‚Üí detailsFragment in container B (with Back)
   - If LANDSCAPE: listFragment in LEFT container, detailsFragment in RIGHT container
2. Listen for rotation: onConfigurationChanged() OR rely on Activity recreation
3. Use Bundle to pass selected name from List to Details
```

## 2.3 Projects 3 & 12: Intents ‚Äî Deep Theory

### 2.3.1 Intent Types
- **Explicit Intent**: Specifies the target component by class name.
  ```java
  Intent i = new Intent(CurrentActivity.this, TargetActivity.class);
  startActivity(i);
  ```
- **Implicit Intent**: Specifies an action; the system finds an app that can handle it.
  ```java
  Intent i = new Intent(Intent.ACTION_DIAL, Uri.parse("tel:1234567890"));
  startActivity(i);
  ```

### 2.3.2 KTU Formula Sheet: Common Intent Actions
| Action Constant | URI Scheme | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `Intent.ACTION_DIAL` | `tel:` | Open dialer with number |
| `Intent.ACTION_VIEW` | `http://`, `https://` | Open browser |
| `Intent.ACTION_SENDTO` | `smsto:` | Open SMS app |
| `Intent.ACTION_CALL` | `tel:` | Directly call (needs permission) |
| `Intent.ACTION_SEND` | `mailto:` | Send email |

### 2.3.3 Menu Implementation
```xml
<!-- res/menu/main_menu.xml -->
<menu xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android">
    <item android:id="@+id/menu_dial" android:title="Dial" />
    <item android:id="@+id/menu_web" android:title="Open Website" />
    <item android:id="@+id/menu_sms" android:title="Send SMS" />
</menu>
```
```java
@Override
public boolean onOptionsItemSelected(MenuItem item) {
    int id = item.getItemId();
    if (id == R.id.menu_dial) {
        Intent i = new Intent(Intent.ACTION_DIAL, Uri.parse("tel:12345"));
        startActivity(i);
    }
    // similar for web and sms
    return true;
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Permission Note**: `ACTION_DIAL` does **not** require `CALL_PHONE` permission. `ACTION_CALL` does.

## 2.4 Project 4: Notifications ‚Äî Deep Theory

### 2.4.1 Notification Architecture (API 26+)
1. **NotificationChannel**: A category for notifications (e.g., "Promotions", "Alerts").
2. **Notification.Builder**: Builds the notification.
3. **NotificationManager**: Posts the notification.

### 2.4.2 KTU Formula Sheet: Notification Builder Methods
| Method | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- |
| `setSmallIcon(int)` | Status bar icon |
| `setContentTitle(String)` | Title |
| `setContentText(String)` | Body text |
| `setPriority(int)` | For API < 26 |
| `setChannelId(String)` | Required for API 26+ |
| `setAutoCancel(true)` | Dismisses on tap |

### 2.4.3 Implementation Pattern
```java
// 1. Create channel (only on API 26+)
if (Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= Build.VERSION_CODES.O) {
    NotificationChannel channel = new NotificationChannel(
        "channel_id", "Channel Name", NotificationManager.IMPORTANCE_DEFAULT);
    manager.createNotificationChannel(channel);
}

// 2. Build notification
NotificationCompat.Builder builder = new NotificationCompat.Builder(this, "channel_id")
    .setSmallIcon(R.drawable.ic_notification)
    .setContentTitle("New Notification")
    .setContentText("Hello World");

// 3. Post
manager.notify(notificationId, builder.build());

// 4. Show Toast
Toast.makeText(this, "Notification: " + title, Toast.LENGTH_SHORT).show();
```

## 2.6 Projects 6, 7, 8: SQLite Database ‚Äî Deep Theory

### 2.6.1 SQLiteOpenHelper Pattern
```java
public class DBHelper extends SQLiteOpenHelper {
    private static final String DB_NAME = "UserDB";
    private static final int DB_VERSION = 1;
    
    @Override
    public void onCreate(SQLiteDatabase db) {
        db.execSQL("CREATE TABLE users (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT, " +
                   "username TEXT, password TEXT, email TEXT, age INTEGER)");
    }
    
    @Override
    public void onUpgrade(SQLiteDatabase db, int oldV, int newV) {
        db.execSQL("DROP TABLE IF EXISTS users");
        onCreate(db);
    }
}
```

### 2.6.2 CRUD Operations Formula Sheet
| Operation | SQL / Method | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Create | `INSERT INTO` | `db.insert("users", null, values)` |
| Read | `SELECT * FROM` | `db.query("users", null, null, null, null, null, null)` |
| Update | `UPDATE ... SET ...` | `db.update("users", values, "id=?", new String[]{id})` |
| Delete | `DELETE FROM` | `db.delete("users", "id=?", new String[]{id})` |

```java
Cursor cursor = getContentResolver().query(
    ContactsContract.Contacts.CONTENT_URI,
    null, null, null, null);

while (cursor.moveToNext()) {
    String name = cursor.getString(cursor.getColumnIndex(
        ContactsContract.Contacts.DISPLAY_NAME));
    String id = cursor.getString(cursor.getColumnIndex(
        ContactsContract.Contacts._ID));

        ContactsContract.CommonDataKinds.Phone.CONTENT_URI,
        null,
        ContactsContract.CommonDataKinds.Phone.CONTACT_ID + " = ?",
        new String[]{id}, null);
    // ...
}
```

## 2.9 Project 11: AlarmManager ‚Äî Deep Theory

### 2.9.1 Alarm Types
| Type | Constant | Wakes Device? | Exact? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| One-time | `RTC_WAKEUP` | Yes | No (inexact) |
| Repeating | `setRepeating()` | Yes | No (inexact) |
| Exact | `setExactAndAllowWhileIdle()` | Yes | Yes |
| Inexact | `setAndAllowWhileIdle()` | No | No |

### 2.9.2 Calendar Setup for "Every Sunday at 8:00 AM"
```java
Calendar calendar = Calendar.getInstance();
calendar.set(Calendar.DAY_OF_WEEK, Calendar.SUNDAY);
calendar.set(Calendar.HOUR_OF_DAY, 8);
calendar.set(Calendar.MINUTE, 0);
calendar.set(Calendar.SECOND, 0);

// If today is Sunday and 8 AM has passed, schedule for next Sunday
if (calendar.getTimeInMillis() < System.currentTimeMillis()) {
    calendar.add(Calendar.DAY_OF_YEAR, 7);
}

Intent intent = new Intent(this, AlarmReceiver.class);
PendingIntent pi = PendingIntent.getBroadcast(this, 0, intent, 
    PendingIntent.FLAG_UPDATE_CURRENT);

AlarmManager am = (AlarmManager) getSystemService(ALARM_SERVICE);
am.setRepeating(AlarmManager.RTC_WAKEUP, 
    calendar.getTimeInMillis(),
    AlarmManager.INTERVAL_DAY * 7,  // 7 days
    pi);
```

### Step 3: MainActivity.java
```java
public class MainActivity extends AppCompatActivity {
    private EditText etUsername, etPassword, etAddress, etAge;
    private RadioGroup rgGender;
    private Button btnDob, btnSubmit;
    private TextView tvDob, tvResult;
    private Spinner spinnerState;
    private int year, month, day;

    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_main);

        // Initialize views
        etUsername = findViewById(R.id.etUsername);
        etPassword = findViewById(R.id.etPassword);
        etAddress = findViewById(R.id.etAddress);
        etAge = findViewById(R.id.etAge);
        rgGender = findViewById(R.id.rgGender);
        btnDob = findViewById(R.id.btnDob);
        btnSubmit = findViewById(R.id.btnSubmit);
        tvDob = findViewById(R.id.tvDob);
        tvResult = findViewById(R.id.tvResult);
        spinnerState = findViewById(R.id.spinnerState);

        // Set default date to today
        Calendar c = Calendar.getInstance();
        year = c.get(Calendar.YEAR);
        month = c.get(Calendar.MONTH);
        day = c.get(Calendar.DAY_OF_MONTH);

        // DOB Picker
        btnDob.setOnClickListener(v -> {
            DatePickerDialog dpd = new DatePickerDialog(MainActivity.this,
                (view, year1, month1, dayOfMonth) -> {
                    tvDob.setText("DOB: " + dayOfMonth + "/" + (month1+1) + "/" + year1);
                }, year, month, day);
            dpd.show();
        });

        // Submit
        btnSubmit.setOnClickListener(v -> {
            String username = etUsername.getText().toString();
            String password = etPassword.getText().toString();
            String address = etAddress.getText().toString();
            int selectedId = rgGender.getCheckedRadioButtonId();
            String gender = (selectedId == R.id.rbMale) ? "Male" : "Female";
            String age = etAge.getText().toString();
            String dob = tvDob.getText().toString();
            String state = spinnerState.getSelectedItem().toString();

            String result = "Username: " + username + "\n" +
                            "Password: " + password + "\n" +
                            "Address: " + address + "\n" +
                            "Gender: " + gender + "\n" +
                            "Age: " + age + "\n" +
                            "DOB: " + dob + "\n" +
                            "State: " + state;
            tvResult.setText(result);
        });
    }
}
```

| **Performance** | Fast | Slower (nested) | Fast |
| **Use Case** | Sequential forms | Overlapping views | Form-like |

### Step 2: Login Layout

```xml
<LinearLayout ...>
    <EditText android:id="@+id/etLoginUser" android:hint="Username" />
    <EditText android:id="@+id/etLoginPass" android:hint="Password" 
              android:inputType="textPassword" />
```

### Step 3: Login Logic
```java
btnLogin.setOnClickListener(v -> {
    String inputUser = etLoginUser.getText().toString();
    String inputPass = etLoginPass.getText().toString();
    boolean success = false;

    try {
        FileInputStream fis = openFileInput("users.txt");
        BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(fis));
        String line;
        while ((line = br.readLine()) != null) {
            String[] parts = line.split("\t");
            if (parts[0].equals(inputUser) && parts[1].equals(inputPass)) {
                success = true;
                break;
            }
        }
        br.close();
    } catch (IOException e) {
        e.printStackTrace();
    }

    if (success) {
        new AlertDialog.Builder(this)
            .setTitle("Success")
            .setMessage("Login Successful")
            .setPositiveButton("OK", null)
            .show();
    } else {
        new AlertDialog.Builder(this)
            .setTitle("Failed")
            .setMessage("Login Failed")
            .setPositiveButton("OK", null)
            .show();
    }
});
```

## 3.3 Project 8: Admin CRUD ‚Äî Database Schema & Logic

### Step 1: DBHelper.java
```java
public class DBHelper extends SQLiteOpenHelper {
    private static final String DB_NAME = "AdminDB";
    private static final int DB_VERSION = 1;
    public static final String TABLE_USERS = "users";
    public static final String COL_ID = "id";
    public static final String COL_USERNAME = "username";
    public static final String COL_PASSWORD = "password";
    public static final String COL_EMAIL = "email";

    public DBHelper(Context context) {
        super(context, DB_NAME, null, DB_VERSION);
    }

    @Override
    public void onCreate(SQLiteDatabase db) {
        String createQuery = "CREATE TABLE " + TABLE_USERS + " (" +
                COL_ID + " INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT, " +
                COL_USERNAME + " TEXT, " +
                COL_PASSWORD + " TEXT, " +
                COL_EMAIL + " TEXT)";
        db.execSQL(createQuery);
    }

    @Override
    public void onUpgrade(SQLiteDatabase db, int oldV, int newV) {
        db.execSQL("DROP TABLE IF EXISTS " + TABLE_USERS);
        onCreate(db);
    }
}
```

### Step 2: AdminActivity Logic
```java
// Load all users
private void loadUsers() {
    DBHelper dbHelper = new DBHelper(this);
    SQLiteDatabase db = dbHelper.getReadableDatabase();
    Cursor cursor = db.query(DBHelper.TABLE_USERS, null, null, null, null, null, null);
    
    String[] from = {DBHelper.COL_USERNAME, DBHelper.COL_EMAIL};
    int[] to = {android.R.id.text1, android.R.id.text2};
    
    SimpleCursorAdapter adapter = new SimpleCursorAdapter(this,
        android.R.layout.simple_list_item_2, cursor, from, to, 0);
    listView.setAdapter(adapter);
}

// On item click ‚Üí show edit dialog
listView.setOnItemClickListener((parent, view, position, id) -> {
    showEditDialog(id);
});

private void showEditDialog(long id) {
    // Fetch current data, show dialog with EditTexts, on save:
    DBHelper dbHelper = new DBHelper(this);
    SQLiteDatabase db = dbHelper.getWritableDatabase();
    ContentValues values = new ContentValues();
    values.put(DBHelper.COL_USERNAME, newUsername);
    values.put(DBHelper.COL_PASSWORD, newPassword);
    db.update(DBHelper.TABLE_USERS, values, DBHelper.COL_ID + "=?",
        new String[]{String.valueOf(id)});
    loadUsers(); // refresh
}
```

## 3.4 Project 11: AlarmManager ‚Äî Detailed Step-by-Step

### Step 1: Create BroadcastReceiver
```java
public class AlarmReceiver extends BroadcastReceiver {
    @Override
    public void onReceive(Context context, Intent intent) {
        // Show notification and play sound
        NotificationManager nm = (NotificationManager) 
            context.getSystemService(Context.NOTIFICATION_SERVICE);
        // ... build notification
        nm.notify(1, builder.build());
        
        // Optional: Reschedule for next Sunday
        Intent reschedule = new Intent(context, MainActivity.class);
        reschedule.addFlags(Intent.FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK);
        context.startActivity(reschedule);
    }
}
```

### Step 2: Register Receiver in Manifest
```xml
<receiver android:name=".AlarmReceiver" android:exported="false" />
```

### Step 3: Set Alarm
```java
private void setAlarm() {
    Calendar calendar = Calendar.getInstance();
    calendar.set(Calendar.DAY_OF_WEEK, Calendar.SUNDAY);
    calendar.set(Calendar.HOUR_OF_DAY, 8);
    calendar.set(Calendar.MINUTE, 0);
    calendar.set(Calendar.SECOND, 0);
    
    if (calendar.getTimeInMillis() <= System.currentTimeMillis()) {
        calendar.add(Calendar.DAY_OF_YEAR, 7);
    }
    
    Intent intent = new Intent(this, AlarmReceiver.class);
    PendingIntent pi = PendingIntent.getBroadcast(this, 0, intent,
        PendingIntent.FLAG_UPDATE_CURRENT | PendingIntent.FLAG_IMMUTABLE);
    
    AlarmManager am = (AlarmManager) getSystemService(ALARM_SERVICE);
    // For Android 12+ (API 31+), must specify whether alarm is exact
    if (Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= Build.VERSION_CODES.S) {
        if (am.canScheduleExactAlarms()) {
            am.setExactAndAllowWhileIdle(AlarmManager.RTC_WAKEUP,
                calendar.getTimeInMillis(), pi);
        } else {
            am.setAndAllowWhileIdle(AlarmManager.RTC_WAKEUP,
                calendar.getTimeInMillis(), pi);
        }
    } else {
        am.setRepeating(AlarmManager.RTC_WAKEUP,
            calendar.getTimeInMillis(),
            AlarmManager.INTERVAL_DAY * 7, pi);
    }
}
```

```java
FusedLocationProviderClient fusedLocationClient = 
    LocationServices.getFusedLocationProviderClient(this);

LocationRequest locationRequest = new LocationRequest.Builder(
        Priority.PRIORITY_HIGH_ACCURACY, 10000)
    .setMinUpdateIntervalMillis(5000)
    .build();

LocationCallback locationCallback = new LocationCallback() {
    @Override
    public void onLocationResult(LocationResult locationResult) {
        if (locationResult == null) return;
        for (Location location : locationResult.getLocations()) {
            double lat = location.getLatitude();
            double lng = location.getLongitude();
            tvLocation.setText("Lat: " + lat + "\nLng: " + lng);
        }
    }
};

fusedLocationClient.requestLocationUpdates(locationRequest, 
    locationCallback, Looper.getMainLooper());
```
